title: Chemical Structure Search
text: Chemical Structure Search lets you start from a molecule — the compound you are interested in — and find biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) in the database whose predicted natural products are structurally similar. You provide your molecule as a SMILES string, and the platform compares it against all BGC-associated products using chemical fingerprint similarity.

title: Chemical Structure Search
text: Chemical Structure Search lets you start from a molecule — the compound you are interested in — and find biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) in the database whose predicted natural products are structurally similar. You provide your molecule as a SMILES string, and the platform compares it against all BGC-associated products using chemical fingerprint similarity.

title: Chemical Structure Search
text:
When to use

You have a bioactive compound (e.g. an antibiotic, antifungal, or anticancer lead) and want to find BGCs that might produce structural analogs.
You want to explore the chemical space around a lead molecule to discover related natural products with potentially improved properties.
You have a known natural product and want to identify which environmental assemblies carry BGCs predicted to produce it or something similar.

If you have a protein sequence rather than a chemical structure, use Sequence Search. If you know the biosynthetic domains involved, use the Domain Query Builder.

title: Chemical Structure Search
text:
Key concepts

SMILES (Simplified Molecular Input Line Entry System)

A text notation for describing chemical structures. For example, the SMILES string CC(=O)OC1=CC=CC=C1C(O)=O represents aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid). SMILES strings can be obtained from databases such as PubChem, ChEBI, or ChemSpider, or generated by chemical drawing tools like ChemDraw or the free JSME editor.

Morgan fingerprint

A numerical representation of a molecule’s structure. The algorithm examines each atom in the molecule and records the chemical environment within a certain radius (number of bonds). The result is a binary vector (a long string of 0s and 1s) that captures which structural features are present. Two molecules with similar substructures will share many fingerprint bits.

Tanimoto coefficient (Tc)

The similarity metric used to compare Morgan fingerprints. It is defined as:


\[
T_c = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}
\]
where A and B are the sets of fingerprint bits that are “on” (set to 1) in each molecule. A Tanimoto coefficient of 1.0 means the two molecules have identical fingerprints (and are very likely the same compound or extremely close analogs); 0.0 means they share no structural features. See Understanding Similarity Scores for a deeper discussion.

Predicted product

Many BGCs in the database have one or more associated natural product structures, predicted by biosynthetic analysis tools or linked from the MIBiG reference database. Chemical search only returns BGCs that have at least one associated product structure.

title: Chemical Structure Search
section: Step-by-step: running a chemical structure search
text:
Step-by-step: running a chemical structure search

1. Open the Query tab
In Search mode, click the Query tab in the sidebar. Scroll to the Chemical Structure Search section.


2. Enter your SMILES string
Paste a SMILES string into the input box. For example:
CC1=C2C(=O)C3=C(C(=O)C(O)=C(C)C3O)C(=O)C2=C(O)C(=O)C1=O
This represents tetracycline, a well-known antibiotic. The platform validates the SMILES on input and shows the rendered structure below the input box so you can verify it matches your intended molecule.






TipWhere to get SMILES strings



If you have a compound name but not the SMILES, look it up in PubChem or ChEBI. Both databases provide canonical SMILES on each compound’s page. You can also draw your molecule in any chemical editor and export as SMILES.




3. Adjust the similarity threshold
The threshold slider controls the minimum Tanimoto coefficient for a hit to appear in your results. The default is 0.85, which is fairly stringent:







Threshold
What you will find




0.70
Broadly related scaffolds. Useful for exploring chemical diversity. May include compounds with the same core ring system but different substituents.


0.85 (default)
Close analogs. Compounds that share most structural features with your query. A good starting point.


0.95
Near-identical structures. Only compounds with very minor modifications (e.g. a single hydroxylation or methylation difference).





4. Run the query
Click Run Query. The platform computes the Morgan fingerprint of your query molecule and compares it against all BGC-associated product fingerprints. Results appear in the BGC Roster, ranked by Tanimoto similarity.

title: Chemical Structure Search
text:
What you will see
The BGC Roster displays matching BGCs with these columns:







Column
Description




Similarity
Tanimoto coefficient between your query compound and the BGC’s best-matching predicted product.


Accession
The unique BGC identifier.


Classification
The predicted BGC class.


Novelty
How different the BGC is from characterised references.


Domain Novelty
How unusual the BGC’s domain architecture is.


Size (bp)
The length of the BGC region.



Click a row to open the BGC Detail panel, where you can see the predicted product structure alongside your query structure, making visual comparison straightforward.

title: Chemical Structure Search
section: Worked example: finding producers of tetracycline analogs
text:
Worked example: finding producers of tetracycline analogs
You are interested in tetracycline-family antibiotics and want to find environmental BGCs that might produce novel analogs.

Look up tetracycline on PubChem and copy its canonical SMILES.
Paste it into the Chemical Structure Search box.
Set the threshold to 0.80 to allow moderately divergent analogs.
Click Run Query.
The top hits will include BGCs whose predicted products share the tetracycline ring system. Examine the BGC Detail for each hit to see the full predicted structure and how it differs from tetracycline (e.g. additional hydroxyl groups, different side chains).
Check the Assembly Triad below the BGC results. Apply the Type Strain filter to identify hits from organisms available in culture collections, which you could grow and extract for chemical validation.

title: Chemical Structure Search
section: Worked example: exploring chemical space around a lead compound
text:
Worked example: exploring chemical space around a lead compound
Your lab has isolated a bioactive compound from a soil sample and determined its structure. You want to know what related compounds might exist in nature.

Draw the structure in a chemical editor and export as SMILES (or look it up in a database if it is known).
Paste the SMILES and set the threshold to 0.70 for a broad search.
Run the query.
Sort by similarity. Compounds scoring 0.85+ are close analogs; those between 0.70 and 0.85 may represent related scaffolds with different decorations. These lower-scoring hits can point you toward BGCs that produce compounds in the same chemical family but with potentially different bioactivity profiles.

title: Chemical Structure Search
text:
Limitations

Only BGCs with predicted products are searchable. If a BGC does not have an associated product structure in the database, it will not appear in chemical search results. To search those BGCs, use Domain Query or Sequence Search.
Tanimoto similarity depends on fingerprint radius. The platform uses a fixed Morgan radius. Some structural differences (e.g. stereochemistry) may not be fully captured by the fingerprint. Visual inspection of hit structures in the BGC Detail panel is always recommended.
SMILES must be valid. If the platform cannot parse your SMILES string, an error message will appear. Double-check for typos or missing parentheses.

title: Chemical Structure Search
text:
Tips

Use canonical SMILES. Different SMILES notations can represent the same molecule (e.g. c1ccccc1 and C1=CC=CC=C1 both encode benzene). The platform canonicalises internally, but starting with a canonical SMILES avoids confusion.
Combine with domain queries. If you know both the compound structure and the expected biosynthetic domains (e.g. NRPS for a peptide natural product), add both query types. The platform intersects the results, giving you BGCs that match on both chemistry and genetics.
Lower the threshold gradually. Start at the default (0.85) and lower in increments of 0.05 if you want to broaden the search. Going below 0.70 tends to return many weakly related hits.
Check the BGC class. If your compound is a polyketide, the matching BGCs should mostly be PKS-type clusters. Unexpected BGC classes in the results may indicate interesting biosynthetic diversity or, occasionally, a false positive worth verifying.

title: Chemical Structure Search
text:
Related pages

title: Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate
text: To use the links and actions embedded in the Assembly and BGC Assessment Reports to pivot into Explore or Search mode without losing context. Evaluate mode gives you a deep view of a single asset; cross-mode transitions let you zoom back out to see the broader landscape around that asset.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate
text: To use the links and actions embedded in the Assembly and BGC Assessment Reports to pivot into Explore or Search mode without losing context. Evaluate mode gives you a deep view of a single asset; cross-mode transitions let you zoom back out to see the broader landscape around that asset.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate
text:
When to use cross-mode transitions
Use these transitions when:

You have just read an assessment report and want to see what other assemblies share a similar biosynthetic profile.
A BGC report revealed an interesting Gene Cluster Family (GCF) and you want to browse all members of that family.
The report mentions that a type strain carries a similar BGC and you want to find that strain in the database.
You want to search for BGCs with a domain architecture similar to the one highlighted in the report.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate
text:
Available transitions

From the Assembly Assessment Report
The Assembly Assessment Report contains several clickable links and action buttons that take you to other modes:

Browse Similar Assemblies (Evaluate to Explore)
At the bottom of the BGC Embeddings Map section, click Browse Similar Assemblies. This switches the dashboard to Explore mode with the following pre-applied settings:

The Assembly Space Map centres on the evaluated assembly’s position and highlights its nearest neighbours.
The filter panel is pre-populated with the evaluated assembly’s biome and taxonomic lineage, so you see organisms from a similar ecological context.
The Assembly Roster sorts by proximity to the evaluated assembly in embedding space.

This transition answers the question: what other assemblies in the database look like this one, and are any of them easier to work with?


Find in Purchasable Strains (Evaluate to Search with Type Strain filter)
If the Redundancy Matrix shows BGCs in the “Known GCF, type strain available” category, click Find in Purchasable Strains. This switches to Search mode (or Explore mode, depending on context) with the Type Strain filter enabled. The result set is narrowed to assemblies that correspond to strains deposited in public culture collections — organisms you can order and grow in your laboratory.
This transition answers the question: can I get a culturable organism that carries similar chemistry?


Explore a Specific BGC Class (Evaluate to Explore)
In the scoped BGC Triad, each BGC class label is clickable. Clicking a class label (for example, “Polyketide” or “RiPP”) switches to Explore mode with the BGC Class filter set to that class. This lets you see the full landscape of that BGC class across the database, with the evaluated assembly highlighted.
This transition answers the question: how does this assembly’s polyketide (or NRP, or RiPP, etc.) compare with all others of the same class?



From the BGC Assessment Report

Browse GCF Members (Evaluate to Explore)
In the GCF Placement section, click the family ID link or the Browse Family button. This switches to Explore mode with a filter that restricts the BGC Roster to members of the selected GCF. You can then scroll through all family members, compare their novelty scores, and see which assemblies carry them.
This transition answers the question: what do the other members of this gene cluster family look like, and where are they found?


Search by Domain Architecture (Evaluate to Search)
In the Domain Architecture Differential section, click Search Similar Architectures. This switches to Search mode and pre-populates the Domain Query with the core domains from the evaluated BGC. The query runs automatically, returning BGCs across the database that share the same enzymatic backbone.
This transition answers the question: what other BGCs in the database use the same core enzymes?


View Nearest MIBiG Entry (Evaluate to external)
In the Domain Architecture Comparison section, the MIBiG accession is a hyperlink that opens the corresponding entry on the MIBiG website in a new tab. This is not a mode transition within the platform, but it provides essential context — the MIBiG entry contains compound structures, bioactivity data, and literature references for the nearest validated BGC.


Find Parent Assembly (Evaluate to Evaluate)
At the top of the BGC report, the parent assembly accession is clickable. Clicking it generates a new Assembly Assessment Report, replacing the BGC report. This lets you step up from a single gene cluster to the organism that carries it.
This transition answers the question: is the organism that carries this BGC worth pursuing as a whole, or is this one BGC its only interesting feature?

title: Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate
section: Step-by-step: pivoting from a BGC report to the GCF landscape
text:
Step-by-step: pivoting from a BGC report to the GCF landscape

Evaluate a BGC using any of the entry points described in Evaluate a Database BGC or Upload a BGC.
Read the GCF Placement section. Note the family ID and member count.
Click the family ID or the Browse Family button.
The dashboard switches to Explore mode. The BGC Roster now shows all members of the GCF, sorted by novelty score. The BGC Space Map highlights family members.
Sort and filter to find the most novel or the most accessible members. For example, enable the Type Strain filter to see only family members from culturable organisms.
Select a different family member and right-click to evaluate it, returning to Evaluate mode with a new report.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate
text:
Tips

Cross-mode transitions preserve your shortlist. Any assemblies or BGCs you pinned before switching modes remain in your shortlist and are visible in the new mode.
Use the browser back button. After a cross-mode transition, your browser history contains the previous view. Click back to return to the assessment report without regenerating it.
Chain transitions for deeper exploration. A common workflow is: Evaluate BGC, browse GCF members (Explore), evaluate a different family member (Evaluate), and compare the two reports side by side in separate tabs.
The Type Strain filter is your bridge to the bench. Whenever a report suggests interesting chemistry, use “Find in Purchasable Strains” to check whether a convenient organism already exists.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate
text:
Related pages

Assembly Assessment Report — the report you are transitioning from (assembly)
BGC Assessment Report — the report you are transitioning from (BGC)

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text: This guide walks you through a single, end-to-end session: starting from the landing page, finding a promising assembly, inspecting its BGCs, and exporting a shortlist. No prior experience with the platform is required.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Goal
By the end of this page you will have:

Opened the Discovery Platform.
Filtered the assembly catalogue to a manageable set.
Sorted assemblies by novelty to surface the most promising candidates.
Inspected the BGCs of a single assembly.
Added a BGC to your shortlist.
Exported the shortlist.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Before you begin
You need a modern web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, or Edge) and an internet connection. The platform does not require login or installation.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 1: Open the platform
Navigate to the Discovery Platform. You will land on the Explore Assemblies mode. The screen is divided into two halves:

Top half (Assembly Triad): a sortable roster of assemblies on the left, a scatter plot (Assembly Space Map) in the centre, and summary statistics on the right.
Bottom half (BGC Triad): currently empty — it will populate when you select an assembly.

A filter sidebar sits on the left edge of the screen. It is collapsed by default.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
section: Step 2: Enable the Type Strain toggle
text:
Step 2: Enable the Type Strain toggle
Open the filter sidebar by clicking the funnel icon. At the top of the sidebar you will see a Type Strain toggle. Turn it on.
This restricts results to assemblies that correspond to organisms deposited in public culture collections. Type strains are organisms you can actually order and grow in the lab, which makes them the most actionable candidates for bioprospecting.






Tip



If you are open to working with uncultured organisms (metagenome-assembled genomes, for example), leave this toggle off. You will see a much larger catalogue, but some candidates may be difficult to obtain.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 3: (Optional) Apply additional filters
While the sidebar is open, you can narrow the results further:

Taxonomy tree: Expand the tree to restrict results to a particular phylum, class, or genus. For example, if you are interested in actinobacterial natural products, expand Bacteria > Actinomycetota and click the checkbox.
BGC Class toggles: Enable one or more BGC classes to focus on a particular type of biosynthetic machinery. For a first pass, leave all classes enabled.
Biome lineage: If you are studying a specific environment (e.g. marine sediment), type the first few letters and select from the autocomplete list.

For this quickstart, applying the Type Strain toggle alone is sufficient.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 4: Click Run Query
Press the Run Query button at the bottom of the filter sidebar (or at the top of the Assembly Roster). The platform sends your filters to the server and returns matching assemblies. Depending on your filters, this may take one to three seconds.
The Assembly Roster now shows a table of assemblies. Each row displays:







Column
What it means




Accession
The unique identifier for the assembly (e.g. GCA_000203835.1)


Organism
The species or strain name


BGC count
How many BGCs were detected in this assembly


Novelty
Average distance of this assembly’s BGCs from known, validated gene clusters (0–1 scale; higher is more novel)


Diversity
Fraction of known BGC classes represented in this assembly (0–1 scale)


Density
Number of BGCs per megabase of assembled sequence

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 5: Sort by Novelty
Click the Novelty column header to sort assemblies in descending order. The assemblies at the top of the list have BGCs that are, on average, most distant from any experimentally characterised gene cluster. These are your highest-priority candidates for discovering new chemistry.






Note



High novelty does not guarantee a useful compound. It means the BGCs are unlike anything already in the MIBiG database of validated clusters. Some of these may be genuinely new; others may be partial or poorly annotated. The next steps help you distinguish between the two.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 6: Click an assembly
Click any row in the Assembly Roster. Two things happen:

The assembly is highlighted in the Assembly Space Map (the scatter plot). You can see where it sits relative to the rest of the catalogue.
The BGC Triad in the bottom half of the screen populates with the BGCs belonging to that assembly.

The BGC Triad has three panels:

BGC Roster (left): A table listing every BGC in the selected assembly, with columns for accession, classification, size, novelty score, and domain novelty.
BGC Space Map (centre): A UMAP scatter plot showing all BGCs, with MIBiG validated BGCs as labelled landmarks. The selected assembly’s BGCs are highlighted.
BGC Stats (right): Summary charts showing the distribution of BGC classes and ChemOnt classifications for the selected assembly’s BGCs.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 7: Inspect individual BGCs
In the BGC Roster, click a row to open the BGC Detail Panel. This panel shows:

Classification path: The BGC class hierarchy (e.g. Polyketide.Macrolide).
Completeness badge: Whether the BGC is complete or partial.
Scores: Novelty, Domain Novelty, and Nearest Validated Distance with the MIBiG accession of the closest match.
Region plot: A graphical representation of the gene positions along the contig, with Pfam domains colour-coded on each coding sequence.
Chemical compounds: If the BGC has predicted natural products, their ChemOnt classification hierarchy and SMILES structure appear here.

Spend a moment evaluating the BGC:

Is it complete (not partial)?
Is the novelty score high?
Does the domain architecture look reasonable (multiple biosynthetic domains in a coherent arrangement)?
Is the nearest validated BGC distant (high nearest-validated distance)?

If the answer to most of these is yes, the BGC is a strong candidate. For a detailed guide to reading this panel, see BGC Detail Panel.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 8: Add to your shortlist
If the BGC looks promising, click the Add to BGC Shortlist button in the Detail Panel. A small counter in the header bar increments, confirming the addition. You can add up to 20 BGCs.
To shortlist the entire assembly instead, return to the Assembly Roster and click the pin icon next to the assembly row, or use the Add to Assembly Shortlist button in the assembly detail view. You can shortlist up to 20 assemblies.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 9: Repeat
Return to the Assembly Roster and click the next assembly in the list. Repeat steps 6–8 for as many candidates as you like. You can also adjust your filters and re-run the query at any time — your shortlists are preserved.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Step 10: Export your shortlist
When you are satisfied with your selections, open the shortlist panel by clicking the shortlist icon in the header bar.

Assembly shortlist: Click Export CSV. You will receive a comma-separated file containing each assembly’s accession, organism name, scores, BGC count, and type strain catalogue URL. This file is ready to share with collaborators or import into a spreadsheet.
BGC shortlist: Click Export GenBank. You will receive a multi-record GenBank file containing the nucleotide sequence, gene annotations, and Pfam domain annotations for each shortlisted BGC. This file can be opened in tools such as antiSMASH, Artemis, or SnapGene.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
What to do next

Understand the scores in depth: Read Assembly Scores and the BGC Detail Panel guide.
Learn about all available filters: See the Filtering page.
Search for specific chemistry: Switch to Search BGCs mode and try a domain query, a sequence search, or a chemical structure search.
Evaluate a candidate in depth: Select an assembly or BGC and switch to Evaluate Asset mode for a comprehensive assessment report.
Review the terminology: The Key Concepts page defines every term used in the platform.

title: Quickstart: Find an Assembly in 5 Minutes
text:
Tips

You can right-click any assembly or BGC row to access shortlist actions without opening the detail panel.
The Assembly Space Map axes are configurable. Try switching from the default axes to Novelty vs. Density to spot assemblies that are both novel and BGC-rich.
Shortlists persist in your browser’s local storage. They survive page reloads and are still there when you come back tomorrow.
Filters are preserved when you switch between Explore, Search, and Evaluate modes. Set your taxonomy filter once and it applies everywhere.

title: BGC Classes
text: To explain how the BGC Discovery Platform classifies biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) by the type of enzymatic machinery they encode, and to provide a reference for every class and subclass in the system. Understanding BGC classes helps you filter the database, interpret scores, and connect computational predictions to the kind of chemistry you might observe at the bench.

title: BGC Classes
text: To explain how the BGC Discovery Platform classifies biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) by the type of enzymatic machinery they encode, and to provide a reference for every class and subclass in the system. Understanding BGC classes helps you filter the database, interpret scores, and connect computational predictions to the kind of chemistry you might observe at the bench.

title: BGC Classes
text:
When to use this reference
Consult this page when:

You see a BGC class label (such as “Polyketide” or “RiPP”) in the platform and want to know what it means.
You are building a filter in Explore or Search mode and want to choose the right class or subclass.
You want to understand why a BGC is classified the way it is, especially when the classification seems to conflict with the predicted chemical product.

title: BGC Classes
text:
What BGC classes represent
A BGC class describes the type of enzymatic logic that a gene cluster uses to assemble its product. It does not describe the final chemical structure of the product itself — that is the domain of the Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt) system.
The distinction matters. A Polyketide BGC uses polyketide synthase (PKS) enzymes to build its product from small acyl-CoA building blocks. The final product might be a macrolide, a linear polyene, an aromatic compound, or something else entirely. The BGC class tells you about the assembly line; the ChemOnt annotation tells you about the molecule that comes off the line.
BGC classes are assigned based on the protein domains detected in the gene cluster. The platform identifies Pfam domains in each gene’s protein product and matches domain signatures against known biosynthetic rules. This means classification is based on the genetics of the organism, not on any chemical analysis of its products.

title: BGC Classes
text:
Hierarchical classification
BGC classes are organised in a two-level hierarchy stored as dot-delimited paths (for example, Polyketide.Macrolide). The first level is the broad class; the second is the subclass. Some BGCs are classified only at the broad level if the domain signature does not allow a confident subclass assignment.

title: BGC Classes
text:
Class reference

Polyketide
Broad class: Polyketide
Enzymatic logic: Polyketide synthases (PKS) — large, modular enzyme complexes that extend a carbon chain by iteratively adding two-carbon units derived from malonyl-CoA or methylmalonyl-CoA. Each module in the PKS performs one round of chain extension and may include optional reduction, dehydration, or methylation steps.
Subclasses:








Subclass
Description
Example products




Macrolide
Products contain a macrolactone ring (a large lactone of 12 or more atoms). The PKS typically terminates with a thioesterase domain that cyclises the chain.
Erythromycin, pikromycin


Aromatic
Products contain one or more aromatic rings built by iterative (type II) PKS systems that fold and cyclise the polyketide chain.
Tetracycline, doxorubicin


Linear
Products remain as linear or minimally cyclised polyketide chains.
Mupirocin, amphotericin B (linear precursor)





NRP (Non-Ribosomal Peptide)
Broad class: NRP
Enzymatic logic: Non-ribosomal peptide synthetases (NRPS) — modular enzymes that select, activate, and join amino acid monomers (including non-proteinogenic amino acids) into peptide chains without using the ribosome. Each module typically contains an adenylation domain (selects the amino acid), a thiolation domain (tethers it), and a condensation domain (forms the peptide bond).
Subclasses:








Subclass
Description
Example products




Cyclic
Products are cyclised peptides, often with unusual cross-links, D-amino acids, or N-methylations.
Vancomycin, daptomycin, cyclosporin


Linear
Products are linear peptides or lipopeptides that do not undergo macrocyclisation.
Gramicidin, surfactin (lipopeptide)





RiPP (Ribosomally synthesised and Post-translationally modified Peptide)
Broad class: RiPP
Enzymatic logic: Unlike NRPs, RiPPs start as ribosomally synthesised precursor peptides that are then extensively modified by tailoring enzymes encoded in the same gene cluster. Modifications include cyclisation, dehydration, thioether bridge formation, and many others.
Subclasses:








Subclass
Description
Example products




Lanthipeptide
Modified by lanthionine bridges (thioether cross-links between serine/threonine and cysteine residues).
Nisin, mersacidin


Thiopeptide
Contain a characteristic six-membered nitrogen-containing heterocycle and multiple thiazole or oxazole rings.
Thiostrepton, nosiheptide


Sactipeptide
Contain sulfur-to-alpha-carbon thioether bonds (sactionine bridges) formed by radical SAM enzymes.
Subtilosin A, thuricin CD





Terpene
Broad class: Terpene
Enzymatic logic: Terpene synthases (also called cyclases) convert linear prenyl diphosphate precursors into cyclic or polycyclic terpene scaffolds through carbocation-mediated cyclisation cascades. The precursors are built from five-carbon isoprene units by the mevalonate or MEP pathway.
Subclasses:








Subclass
Description
Example products




Sesquiterpene
Built from three isoprene units (15 carbons, C15).
Pentalenene, albaflavenone


Diterpene
Built from four isoprene units (20 carbons, C20).
Gibberellin, terpentecin





Saccharide
Broad class: Saccharide
Enzymatic logic: Glycosyltransferases and sugar-modifying enzymes that build specialised sugar chains or aminosugar-containing products. These BGCs often work in concert with other biosynthetic classes (for example, attaching sugar moieties to polyketide or NRP scaffolds).
Subclasses:








Subclass
Description
Example products




Aminoglycoside
Products are aminoglycosides — sugar-based antibiotics with amino groups on the sugar rings.
Streptomycin, kanamycin





Alkaloid
Broad class: Alkaloid
Enzymatic logic: Diverse enzymatic routes that produce nitrogen-containing heterocyclic compounds. Unlike NRPs, alkaloid biosynthesis does not follow a modular assembly-line logic; instead, individual enzymes catalyse specific transformations (oxidations, cyclisations, methylations) on amino acid-derived precursors.
Subclasses:








Subclass
Description
Example products




Indole
Products contain an indole ring system, typically derived from tryptophan.
Rebeccamycin, staurosporine


Pyrrolidine
Products contain a pyrrolidine ring, often derived from proline or ornithine.
Prodiginine-related alkaloids





Other
Broad class: Other
Enzymatic logic: Biosynthetic gene clusters that do not fit neatly into the major classes above. This category includes diverse enzymatic strategies.
Subclasses:








Subclass
Description
Example products




Phosphonate
Products contain a direct carbon-phosphorus bond, formed by phosphoenolpyruvate mutase.
Fosfomycin, phosphinothricin


Aminocoumarin
Products contain an aminocoumarin ring system, typically targeting DNA gyrase.
Novobiocin, clorobiocin

title: BGC Classes
text:
Hybrid BGCs
Some BGCs encode enzymes from multiple classes. For example, a PKS-NRPS hybrid BGC contains both polyketide synthase and non-ribosomal peptide synthetase modules. The platform represents hybrids by listing all applicable classes, separated by a forward slash (e.g., Polyketide/NRP). When filtering by class, a hybrid BGC matches filters for any of its component classes.

title: BGC Classes
text:
How classification relates to filtering
In the platform’s filter panel, you can filter by broad class (top level) or by subclass (second level). Selecting a broad class includes all its subclasses. Selecting a specific subclass excludes BGCs from other subclasses of the same broad class.

title: BGC Classes
text:
Tips

BGC class reflects the enzyme, not the product. Two BGCs in the same class can produce very different molecules. Use the ChemOnt annotation to filter by predicted product chemistry.
Hybrid BGCs are common in actinobacteria. If you are exploring Streptomyces assemblies, expect to see many PKS/NRPS hybrids.
“Other” is not a low-quality label. BGCs classified as “Other” use legitimate but less common biosynthetic strategies. Some of the most interesting discoveries come from this category.

title: BGC Classes
text:
Related pages

Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt) — classification by predicted product chemistry
Scores and Metrics Reference — how class diversity contributes to assembly scores
GCF Explained — how BGCs are grouped into families regardless of class
Glossary — quick definitions of all terms

title: Sequence Search
text: Sequence Search lets you paste an amino acid sequence and find all BGCs in the database that encode a similar protein. The platform converts your query sequence into a numerical representation (an embedding) and compares it against pre-computed embeddings for every BGC, returning hits ranked by cosine similarity. This approach is fast — typically completing in a few seconds — and effective at finding close and moderately distant relatives.

title: Sequence Search
text: Sequence Search lets you paste an amino acid sequence and find all BGCs in the database that encode a similar protein. The platform converts your query sequence into a numerical representation (an embedding) and compares it against pre-computed embeddings for every BGC, returning hits ranked by cosine similarity. This approach is fast — typically completing in a few seconds — and effective at finding close and moderately distant relatives.

title: Sequence Search
text:
When to use

You have cloned or identified a biosynthetic gene and want to find BGCs in environmental assemblies that encode a similar protein.
You have a protein sequence from a published BGC (e.g. from MIBiG) and want to see what environmental homologs exist.
You want to identify novel enzymes by finding BGCs whose proteins are related to, but distinct from, a known reference.

If you do not have a sequence but know the Pfam domain architecture you are looking for, use the Domain Query Builder instead. If you have a chemical structure rather than a protein sequence, use Chemical Structure Search.

title: Sequence Search
text:
Key concepts

Protein embedding

A numerical vector (a list of 1,152 numbers) that captures the biochemical properties and evolutionary relationships of a protein sequence. The platform generates embeddings using a protein language model (ESM). Two proteins with similar sequences, structures, or functions will have embeddings that point in similar directions in this high-dimensional space.

Cosine similarity

The metric used to compare embeddings. It measures the angle between two vectors, ignoring their magnitude. A cosine similarity of 1.0 means two embeddings point in exactly the same direction (identical or near-identical proteins); 0.0 means they are unrelated. In practice, biologically meaningful similarity typically starts around 0.5. See Understanding Similarity Scores for more detail.

Asynchronous execution

Because computing the embedding for your query sequence and comparing it against hundreds of thousands of pre-computed BGC embeddings takes a moment, the search runs as a background task. You will see a progress indicator, and results appear when the computation finishes. You can continue interacting with the platform while the search runs.

title: Sequence Search
text:
Step-by-step: running a sequence search

1. Open the Query tab
In Search mode, click the Query tab in the sidebar. Scroll to the Sequence Search section.


2. Paste your sequence
Enter your query in one of two formats:

FASTA format — a header line starting with > followed by the amino acid sequence on subsequent lines:
>my_query_protein
MKTLLLTGAGQGIGRSILEELISAGATHVTAADVSDSQASL
EALQRAQAEAGPIDILVNNAGITRDNLLLRMKPEDFDK
Raw sequence — just the amino acid letters, no header:
MKTLLLTGAGQGIGRSILEELISAGATHVTAADVSDSQASL
EALQRAQAEAGPIDILVNNAGITRDNLLLRMKPEDFDK

Both formats are accepted. Whitespace and line breaks within the sequence are ignored.


3. Adjust the similarity threshold
The threshold slider controls the minimum cosine similarity a BGC must achieve to appear in your results. The slider ranges from 0.5 to 1.0:







Threshold
Effect




0.5
Very permissive — returns distant relatives and some false positives. Good for exploratory searches.


0.7
Moderate — a reasonable starting point for most searches.


0.85
Stringent — returns only close homologs.


0.95+
Very stringent — essentially identical proteins only.



The default threshold is set to balance sensitivity and specificity. You can adjust it before or after running the search.


4. Run the query
Click Run Query. A progress indicator appears while the platform:

Computes the embedding for your query sequence.
Compares it against all BGC embeddings in the database using cosine similarity.
Filters results by your threshold and any active sidebar filters.
Returns ranked hits to the BGC Roster.



5. Review results
Matching BGCs appear in the BGC Roster, sorted by cosine similarity (highest first). The Similarity column shows the score for each hit.

title: Sequence Search
text:
What you will see
The BGC Roster in search mode includes these columns:







Column
Description




Similarity
Cosine similarity between your query embedding and the BGC embedding.


Accession
The unique BGC identifier.


Classification
The predicted BGC class (e.g. “NRPS”, “Type I PKS”).


Novelty
How different the BGC is from characterised references (0 = well-known, 1 = highly novel).


Domain Novelty
How unusual the BGC’s domain composition is relative to its class.


Size (bp)
The length of the BGC region in base pairs.



Click a row to open the BGC Detail panel, where you can see the full gene arrangement, domain architecture, and which protein in the BGC produced the best match to your query.

title: Sequence Search
text:
Sequence length limits
The platform accepts query sequences up to 5,000 amino acids in length. Most biosynthetic proteins (e.g. individual NRPS modules, PKS domains, terpene synthases) are well within this limit. If your sequence exceeds 5,000 amino acids, consider submitting individual domains or modules separately.

title: Sequence Search
section: Worked example: finding relatives of a known lantibiotic synthetase
text:
Worked example: finding relatives of a known lantibiotic synthetase
You have the amino acid sequence of LanM, a lanthipeptide synthetase from a published Streptomyces BGC, and want to find environmental assemblies that encode similar enzymes.

Copy the LanM amino acid sequence from UniProt or the MIBiG entry.
Paste it into the Sequence Search box in FASTA or raw format.
Set the threshold to 0.7 for a moderately broad search.
Click Run Query.
Review the hits. BGCs scoring above 0.85 are likely to encode LanM orthologs — proteins performing the same function in a related pathway. BGCs scoring 0.7–0.85 may encode paralogs or functionally divergent homologs worth investigating.
Switch to the Assembly Triad below to see which assemblies carry these BGCs. Apply the Type Strain filter to find hits in culturable organisms that you could obtain for laboratory validation.

title: Sequence Search
section: Worked example: exploring homologs of a novel enzyme
text:
Worked example: exploring homologs of a novel enzyme
You have identified an unusual oxidase in a metagenome-assembled genome and want to know whether similar enzymes appear in BGCs elsewhere.

Paste the oxidase sequence.
Set the threshold to 0.5 to capture distant relatives.
Run the query.
Sort results by similarity. Examine the top hits to see whether the oxidase appears consistently in a particular BGC class, which would suggest a conserved biosynthetic role.

title: Sequence Search
text:
Tips

Embedding similarity is not the same as percent sequence identity. Two proteins can share 40% identity but have a cosine similarity of 0.8, or vice versa. Embeddings capture functional and structural features beyond raw alignment. See Understanding Similarity Scores for guidance.
One protein at a time. The sequence search accepts a single protein sequence per query. If you want to search with multiple proteins, run separate searches or use the Domain Query Builder to describe the domain combination.
Combine with domain queries for precision. Run a domain query to find BGCs with the right enzymatic machinery, then add a sequence search to rank those BGCs by how similar their proteins are to your reference. The platform intersects the results automatically.
Lower the threshold if you get few results. Embedding space can be sparse for unusual protein families. If your initial search returns only a handful of hits, try lowering the threshold by 0.1 increments.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
text: To produce a comprehensive assessment report for a single biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) that exists in the BGC Discovery Platform database. The report reveals why this BGC scores the way it does, breaks its novelty into independent axes, compares its domain architecture against the nearest experimentally validated BGC, and places it in the context of the full chemical and sequence space.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
text: To produce a comprehensive assessment report for a single biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) that exists in the BGC Discovery Platform database. The report reveals why this BGC scores the way it does, breaks its novelty into independent axes, compares its domain architecture against the nearest experimentally validated BGC, and places it in the context of the full chemical and sequence space.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
text:
When to use this workflow
Use this workflow when:

You spotted a high-novelty BGC in the BGC Roster (in Explore or Search mode) and want to understand what makes it novel.
You are comparing two BGCs from different assemblies and need quantitative detail beyond the summary novelty score.
You want to see the domain-level differences between a candidate BGC and the closest characterised BGC in the MIBiG database.
You need to determine whether a BGC belongs to a large, uncharacterised Gene Cluster Family (GCF) — a signal of widespread but unstudied biosynthetic potential.

If you want to evaluate your own unpublished BGC, see Upload a BGC instead.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
section: Entry point 1: right-click from the BGC Roster
text:
Entry point 1: right-click from the BGC Roster
This is the most common route.

Locate the BGC in the BGC Roster table. In Explore mode the BGC Roster appears in the BGC Triad after you select an assembly. In Search mode it shows your query hits.
Right-click the row. A context menu appears.
Select “Evaluate BGC”. The dashboard switches to Evaluate mode and begins generating the BGC assessment report.
Wait for the report. Pre-computed data makes this fast — typically two to five seconds.
Read the report. The main area now displays the BGC Assessment Report.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
section: Entry point 2: the Evaluate tab with an accession
text:
Entry point 2: the Evaluate tab with an accession

Switch to Evaluate mode by clicking the Evaluate tab in the mode selector.
Select the BGC tab in the sidebar input area. Make sure the BGC sub-tab is active, not the Assembly sub-tab.
Type or paste the BGC accession. Enter the identifier in the text field (for example, BGC0002345 for a MIBiG entry, or the platform’s internal BGC identifier visible in any roster row).
Press Enter or click Evaluate.
Wait for the report.
Read the report.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
text:
What happens behind the scenes
When you trigger evaluation of a database BGC, the platform:

Retrieves the BGC’s full annotation — domain architecture, predicted product classes, ChemOnt predictions, and the protein-level embedding vector (a 1152-dimensional numerical fingerprint that captures the BGC’s overall biochemical character).
Identifies the BGC’s Gene Cluster Family (GCF) — the group of BGCs that share similar domain architecture and sequence content. The platform reports the family identifier, member count, and how many members have been experimentally validated.
Computes the novelty decomposition — three independent axes (Sequence Novelty, Chemistry Novelty, Architecture Novelty) that explain why the overall novelty score is high or low.
Compares domain architecture against the nearest MIBiG-validated BGC, highlighting core, variable, and rare domains.
Computes percentile ranks against the full BGC distribution in the database.
Generates the embedding maps — UMAP projections that show where this BGC sits in the global sequence space and in the chemical prediction space.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
text:
What you will see
The main area fills with the BGC Assessment Report. The section-navigation bar on the right edge lists every section:

GCF Placement — which family this BGC belongs to, how many members the family has, and how far this BGC sits from the family representative.
Novelty Decomposition — three bar charts breaking overall novelty into Sequence, Chemistry, and Architecture components.
Domain Architecture Differential — a categorised list of this BGC’s protein domains, tagged as Core (present in most family members), Variable (present in some), or Rare (present in very few or unique to this BGC).
Domain Architecture Comparison — a side-by-side visual alignment of this BGC’s domains with those of the nearest MIBiG-validated BGC.
BGC Space Map — a UMAP projection of nearby BGCs coloured by class or novelty, with MIBiG landmarks shown as reference points.
Embeddings Map — a second UMAP projection based on the protein embedding vectors, providing a complementary view of the BGC’s position in sequence space.

For a detailed guide to each section, see Reading the BGC Assessment Report.

title: Evaluate a Database BGC
text:
Tips

Pay attention to GCF size and validation count. A BGC that belongs to a large GCF (many members) with zero validated members represents a widespread but completely uncharacterised biosynthetic strategy — this is often the highest-value target for discovery.
Look for “Novel Singleton” status. If the GCF Placement section labels this BGC as a Novel Singleton (a family of one), it means no other BGC in the database shares similar architecture. This is the strongest novelty signal possible but also means you have no close relatives to guide experimental design.
Use the Domain Architecture Comparison to plan experiments. The side-by-side view with the nearest MIBiG BGC shows which domains are shared and which are unique. Shared domains suggest conserved chemistry; unique domains suggest the novel parts of the pathway.
Check all three novelty axes. A BGC can score high on Sequence Novelty (its proteins are distant from known sequences) but low on Architecture Novelty (it uses the same domain arrangement as a known BGC). Understanding which axis drives the score helps you assess the type of novelty — is it a new enzyme doing familiar chemistry, or a familiar enzyme in an unfamiliar arrangement?

title: Reading Search Results
text: After running a search query, you need to interpret the results and decide which BGCs — and which assemblies — warrant further investigation. This page walks you through the results layout, explains each column, and describes the path from a list of matching BGCs to actionable assemblies you can obtain from culture collections.

title: Reading Search Results
text: After running a search query, you need to interpret the results and decide which BGCs — and which assemblies — warrant further investigation. This page walks you through the results layout, explains each column, and describes the path from a list of matching BGCs to actionable assemblies you can obtain from culture collections.

title: Reading Search Results
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when you have run a search and want to understand what the results are telling you, or when you want to refine your result set into a shortlist of candidates for laboratory follow-up.

title: Reading Search Results
text:
The results layout
Search results are displayed in two horizontal bands, each containing a triad of panels.

Upper band: BGC Triad
The BGC Triad is your primary results view. It contains three panels:

BGC Roster (left)

A ranked table of BGCs that matched your query. In Search mode, the roster includes a Similarity column that does not appear in Explore mode. Rows are sorted by similarity score (highest first) by default, though you can re-sort by any column.

BGC Space Map (centre)

A two-dimensional UMAP projection of the matching BGCs. Each point represents one BGC, and proximity on the map indicates structural relatedness. Points are coloured by the attribute you select (BGC class, biome, novelty score, or similarity score). This view helps you spot clusters of related hits and outliers.

BGC Detail (right)

A deep-dive panel that activates when you select a single BGC from the roster or the map. It shows domain architecture, predicted products, genomic context, and links to external resources.




Lower band: Assembly Triad
The Assembly Triad answers the question: “Now that I have found interesting BGCs, where do they come from, and can I get my hands on them?”

Assembly Roster (left)

Lists the parent assemblies of BGCs currently selected or shortlisted in the upper band. Columns include assembly accession, quality score, biome, taxonomic lineage, number of BGCs, and culture-collection availability.

Assembly Space Map (centre)

A UMAP projection of the parent assemblies. This view reveals whether your BGC hits come from a tight taxonomic cluster or are spread across diverse lineages.

Assembly Detail (right)

Full report on a single assembly. Shows all BGCs it carries (not just the ones that matched your search), assembly quality metrics, taxonomic classification, and links to strain databases.

title: Reading Search Results
text:
BGC Roster columns in detail



Column
Description
How to use it




Similarity
The score from your search method (Sorensen-Dice, cosine, or Tanimoto). When multiple methods are combined, this shows the minimum score across methods.
Primary ranking criterion. Higher is better. See Understanding Similarity Scores for interpretation.


Accession
A unique identifier for the BGC (e.g. BGC_000123).
Click to open the BGC Detail panel. Use for cross-referencing with other databases or publications.


Classification
The predicted BGC class: NRPS, Type I PKS, Terpene, RiPP, and so on.
Verify that the BGC class matches your expectations. An unexpected class may indicate a false positive or an interesting chimeric cluster.


Novelty
A score from 0 to 1 indicating how different the BGC is from any characterised reference in the MIBiG database. 0 means a near-identical match to a known BGC; 1 means completely novel.
High novelty (> 0.7) suggests the BGC may produce an uncharacterised compound. Low novelty (< 0.3) suggests it is closely related to a known product.


Domain Novelty
A score from 0 to 1 reflecting how unusual the BGC’s Pfam domain composition is compared to other BGCs of the same class.
High domain novelty suggests unusual enzymatic machinery, which may indicate a non-canonical biosynthetic pathway.


Size (bp)
The length of the BGC region in base pairs.
Unusually large or small BGCs may warrant closer inspection. Very small BGCs might be fragments; very large ones might encode complex multi-step pathways.

title: Reading Search Results
text:
Assembly Roster columns in detail








Column
Description
How to use it




Accession
The unique assembly identifier.
Click to open the Assembly Detail panel.


Quality
An overall assembly quality score reflecting completeness and contamination metrics.
Prefer high-quality assemblies for downstream work. Low-quality assemblies may have fragmented or incomplete BGCs.


Biome
The environment the assembly was sampled from (e.g. “Marine”, “Soil”, “Human gut”).
Useful for contextualising your findings ecologically.


Taxonomy
The predicted taxonomic lineage of the organism.
Helps you identify which organisms produce the BGCs you found.


BGC count
How many BGCs the assembly contains in total.
Assemblies with many BGCs may be prolific natural-product producers worth investigating broadly.


Type Strain
Whether the assembly corresponds to a strain available from a culture collection.
Critical for practical follow-up. Type strains can be purchased and grown in the lab.

title: Reading Search Results
section: Step-by-step: from search hits to actionable assemblies
text:
Step-by-step: from search hits to actionable assemblies

1. Scan the BGC Roster
Review the top-ranked hits. Look for a natural break in similarity scores — a cluster of high-scoring hits followed by a gap often separates genuine matches from noise.


2. Inspect promising BGCs
Click individual BGCs to open the BGC Detail panel. Check:

Does the domain architecture make biological sense for your target pathway?
Is the predicted product (if available) consistent with what you expected?
What is the novelty score? High-novelty BGCs may be more interesting but also carry more risk of misprediction.



3. Star BGCs to build a shortlist
Click the star icon next to any BGC in the roster to add it to your shortlist. The shortlist persists across mode switches, so you can return to it later. Shortlisted BGCs automatically populate the Assembly Triad below.


4. Review the Assembly Triad
The Assembly Roster now shows the parent assemblies of your shortlisted BGCs. Examine:

Quality: prioritise assemblies with high quality scores.
Taxonomy: note which organisms carry your BGCs of interest.
BGC count: an assembly with many BGCs may be a prolific producer worth broader investigation.



5. Apply the Type Strain filter
To find assemblies you can actually purchase and grow, activate the Type Strain filter in the Assembly Roster toolbar. This filters the list to only those assemblies with corresponding entries in culture collections (e.g. DSMZ, ATCC, NCTC). Each entry includes a link to the culture collection catalogue where you can place an order.


6. Evaluate candidates
For your most promising assembly–BGC combinations, switch to Evaluate mode to get a comprehensive report. You can do this directly from the Assembly or BGC Detail panel using the “Evaluate” action button.

title: Reading Search Results
section: What the BGC Space Map tells you
text:
What the BGC Space Map tells you
The BGC Space Map uses UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) to reduce the high-dimensional BGC data into a two-dimensional scatter plot. Points that are close together represent BGCs with similar properties (domain composition, sequence features, or both).
In the context of search results, the map reveals:

Tight clusters: your query found a coherent group of related BGCs. This is typical when searching for a well-defined BGC type.
Scattered points: your query matched diverse, unrelated BGCs. This may happen with broad queries (e.g. a single common domain) and suggests you should tighten your search.
Outliers: individual BGCs far from any cluster. These may be chimeric, fragmented, or genuinely unusual — worth a closer look.

Colour the map by Similarity score to see how the score correlates with structural grouping. High-scoring hits that also cluster together are strong candidates.

title: Reading Search Results
text:
Tips

Sort by different columns. While similarity is the default sort, try sorting by novelty to find the most unusual hits, or by size to spot unusually large clusters.
Use the map and the roster together. Select a cluster of points on the map to see the corresponding rows highlighted in the roster, and vice versa.
Export your results. Use the export button in the roster toolbar to download the results as a CSV file for offline analysis or sharing with collaborators.
Do not over-rely on a single score. A BGC with a high similarity score but low assembly quality may not be a reliable hit. Consider all available metadata before selecting candidates.

text:

Shortlists — managing your saved BGCs and assemblies

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text: The BGC Detail Panel is a flyout that appears when you click a BGC row in the BGC Roster. It provides everything you need to evaluate whether a single BGC is worth pursuing: identification, scores, domain architecture, predicted chemical compounds, and actions. This page walks you through every section of the panel.

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text: The BGC Detail Panel is a flyout that appears when you click a BGC row in the BGC Roster. It provides everything you need to evaluate whether a single BGC is worth pursuing: identification, scores, domain architecture, predicted chemical compounds, and actions. This page walks you through every section of the panel.

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when:

You have clicked a BGC in the roster and want to understand all the information displayed.
You want to know what the novelty and domain novelty scores mean at the individual BGC level.
You need to interpret the region plot (gene map with domain annotations).
You want to understand the chemical compound predictions and their ChemOnt classifications.
You want to decide whether a BGC merits shortlisting, evaluation, or further search.

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text:
Panel header
The top of the panel shows:

BGC accession
The structured identifier for the BGC. The format is MGYB{region_id}.{detector_code}.{bgc_number}:

Region ID (MGYB00000123): identifies the aggregated genomic region on the contig where one or more overlapping BGC predictions were merged.
Detector code (e.g. ANT for antiSMASH): a three-letter code identifying which detection tool found this BGC.
BGC number (e.g. 01): a two-digit serial within the region and detector combination.



Classification path
The BGC class hierarchy displayed as a dot-delimited breadcrumb (e.g. Polyketide.Type_I.Modular). The first segment is the top-level class, which corresponds to the type of enzymatic assembly line. Subsequent segments provide finer classification where available.


Completeness badge
A coloured badge indicating whether the BGC is Complete or Partial.

Complete means the BGC detection tool identified both boundaries of the cluster. The full biosynthetic logic is likely captured.
Partial means the BGC sits at the edge of a contig and may be truncated. One or more essential genes could be missing, and scores derived from the BGC’s domain content should be interpreted with caution.



Validated badge
If the BGC corresponds to an entry in the MIBiG database (a curated repository of experimentally verified gene clusters), a Validated badge appears. This means the natural product encoded by this BGC has been isolated, structurally characterised, and published.


Size
The length of the BGC in kilobases (kb). Larger BGCs tend to encode more biosynthetic steps and produce more complex products. Typical sizes range from 5 kb (small terpene clusters) to over 100 kb (large modular polyketide or nonribosomal peptide assembly lines).

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text:
Scores section
The scores section presents three metrics that summarise how novel this BGC is relative to the rest of the database.

Novelty score (0 – 1)
The cosine distance between this BGC’s protein embedding and the nearest validated (MIBiG) BGC embedding. The embedding is a 1,152-dimensional vector computed by the ESM protein language model from the BGC’s protein sequences. Think of it as a numerical fingerprint of the BGC’s overall enzymatic character.







Range
Interpretation




0.0 – 0.2
Very similar to a known, characterised BGC


0.2 – 0.5
Moderate similarity; may be a variant of a known compound


0.5 – 0.7
Substantially different from any validated cluster


0.7 – 1.0
Far from all validated references; potentially new chemistry





Domain novelty (0 – 1)
The fraction of this BGC’s Pfam domain annotations that are not found in any other BGC in the entire database. A Pfam domain is a conserved protein functional unit (e.g. a ketosynthase domain, an adenylation domain).







Range
Interpretation




0.0
Every domain in this BGC also appears in at least one other BGC. The domain architecture is not unique.


0.0 – 0.3
A few domains are unique, but most are shared. The BGC has a mostly familiar architecture with some variations.


0.3 – 0.7
A significant fraction of domains are exclusive to this BGC. The enzymatic logic is unusual.


0.7 – 1.0
Most or all domains are unique. This is highly unusual and could indicate genuinely novel enzymology or a detection artefact.





Nearest validated distance
This line shows two values:

Distance: The raw cosine distance to the closest MIBiG BGC embedding. This is the same value as the novelty score when the nearest BGC is validated.
MIBiG accession: The accession of the validated BGC that is closest in embedding space (e.g. BGC0000194). You can look up this accession in the MIBiG database to see what compound the reference produces.

This pairing lets you answer two questions: “How different is this BGC from anything known?” (the distance) and “What known compound is it most similar to?” (the accession).

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text:
Region plot
The region plot is a graphical representation of the BGC’s genomic context. It shows the stretch of the contig that the BGC occupies, with coding sequences (CDSs) drawn as directional arrows and Pfam domain annotations drawn as coloured blocks within each CDS.

Reading the plot

Horizontal axis: Nucleotide position along the contig. The window extends slightly beyond the BGC boundaries to show flanking genes.
Arrows: Each arrow represents a predicted coding sequence (gene). The direction indicates the strand: right-pointing arrows are on the forward strand, left-pointing arrows on the reverse strand.
Coloured blocks within arrows: Each coloured block is a Pfam domain hit. Hovering over a block shows the Pfam accession, name, description, and e-value.
Cluster boundary markers: Vertical dashed lines indicate the start and end of the BGC as called by the detection tool. Genes outside these boundaries are shown in grey as genomic context.



What to look for

Core biosynthetic domains: Look for the characteristic domains of the BGC’s class. A polyketide BGC should have ketosynthase (KS), acyltransferase (AT), and acyl carrier protein (ACP) domains arranged in a modular pattern. An NRP BGC should have condensation (C), adenylation (A), and thiolation (T) domains.
Unusual domains: Domains not typically associated with the BGC class may indicate tailoring reactions that produce structural novelty. A halogenase domain in a polyketide BGC, for example, suggests a halogenated product.
Gene density and arrangement: A well-organised cluster with many large multi-domain genes is more likely to be a genuine, functional BGC than a sparse region with few small genes.
Partial indicators: If the cluster boundary coincides with the contig edge (indicated by a ragged line), the BGC is partial and may be missing essential genes.



CDS details
Click any arrow (CDS) in the region plot to see its details:

Protein identifier
Amino acid length
Start and end positions on the contig
Strand
Gene caller (the tool that predicted the CDS)
Cluster representative (if the protein belongs to a protein cluster, the representative’s identifier)
Full list of Pfam domain annotations with accessions, descriptions, and scores

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text:
Chemical compounds section
If the BGC has predicted natural products, they appear in the Chemical Compounds section below the region plot. Each compound entry shows:

Compound name
The name of the predicted or characterised natural product (e.g. “Erythromycin A”). For validated BGCs, this is the experimentally confirmed product. For predicted products, the name comes from computational annotation.


SMILES structure
The SMILES (Simplified Molecular-Input Line-Entry System) string encoding the compound’s 2D structure. Where available, a rendered structure image is also shown.


ChemOnt classification
The Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt) hierarchy for the compound. ChemOnt classifies molecules by structural features — functional groups, ring systems, substituent patterns — independently of how they are biosynthesised. The classification is displayed as a nested tree showing progressively finer categories.
Each ChemOnt node includes:

ChemOnt ID (e.g. CHEMONTID:0000147): the identifier in the ClassyFire ontology.
Class name (e.g. “Macrolides and analogues”): a human-readable label.
Probability (0 – 1): the confidence that the predicted product belongs to this chemical class. Higher values indicate stronger computational evidence.

Multiple ChemOnt nodes may be annotated on the same compound, reflecting different levels of the classification hierarchy or alternative structural interpretations.


NP class path
In addition to ChemOnt, each compound may have a natural product class path (e.g. Polyketide.Macrolide.Erythromycin). This is a simpler, biosynthesis-oriented classification that aligns more closely with the BGC class.

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text:
Actions
The bottom of the BGC Detail Panel provides three action buttons:

Add to Shortlist
Adds this BGC to your BGC Shortlist (up to 20 items). A counter in the header bar increments to confirm. If the shortlist is already full, the button is disabled and a tooltip explains the limit.


Find Similar
Switches to Search BGCs mode and runs a sequence-similarity search using this BGC’s protein embedding as the query. The results are other BGCs in the database ranked by cosine similarity. This is useful when you find an interesting BGC and want to see whether related clusters exist in other organisms.


Evaluate
Switches to Evaluate Asset mode and runs a full BGC assessment. The resulting report includes:

GCF context: Which gene cluster family (GCF) the BGC belongs to, with member count, validated count, and mean novelty.
Domain differential: A comparison of this BGC’s Pfam domains against the GCF’s core, variable, and rare domain sets.
Novelty decomposition: A breakdown of the overall novelty into sequence, chemistry, and architecture components.
Chemical space map: A UMAP view showing this BGC, its nearest neighbours, and validated reference points.

title: Reading the BGC Detail Panel
text:
Tips

Compare the novelty score with the domain novelty score. A BGC with high novelty but low domain novelty has familiar enzymatic domains arranged in a way that still produces something distant from validated references — possibly a known scaffold with unusual tailoring. A BGC with high domain novelty has genuinely unusual enzymology.
Check the completeness badge before shortlisting. Partial BGCs can produce misleadingly high novelty scores because missing domains make the embedding atypical.
Use the nearest validated accession to look up the closest known compound in MIBiG. This tells you what chemical space the BGC is likely operating in.
If the Chemical Compounds section is empty, the BGC does not have predicted natural product annotations. This does not mean it lacks products — it means the computational pipeline could not assign a product prediction with sufficient confidence.
The region plot is the most information-dense part of the panel. Zoom in to examine individual domain annotations when the cluster is large.

title: Upload a BGC for Evaluation
text: To upload a single biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) from your own sequencing or annotation project and receive the same comprehensive BGC assessment report that the platform generates for database entries. Like assembly uploads, the analysis is ephemeral — the platform processes your file, generates the report, and discards the data when you are done.

title: Upload a BGC for Evaluation
text: To upload a single biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) from your own sequencing or annotation project and receive the same comprehensive BGC assessment report that the platform generates for database entries. Like assembly uploads, the analysis is ephemeral — the platform processes your file, generates the report, and discards the data when you are done.

title: Upload a BGC for Evaluation
text:
When to use this workflow
Use this workflow when:

You ran antiSMASH (or another BGC prediction tool) on your own genome and want to assess one specific predicted region against the public catalogue.
You received a GenBank file from a collaborator and want a quick novelty and architecture analysis without setting up a local pipeline.
You are curating BGC annotations for a publication and want to verify how each cluster compares with known, validated BGCs.

If you want to evaluate an entire assembly (all its BGCs at once), see Upload an Assembly. If the BGC is already in the platform’s database, use Evaluate a Database BGC instead.

title: Upload a BGC for Evaluation
text:
Format requirements
The platform accepts a single GenBank-format file (.gbk or .gb). The file must contain:

One sequence record representing the BGC region. Multi-record GenBank files are not supported for single-BGC evaluation — the platform will read only the first record.
Coding sequence (CDS) features with /translation= qualifiers providing the amino acid sequences. These translations are used to compute the protein embedding and to identify Pfam domains.
A nucleotide sequence. The ORIGIN section must contain the full nucleotide sequence for the region.


Recommended annotations
The following annotations are optional but improve report quality:

antiSMASH cluster annotations (/note="Type: ..." or /product= qualifiers on cluster or region features). If present, the platform uses these to assign BGC class.
Pfam domain annotations (/sec_met_domain= or PFAM qualifiers). If absent, the platform predicts domains from the translated CDS features, which adds a few seconds of processing time.



File size limits

Maximum file size: 50 MB
Maximum number of CDS features: 500



Example file structure
A minimal valid GenBank file looks like this:
LOCUS       my_bgc_region001       45230 bp    DNA     linear   BCT
DEFINITION  Biosynthetic gene cluster from Streptomyces sp. ABC123
FEATURES             Location/Qualifiers
     source          1..45230
                     /organism="Streptomyces sp. ABC123"
     CDS             1200..3500
                     /product="polyketide synthase"
                     /translation="MKTLI..."
     CDS             3510..5800
                     /product="acyltransferase"
                     /translation="MSAEV..."
ORIGIN
        1 atgcgtaa...
//

title: Upload a BGC for Evaluation
text:
Step-by-step

Switch to Evaluate mode by clicking the Evaluate tab in the mode selector at the top of the dashboard.
Locate the upload area in the sidebar. Below the accession text field, you will see the drag-and-drop upload widget.
Select the BGC tab in the upload widget. The widget has two sub-tabs: Assembly and BGC. Click the BGC tab. The accepted file type changes from .tar.gz to .gbk / .gb.
Upload your GenBank file. Drag the file onto the upload area, or click to open a file picker.
Click Upload. The platform begins uploading and processing the file. A status indicator shows the current step:

Parsing GenBank — reading the sequence record and extracting CDS features.
Computing domains — identifying Pfam protein domains from translated CDS sequences (skipped if domain annotations are already present in the file).
Computing embedding — generating the 1152-dimensional protein embedding vector that captures the BGC’s overall biochemical character.
Scoring — computing novelty scores and finding the nearest Gene Cluster Family and nearest validated BGC.
Generating report — assembling the assessment report.

Processing time is typically 10–30 seconds.
Read the report. The main area displays the BGC Assessment Report with an Uploaded badge in the header.

title: Upload a BGC for Evaluation
text:
What you will see
The report contains all the same sections as a database BGC evaluation:

GCF Placement — which Gene Cluster Family your BGC is most similar to, the distance to the family representative, and whether your BGC qualifies as a Novel Singleton.
Novelty Decomposition — Sequence Novelty, Chemistry Novelty, and Architecture Novelty broken out as independent axes.
Domain Architecture Differential — your BGC’s domains categorised as Core, Variable, or Rare relative to the assigned GCF.
Domain Architecture Comparison — side-by-side alignment with the nearest MIBiG-validated BGC.
BGC Space Map and Embeddings Map — UMAP projections placing your BGC in the context of the full database.

The only difference from a database evaluation is the Uploaded badge and a note that the data is ephemeral.

title: Upload a BGC for Evaluation
text:
Tips

Include CDS translations. If your GenBank file lacks /translation= qualifiers, the platform must translate the CDS features from the nucleotide sequence, which can fail if the file uses non-standard genetic codes. Always include pre-computed translations.
One BGC per file. If you have multiple BGC regions to evaluate, upload them one at a time. Each upload replaces the previous report. To evaluate all BGCs from an assembly simultaneously, use the Upload an Assembly workflow instead.
Use antiSMASH output directly. The .region0XX.gbk files that antiSMASH produces are already in the correct format. You can upload them directly without modification.
Compare with MIBiG manually. After reading the report, note the nearest MIBiG BGC identifier from the Domain Architecture Comparison section. You can look up that entry on the MIBiG website for compound structures and literature references.
Your data stays private. The upload is processed in your browser session and is never persisted to the server or visible to other users.

title: About
text:
The BGC Discovery Platform is developed by the Microbiome Informatics team at the European Bioinformatics Institute (EMBL-EBI), part of the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL). The platform is a component of MGnify, EMBL-EBI’s resource for the analysis and archival of metagenomic and metatranscriptomic data.
The platform was designed to help experimental biochemists identify organisms and biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) worth pursuing in the laboratory. It combines large-scale computational annotation of public genomic data with interactive visualisation and scoring tools, making bioinformatics-derived insights accessible to researchers who work primarily at the bench.

title: About
text:
Team
The platform is built and maintained by the Microbiome Informatics team at EMBL-EBI. The team develops computational infrastructure for analysing microbial communities, with a focus on making complex genomic data useful for researchers across disciplines.
Core contributors include researchers and software engineers specialising in natural product genomics, metagenomics, machine learning, and web application development.

title: About
text:
Funding
Development of the BGC Discovery Platform is supported by:

EMBL — core funding for the European Bioinformatics Institute and the Microbiome Informatics team.
Wellcome Trust — grant funding supporting the development of MGnify and related bioinformatics infrastructure.

The platform builds on data and tools produced by the broader natural products research community, including antiSMASH, MIBiG, Pfam, and ClassyFire/ChemOnt.

title: About
text:
Citation
If you use the BGC Discovery Platform in your research, please cite:

Citation details will be added upon publication. In the meantime, please reference the platform URL and the MGnify resource.

If your work relies on specific components, please also cite the relevant upstream tools:

antiSMASH for BGC prediction
MIBiG for the validated BGC reference set
ESM (Evolutionary Scale Modeling) for protein embeddings
MGnify for metagenomic assembly data

title: About
text:
Data sources
The platform integrates data from several public resources:



Source
What it provides




MGnify
Metagenomic assemblies and associated metadata


GTDB
Standardised taxonomic classifications


MIBiG
Experimentally validated BGCs and known compounds


INSDC (ENA, NCBI, DDBJ)
Genome assemblies and nucleotide sequences


GOLD
Ecosystem and biome classifications


Pfam / InterPro
Protein domain definitions

title: About
text:
Technology
The platform is built with open-source technologies:

Django and Django Ninja for the web application and REST API
PostgreSQL with pgvector for vector similarity search
Celery with RabbitMQ for asynchronous task processing
Redis for caching
Nextflow for the data ingestion and annotation pipeline

The full source code is available on GitHub.

title: About
text:
Source code
The platform’s source code is hosted on GitHub:

Main repository: github.com/EBI-Metagenomics/mgnify-bgcs-v2
Data portal (Django application): github.com/Finn-Lab/bgc_data_portal
ETL pipeline (Nextflow): github.com/Finn-Lab/mgnify-bgcs-etl

Contributions, bug reports, and feature requests are welcome via GitHub Issues on the appropriate repository.

title: About
text:
Contact and feedback
We welcome feedback from users at all levels of experience. If you have questions, encounter problems, or want to suggest improvements:

Bug reports and feature requests: Open an issue on the GitHub repository.
General questions: Contact the MGnify helpdesk at metagenomics-help@ebi.ac.uk.
Collaboration inquiries: Contact the Microbiome Informatics team through the EMBL-EBI website.

title: About
text:
Privacy and data handling

Database entries are derived from publicly available genomic data and are freely accessible.
Uploaded files (in Evaluate mode) are processed ephemerally in your browser session. They are never stored on the server, added to the database, or shared with other users.
Shortlists are stored locally in your browser using browser storage. They are not transmitted to the server unless you explicitly trigger an export.
The platform does not require user accounts or authentication for read-only access to public data.

title: About
text:
Version
The current platform version and the versions of key data dependencies (MIBiG release, GTDB release, antiSMASH version) are displayed in the footer of the platform dashboard. This documentation corresponds to the platform version shown at the time of writing.
