title: Glossary
text:
To provide a quick-reference glossary of every technical term used in the BGC Discovery Platform. Each entry gives a brief definition and links to the page where the term is explained in full context.



A sequenced genome, metagenome-assembled genome (MAG), or genomic region from an environmental sample. Assemblies are the organism-level entities in the platform. Each assembly carries one or more BGCs and is scored on novelty, diversity, and density. See Platform Overview.



A contiguous stretch of DNA that encodes the enzymes, regulators, and transport proteins needed to produce a natural product (such as an antibiotic, siderophore, or pigment). Each BGC belongs to one assembly and is classified by its enzymatic machinery (BGC class) and predicted product chemistry (ChemOnt). See Platform Overview.



A classification of a BGC based on the type of enzymatic machinery it uses to build its product. Major classes include Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, Terpene, Saccharide, Alkaloid, and Other. Classes are organised hierarchically with broad classes and subclasses, stored as dot-delimited paths (for example, Polyketide.Macrolide). See BGC Classes.



A hierarchical path describing the environmental origin of a metagenomic assembly, following the GOLD Ecosystem Classification. Formatted as colon-delimited strings (for example, root:Environmental:Terrestrial:Soil). Available only for metagenome-derived assemblies. See Biome Ontology.



A hierarchical classification system for chemical compounds based on structural features, developed as part of the ClassyFire project. The platform uses ChemOnt to annotate each BGC with a prediction of the type of molecule its gene cluster produces. Each annotation carries a probability score (0–1) reflecting prediction confidence. The ontology contains approximately 4,825 terms. See Chemical Ontology.



An assembly-level score measuring the number of BGCs per megabase (Mb) of assembled sequence. Higher density indicates an organism that devotes a larger fraction of its genome to secondary metabolism. Typical range is 0–50 BGCs/Mb. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



An assembly-level score (0–1) measuring the normalised richness of BGC classes represented in an assembly. Calculated as the number of distinct BGC broad classes divided by the total number of broad classes observed across the database. Higher values indicate a broader biosynthetic repertoire. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



A BGC-level score (0–1) measuring the fraction of Pfam protein domains in a BGC that are not found in any MIBiG-validated BGC. A score of 0 means every domain is known from validated BGCs; a score of 1 means none of the domains have been observed in validated BGCs. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



A group of BGCs from across the database that share similar domain architecture and protein sequence content, clustered by cosine similarity of their embedding vectors. Each GCF has a representative member (medoid), a member count, and a validated member count. GCFs are used to assess redundancy and discovery potential. See GCF Explained.



Minimum Information about a Biosynthetic Gene cluster — a curated, public database of approximately 2,500 experimentally characterised BGCs linked to known natural products. MIBiG entries serve as the “validated” reference set for all novelty calculations in the platform. See MIBiG and Validated BGCs.



A BGC-level score (0–1) representing the normalised Euclidean distance in embedding space between a BGC and the closest MIBiG-validated BGC. Functionally similar to the Novelty Score but uses Euclidean rather than cosine distance. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



A BGC that is the sole member of its Gene Cluster Family — no other BGC in the database is similar enough to be grouped with it. This is the strongest possible novelty signal, indicating a BGC unlike anything else in both the validated and unvalidated catalogues. See GCF Explained.



An assembly-level score (0–1) representing the mean Novelty Score across all BGCs belonging to that assembly. Higher values indicate an assembly whose BGCs are, on average, more distant from characterised gene clusters. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



A BGC-level score (0–1) representing the cosine distance between the BGC’s protein embedding vector and the embedding of the nearest MIBiG-validated BGC. A score of 0 means the BGC is identical to a known BGC in embedding space; a score approaching 1 means it is maximally distant from all known BGCs. This is the primary metric for identifying novel biosynthetic potential. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



A value (0–100) computed in Evaluate mode that indicates what fraction of all assemblies (or BGCs) in the database score lower than the evaluated asset on a given metric. A percentile of 90 means the asset scores higher than 90% of all entries. Percentile ranks are calculated on demand and are not stored in the database. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



A conserved protein domain as defined in the Pfam database. Pfam domains represent functional units within proteins — for example, a ketosynthase domain (PF00109) or an acyltransferase domain (PF00698). The platform identifies Pfam domains in each BGC’s proteins and uses them for classification, domain architecture analysis, and the Domain Query search. See BGC Classes.



An assembly-level score (0–1) measuring how under-represented the assembly’s taxonomic lineage is in the database. Calculated as the inverse frequency of the assembly’s genus (or nearest resolved taxonomic rank) among all assemblies. High values indicate organisms from under-explored lineages. See Scores and Metrics Reference.



An assembly that corresponds to a microbial strain deposited in a public culture collection (such as DSMZ, ATCC, or NCTC). Type strains can be ordered, cultured, and studied in the laboratory. The platform flags assemblies with type strain status, making it easy to filter for organisms you can physically obtain. Assemblies without type strain status may represent organisms known only from sequence data.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text: This page explains the four scores that the platform computes for every assembly. After reading it, you will know what each score measures, how it is calculated, what its range means, and how to use it when deciding which assemblies to pursue.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text: This page explains the four scores that the platform computes for every assembly. After reading it, you will know what each score measures, how it is calculated, what its range means, and how to use it when deciding which assemblies to pursue.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when:

You are sorting the Assembly Roster and want to understand what the numbers mean.
You are comparing two assemblies and need to know which score dimension matters most for your question.
You are reading an Evaluate Asset report and want to interpret the percentile ranks.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
Overview









Score
Range
Measures
High value means




Novelty
0 – 1
Average distance from validated BGCs
BGCs are unlike anything characterised


Diversity
0 – 1
Fraction of known BGC classes present
Assembly covers many types of chemistry


Density
0 – open
BGCs per megabase of assembled sequence
Genome is packed with biosynthetic clusters


Taxonomic Novelty
0 – 1
Underrepresentation of the taxon in the database
Organism’s lineage is rare or unstudied



All four scores are precomputed during data ingestion and updated when new assemblies are loaded. Percentile ranks (0–100) are also available, so you can see how a given assembly compares to the full catalogue.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
Novelty

What it measures
The assembly novelty score reflects how different its BGCs are, on average, from experimentally validated gene clusters in the MIBiG database. It answers the question: “If I work on this organism, how likely am I to find chemistry that has not already been described?”


How it is computed

Each BGC in the database has an embedding — a 1,152-dimensional numerical vector computed from its protein sequences using the ESM protein language model. Think of this vector as a fingerprint that captures the overall biosynthetic character of the BGC.
For each BGC, the platform finds the closest validated (MIBiG) BGC in embedding space using cosine distance. Cosine distance ranges from 0 (identical) to 1 (maximally different). This per-BGC value is stored as the BGC’s novelty score.
The assembly novelty score is the average of the novelty scores across all BGCs in that assembly.



Interpreting the values








Value range
Interpretation
Example




0.0 – 0.2
Most BGCs closely resemble validated clusters
A Streptomyces coelicolor genome whose BGCs are well-known references


0.2 – 0.5
A mix of known and moderately novel BGCs
A Streptomyces species with several characterised BGCs and a few divergent ones


0.5 – 0.7
BGCs are substantially different from validated references
An actinobacterium from an undersampled environment with unusual domain architectures


0.7 – 1.0
BGCs are far from any validated cluster
A MAG from a deep-sea vent with no close MIBiG matches





Practical advice
High novelty is attractive but comes with caveats. A score above 0.7 for a complete genome assembly from a culturable type strain is a strong signal. The same score for a partial metagenome fragment may simply reflect incompleteness or misassembly. Always check the assembly type and the completeness of individual BGCs before committing to a high-novelty candidate.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
Diversity

What it measures
The assembly diversity score reflects the breadth of biosynthetic potential. It answers the question: “Does this organism make many different types of natural products, or does it specialise in one class?”


How it is computed

Each BGC’s classification path is parsed to extract its top-level (L1) class: Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, Terpene, Saccharide, Alkaloid, or Other.
The number of distinct L1 classes present in the assembly is counted. This is the assembly’s L1 class count.
The platform divides the L1 class count by the total number of known L1 classes across the entire database to produce the diversity score.



Interpreting the values








Value range
Interpretation
Example




0.0 – 0.15
Assembly has BGCs from only one class
A cyanobacterium with three terpene BGCs and nothing else


0.15 – 0.4
Two or three BGC classes represented
A Bacillus species with NRP and polyketide BGCs


0.4 – 0.7
Four or five BGC classes represented
A Streptomyces species with PKS, NRPS, RiPP, terpene, and siderophore BGCs


0.7 – 1.0
Nearly all known BGC classes present
Exceptionally versatile biosynthetic genomes





Practical advice
Diversity is most useful when you want a single organism that can serve as a broad platform for natural product discovery. A high-diversity assembly gives you more chances of finding something interesting, even if no single BGC is extremely novel. Conversely, a low-diversity, high-novelty assembly may have just one or two extraordinary BGCs worth investigating.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
Density

What it measures
The assembly density score reflects how much of the organism’s genome is devoted to secondary metabolism. It answers the question: “Is this organism a prolific natural product producer relative to its genome size?”


How it is computed

The total number of BGCs detected in the assembly is divided by the assembly size in megabases.
The result is expressed as BGCs per megabase.

Unlike novelty and diversity, density is not normalised to a 0–1 range. Typical values range from near zero (an organism with few or no BGCs) to 5 or more (an organism with dozens of BGCs in a compact genome).


Interpreting the values








Value range
Interpretation
Example




0.0 – 0.5
Few BGCs relative to genome size
Escherichia coli with 1–2 BGCs in a 5 Mb genome


0.5 – 2.0
Moderate biosynthetic investment
A typical Bacillus or Pseudomonas species


2.0 – 4.0
Substantial biosynthetic genome fraction
Streptomyces species often fall in this range


4.0+
Exceptionally dense
Streptomyces strains with 30+ BGCs in a 7–8 Mb genome





Practical advice
Density is particularly useful when comparing organisms of similar genome size. Among actinomycetes, a density above 3 suggests an organism that has evolved to invest heavily in chemical warfare. Note that density depends on assembly size, which is unknown for some metagenome fragments. In those cases, density is set to zero and should not be used for ranking.






Warning



Density is undefined for assemblies without a known genome size. These assemblies will show a density of 0.0, which does not mean they lack BGCs — it means the score cannot be computed. Filter by assembly type (genome) if density is important to your analysis.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
Taxonomic Novelty

What it measures
The taxonomic novelty score reflects how underrepresented an assembly’s lineage is in the database. It answers the question: “Is this organism from a well-studied clade, or from a lineage where few genomes have been sequenced?”


How it is computed

Each assembly is assigned a taxonomy path based on the GTDB lineage of its contigs (e.g. Bacteria.Actinomycetota.Actinomycetia.Streptomycetales.Streptomycetaceae.Streptomyces).
The platform counts how many assemblies in the database share the same genus (or the most specific rank available).
Assemblies from rare genera receive a high taxonomic novelty score; assemblies from well-represented genera receive a low score. The score is normalised to the 0–1 range using the inverse of the genus count relative to the maximum genus count in the database.



Interpreting the values








Value range
Interpretation
Example




0.0 – 0.2
Genus is very well represented in the database
Streptomyces, with hundreds of genome assemblies


0.2 – 0.5
Genus has moderate representation
A less-studied actinobacterial genus with 10–50 assemblies


0.5 – 0.8
Genus is poorly represented
A genus with fewer than 10 assemblies in the database


0.8 – 1.0
Genus is nearly unique in the database
A single representative of an obscure lineage





Practical advice
Taxonomic novelty is a proxy for unexplored biosynthetic space. Organisms from well-studied genera (Streptomyces, Bacillus) have low taxonomic novelty because their relatives have been extensively screened. Organisms from rare lineages may harbour fundamentally different enzymology. Combining high taxonomic novelty with high BGC novelty produces the strongest candidates for discovering truly new chemistry.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
Using scores together
No single score tells the whole story. Here are common combinations and what they suggest:










Novelty
Diversity
Density
Tax. Novelty
Interpretation




High
High
High
High
Dream candidate: a rare organism packed with diverse, novel BGCs. Prioritise this.


High
Low
Low
High
A rare organism with few BGCs, but those few are highly novel. Worth a look if individual BGCs pass quality checks.


Low
High
High
Low
A well-known prolific producer. Useful for discovering new derivatives of known compounds, less useful for entirely new scaffolds.


High
Low
High
Low
A common genus with many BGCs, but unusually novel ones. May indicate recent horizontal gene transfer.

title: Understanding Assembly Scores
text:
Percentile ranks
Each assembly’s scores are also expressed as percentile ranks (0–100). A percentile rank of 85 for novelty means that 85% of all assemblies in the database have a lower novelty score. Percentile ranks make it easy to compare assemblies without memorising absolute score ranges.
Percentile ranks are computed using SQL window functions across the full assembly catalogue and are updated whenever new data is ingested.

title: Search BGCs
text: Search mode helps you find specific biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) when you already have a target in mind — a protein domain combination, a known amino acid sequence, or a chemical structure. Rather than browsing the full catalogue, you pose a precise query and the platform returns ranked hits scored by similarity to your input.

title: Search BGCs
text: Search mode helps you find specific biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) when you already have a target in mind — a protein domain combination, a known amino acid sequence, or a chemical structure. Rather than browsing the full catalogue, you pose a precise query and the platform returns ranked hits scored by similarity to your input.

title: Search BGCs
text:
When to use Search
Use Search when you can answer “yes” to any of these questions:

Do you have a protein sequence from a published BGC or a novel enzyme you want to find relatives of?
Do you know the Pfam domains that define the biosynthetic machinery you are interested in (e.g. a ketosynthase plus an acyltransferase)?
Do you have a chemical structure (as a SMILES string) and want to find BGCs whose predicted products resemble it?

If you do not yet have a specific target and want to browse what is available, start with Explore mode instead.

title: Search BGCs
section: The Search interface at a glance
text:
The Search interface at a glance
When you switch to Search mode the dashboard rearranges into two horizontal bands and a sidebar.

Sidebar: Filters and Query tabs
The sidebar on the left has two tabs:

Filters — the same filtering controls available in Explore mode (biome, BGC class, assembly quality, and so on). Filters narrow the dataset before your query runs.
Query — where you build your search. The Query tab contains three independent input sections, one per query type:

Domain Query — search by Pfam protein domain composition.
Sequence Search — search by amino acid sequence similarity.
Chemical Structure Search — search by predicted product structure.


You can fill in one, two, or all three sections. When you combine multiple query types the platform intersects the results — only BGCs that pass every active query appear in the results. Each hit carries the similarity score from the method that produced the lowest score for that BGC, so you can see at a glance which criterion was hardest to satisfy.
At the bottom of the sidebar, the Run Query button launches the search. Because some searches (especially sequence searches) can take a few seconds, the platform runs them asynchronously and shows a progress indicator.


Upper band: the BGC Triad
The top half of the main area shows the BGC Triad — the same three-panel view used in Explore mode, but now populated exclusively with your search hits. The triad comprises:







Panel
What it shows




BGC Roster
A ranked table of matching BGCs, sorted by similarity score. Columns include accession, BGC class, novelty score, domain novelty, size, and the similarity score from your query.


BGC Space Map
A two-dimensional projection (UMAP) of the matching BGCs, coloured by the attribute you choose (class, biome, novelty). Clusters in this map indicate groups of structurally related hits.


BGC Detail
A deep-dive panel that appears when you select a single BGC from the roster or the map. Shows domain architecture, predicted products, neighbouring genes, and links to external databases.





Lower band: the Assembly Triad
The bottom half shows the Assembly Triad. Once you have identified interesting BGCs in the upper band, the assembly triad answers the practical question: which assemblies carry these BGCs, and can I obtain them?
The assembly triad derives its contents from the BGCs currently selected or shortlisted above. It mirrors the three-panel layout:







Panel
What it shows




Assembly Roster
Parent assemblies of the matching BGCs, with quality scores, biome, taxonomic lineage, and culture-collection availability.


Assembly Space Map
UMAP projection of those assemblies, useful for spotting whether your hits come from a single clade or are spread across diverse lineages.


Assembly Detail
Full report on a single assembly: all its BGCs, quality metrics, and links to strain databases.

title: Search BGCs
text:
Step-by-step: running your first search

Switch to Search mode using the mode selector at the top of the dashboard.
Open the Query tab in the sidebar.
Enter at least one query. For example, paste an amino acid sequence into the Sequence Search section.
(Optional) Add filters. Switch to the Filters tab to restrict results to a particular biome or BGC class, then switch back to the Query tab.
Click Run Query. A progress spinner appears while the search executes.
Review the BGC Roster. Hits are ranked by similarity score — the highest-scoring BGC appears first.
Select a BGC to inspect its domain architecture and predicted products in the BGC Detail panel.
Look at the Assembly Triad below to see which assemblies carry your top hits. Apply the Type Strain filter if you need culturable organisms.

title: Search BGCs
text:
Tips

Start broad, then narrow. Run your query without filters first to see the full landscape of hits. Add filters in a second pass if the result set is too large.
Combine query types strategically. Domain queries are fast and tolerant of distant homology; sequence searches are precise but slower. A domain query can serve as a coarse pre-filter, with a sequence search refining the hits.
Check similarity scores carefully. A score of 0.95 in one method does not mean the same thing as 0.95 in another. See Understanding Similarity Scores for guidance on interpreting each metric.
Use the shortlist. Star individual BGCs in the roster to build a shortlist that persists across mode switches. You can then evaluate shortlisted BGCs in Evaluate mode.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text: To explain what the MIBiG database is, what “validated” means in the context of the BGC Discovery Platform, and how validated BGCs serve as the reference set against which every novelty score is calculated.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text: To explain what the MIBiG database is, what “validated” means in the context of the BGC Discovery Platform, and how validated BGCs serve as the reference set against which every novelty score is calculated.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text:
When to use this reference
Consult this page when:

You see the label “validated” next to a BGC and want to know what it means.
You want to understand how the platform calculates Novelty Scores and Nearest Validated Distance.
You encounter MIBiG accessions (such as BGC0000001) in the platform and want to know their significance.
You want to understand why MIBiG BGCs appear as diamond-shaped landmarks on the embedding maps.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text:
What is MIBiG?
MIBiG (Minimum Information about a Biosynthetic Gene cluster) is a curated, public database of biosynthetic gene clusters that have been experimentally characterised. For each entry, a researcher has:

Identified the gene cluster in a sequenced genome.
Linked the gene cluster to a specific natural product through experimental evidence (gene knockout, heterologous expression, chemical isolation, or a combination of these).
Deposited the annotation, including the compound structure, bioactivity data, and literature references, in the MIBiG repository.

MIBiG is maintained by the natural products research community and is the most widely used reference database for biosynthetic gene clusters. As of the current version used by the platform, MIBiG contains approximately 2,500 entries covering a broad range of compound classes.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text:
What “validated” means in the platform
In the BGC Discovery Platform, the label validated is applied exclusively to BGCs that have a corresponding entry in the MIBiG database. A validated BGC is one where:

The gene cluster has been experimentally linked to a known compound.
The compound structure, or at least its compound class, has been determined.
The evidence has been peer-reviewed and deposited in MIBiG.

Validated BGCs represent the “known universe” of biosynthetic gene clusters. Everything else in the platform’s database is unvalidated — computationally predicted but not yet experimentally confirmed to produce a specific product.
The distinction is binary: a BGC is either validated (present in MIBiG) or not. There are no intermediate states. This simplicity is intentional — it provides a clear reference line for novelty calculations.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
section: How validated BGCs anchor novelty scoring
text:
How validated BGCs anchor novelty scoring
Every novelty score in the platform is fundamentally a distance measurement from the nearest validated BGC. The logic is straightforward:

The platform computes an embedding vector for every BGC in the database, including all MIBiG entries.
For each unvalidated BGC, the platform measures the cosine distance between its embedding vector and the embedding of every MIBiG BGC.
The minimum distance — the distance to the single closest MIBiG BGC — becomes the Novelty Score.

A Novelty Score of 0 means the BGC is identical (in embedding space) to a known, characterised BGC. A Novelty Score approaching 1 means it is maximally distant from everything in MIBiG. The further a BGC sits from the “known universe”, the more likely it is to represent genuinely new chemistry.
This design means that novelty scores are always relative to the current state of MIBiG. As the research community characterises new BGCs and adds them to MIBiG, some previously “novel” BGCs will see their scores decrease. This is expected and desirable — it reflects genuine progress in the field.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text:
Validated BGCs on embedding maps
On the UMAP embedding maps (visible in Explore mode, Search mode, and the Assessment Reports), validated BGCs appear as diamond-shaped landmarks rather than the circular dots used for unvalidated BGCs. Each diamond is labelled with the MIBiG accession or compound name.
These landmarks serve as orientation points. When you see an unvalidated BGC sitting near a MIBiG diamond, you can infer that it likely produces something related to the labelled compound. When you see an unvalidated BGC far from any diamond, you are looking at uncharted chemical territory.

Reading the landmarks

Dense clusters of diamonds indicate well-characterised regions of chemical space. Many BGCs in these regions have been experimentally studied, and new BGCs found here are less likely to produce truly novel compounds.
Isolated diamonds represent MIBiG entries that are themselves unusual — characterised BGCs with few close relatives. Unvalidated BGCs near these isolated landmarks may produce interesting variants.
Empty regions (no diamonds nearby) represent the most promising territory for novel discovery. BGCs in these regions have no close experimentally characterised reference.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
section: Validated members in Gene Cluster Families
text:
Validated members in Gene Cluster Families
The concept of validated BGCs extends to Gene Cluster Families (GCFs). Each GCF reports how many of its members are validated. This number determines the family’s characterisation status:







Validated members
Interpretation




Zero
The entire family is uncharacterised. No member has been experimentally linked to a product. This is the highest-value target for discovery.


One or few
The family is partially characterised. You know approximately what the family produces, but most members (and their potential product variants) remain unexplored.


Many
The family is well characterised. Multiple members have been studied, and the family’s chemistry is well understood. Lower discovery potential for the compound itself, though individual members may have unique modifications.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text:
Limitations of the MIBiG reference
While MIBiG is the best available reference for BGC novelty, it has known limitations:

Sampling bias. MIBiG overrepresents BGCs from well-studied organisms (especially Streptomyces species) and underrepresents BGCs from uncultured environmental organisms. A BGC from a soil Streptomyces may appear less novel than it truly is because its relatives are overrepresented in MIBiG.
Compound class bias. Some compound classes (polyketides, NRPs) are far better represented in MIBiG than others (RiPPs, terpenes). Novelty scores for underrepresented classes may be inflated simply because MIBiG contains fewer reference points in those regions of chemical space.
Temporal lag. There is typically a delay between a natural product being published in the literature and the corresponding BGC being deposited in MIBiG. Recently characterised BGCs may not yet appear as validated references.

These limitations do not invalidate the novelty scoring system — they simply mean that novelty scores should be interpreted as relative measures rather than absolute truths. The platform mitigates these biases by using embedding-based distances, which are more robust to sampling gaps than simple sequence identity thresholds.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text:
Tips

Use MIBiG accessions to find literature. When the platform identifies a nearest validated BGC (for example, in the Domain Architecture Comparison section of the BGC report), note the MIBiG accession and look it up on mibig.secondarymetabolites.org for compound structures, bioactivities, and publication references.
Validated BGCs make good positive controls. If you are testing the platform for the first time, try evaluating a MIBiG BGC. It should receive a low Novelty Score and be placed near its own landmark on the embedding map.
Watch for MIBiG updates. The platform periodically updates its MIBiG reference set. After an update, novelty scores may shift. Check the platform’s changelog or About page for the current MIBiG version.
Do not equate “validated” with “important.” Many validated BGCs produce compounds with no known bioactivity. Conversely, some unvalidated BGCs may produce critically important molecules that simply have not been characterised yet.

title: MIBiG and Validated BGCs
text:
Related pages

GCF Explained — how validated member counts define family characterisation status
BGC Assessment Report — where nearest validated BGC comparisons appear
BGC Classes — the classification system for the enzymatic machinery
Glossary — quick definitions of all terms

title: Key Concepts
text: This page defines every technical term you will encounter in the Discovery Platform. Each entry explains what the concept is, why it matters for bioprospecting, and gives a practical example. Terms are listed in the order you are most likely to encounter them.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Biosynthetic Gene Cluster (BGC)
A biosynthetic gene cluster is a contiguous stretch of DNA that contains all the genes an organism needs to produce a specific natural product. A single BGC typically encodes biosynthetic enzymes (which build the molecule), tailoring enzymes (which modify it), regulatory proteins (which control when production is switched on), and transport proteins (which export the product from the cell).
Why it matters: BGCs are the fundamental unit of natural product discovery. If you want to find a new antibiotic, you look for BGCs whose enzymatic machinery and predicted products differ from those already characterised.
Example: The erythromycin BGC in Saccharopolyspora erythraea spans roughly 55 kilobases and encodes three large polyketide synthase proteins, several tailoring enzymes, and a resistance gene. In the platform, this cluster appears as a single row with a classification path of Polyketide.Macrolide.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Assembly
An assembly is a reconstructed genome or genome fragment derived from DNA sequencing. The platform uses “assembly” as the primary unit of organism-level analysis because it is the most general term that covers all three types of input data:

Genome
A genome assembly represents a single organism whose DNA was sequenced in isolation (e.g. a pure culture). Genome assemblies are typically the most complete and contiguous. They are the gold standard for bioprospecting because you can culture the organism and extract its natural products directly.


Metagenome
A metagenome assembly (sometimes called a metagenome-assembled genome or MAG) is reconstructed computationally from a mixed environmental sample. Metagenomes are valuable because they capture organisms that may not grow in standard laboratory conditions. However, a MAG may be incomplete or may contain chimeric contigs from multiple organisms. The platform flags metagenome assemblies so you can factor completeness into your decisions.


Region
A region assembly represents a short genomic fragment — often just one or two contigs — that was submitted to a public database as part of a targeted study. Regions are less informative at the organism level (you cannot compute meaningful diversity or density scores from a single contig), but they may contain high-value BGCs not found elsewhere.
Why it matters: The assembly type tells you how much you can trust the organism-level scores. A genome assembly with high novelty is a stronger lead than a metagenome fragment with the same score.

title: Key Concepts
text:
BGC Class
A BGC class describes the type of enzymatic machinery a BGC uses to assemble its product. It is defined by the core biosynthetic logic, not by the chemical structure of the finished molecule. The platform uses the following top-level classes:








Class
Biosynthetic logic
Typical products




Polyketide
Polyketide synthases (PKS) extend a chain using malonyl-CoA units
Erythromycin, tetracycline, lovastatin


NRP (nonribosomal peptide)
Nonribosomal peptide synthetases (NRPS) assemble peptides without ribosomes
Vancomycin, cyclosporine, daptomycin


RiPP (ribosomally synthesised and post-translationally modified peptide)
Ribosomes make a precursor peptide; enzymes modify it
Nisin, thiopeptides, lanthipeptides


Terpene
Terpene cyclases fold linear isoprenoid precursors
Geosmin, albaflavenone, hopanoids


Saccharide
Glycosyltransferases build or modify sugar chains
Aminoglycosides, glycopeptide sugars


Alkaloid
Diverse enzymatic routes producing nitrogen-containing heterocycles
Rebeccamycin, staurosporine


Other
Does not fit neatly into the above categories
Siderophores, melanins, butyrolactones



Why it matters: BGC class is the first level of filtering. If you are looking for new antibiotics in the macrolide family, you would filter for Polyketide BGCs. If you want novel lanthipeptides, you would filter for RiPP.
Important distinction: BGC class describes the enzymatic machinery, not the product chemistry. Two BGCs with the same class may produce structurally different molecules, and conversely a single molecule may require hybrid machinery (e.g. PKS-NRPS hybrids). For product-level chemical classification, see ChemOnt below.

title: Key Concepts
text:
ChemOnt (Chemical Ontology)
ChemOnt is a hierarchical classification of chemical structures maintained by the ClassyFire project. It organises roughly 4,825 chemical classes into a tree based on structural features — functional groups, ring systems, substituent patterns — rather than biosynthetic origin.
In the platform, each BGC’s predicted natural products are annotated with one or more ChemOnt classes and a probability score indicating confidence. Because ChemOnt describes the product rather than the enzyme, it provides complementary information to the BGC class.
Why it matters: ChemOnt lets you search by chemical structure type. If you need a compound with a specific pharmacophore or scaffold, ChemOnt filters will narrow the results far more precisely than BGC class alone.
Example: A macrolide antibiotic would be classified under Organic compounds > Lipids and lipid-like molecules > Prenol lipids > Terpene lactones > Macrolides and analogues. A polyketide BGC that produces a non-macrolide product would have a completely different ChemOnt path, even though both share the Polyketide BGC class.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Gene Cluster Family (GCF)
A gene cluster family is a group of BGCs from different organisms that share significant sequence and domain similarity. GCFs are computed by clustering all BGCs in the database based on their protein-level embeddings. Each family has a representative BGC (the member closest to the cluster centroid) and summary statistics: member count, mean novelty, and whether any members are validated.
Why it matters: GCF membership tells you how common a BGC’s biosynthetic strategy is across the microbial world. A BGC that belongs to a large GCF with validated members is likely producing a known compound. A BGC that sits alone as a singleton may represent genuinely new chemistry — or it may be a fragment.
Example: The erythromycin-family BGCs from S. erythraea, Aeromicrobium erythreum, and several environmental strains all belong to the same GCF. If you find a new BGC assigned to this family, it probably makes an erythromycin-like macrolide. If your BGC is a singleton with no GCF assignment, it may produce something unprecedented.

title: Key Concepts
text:
MIBiG / Validated BGC
MIBiG (Minimum Information about a Biosynthetic Gene cluster) is a curated repository of BGCs whose products have been experimentally verified. A validated BGC in the platform is one that has a corresponding MIBiG entry, meaning its natural product has been isolated, structurally characterised, and published.
Why it matters: Validated BGCs serve as reference points. The platform computes novelty scores by measuring how far each BGC’s embedding is from the nearest validated BGC. A BGC that is distant from all validated references is more likely to produce something new.
Example: The actinorhodin BGC (MIBiG accession BGC0000194) from Streptomyces coelicolor is a validated type II polyketide BGC. In the BGC Space Map, validated BGCs appear as labelled landmark points so you can see where your candidates sit relative to known chemistry.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Novel Singleton
A novel singleton is a BGC that could not be assigned to any gene cluster family during the clustering process. It has no close relatives in the database.
Why it matters: Novel singletons are high-risk, high-reward candidates. They may encode entirely new biosynthetic pathways, but they may also be partial or misannotated clusters. The BGC Detail Panel provides additional context (completeness, domain architecture, nearest validated distance) to help you distinguish genuine novelty from artefact.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Type Strain
A type strain is the designated reference culture for a bacterial or archaeal species. Type strains are deposited in public culture collections (DSMZ, ATCC, JCM, etc.) and are available for anyone to order.
Why it matters: If an assembly is flagged as a type strain, you can obtain the organism from a culture collection and begin wet-lab work immediately. Non-type-strain assemblies may come from environmental samples that are difficult or impossible to re-culture. The platform provides a direct link to the culture collection catalogue when a type strain URL is available.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Biome Lineage
A biome lineage describes the environment from which an assembly was sampled. The platform uses the GOLD (Genomes OnLine Database) classification scheme, which organises biomes into a hierarchical path separated by dots.
Why it matters: Biome information helps you focus on ecologically relevant organisms. If you are interested in soil-dwelling antibiotic producers, filtering by root.Environmental.Terrestrial.Soil eliminates marine and host-associated assemblies.
Example: An assembly from a hot spring in Yellowstone might have the biome path root.Environmental.Terrestrial.Volcanic. A human gut isolate might have root.Host_associated.Human.Digestive_system.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Pfam Domain
A Pfam domain is a conserved protein domain defined in the Pfam database. Each domain represents a functional or structural unit — for example, a ketosynthase domain (PF00109) or an acyltransferase domain (PF00698) — that can be identified by its amino acid sequence profile.
Why it matters: Pfam domains are the building blocks of BGC architecture. The platform annotates every coding sequence within a BGC with its Pfam domain hits, and these domain annotations power two features: the domain architecture view in the BGC Detail Panel and the domain-based search in Search BGCs mode.
Example: A type I polyketide synthase gene typically contains a repeating series of ketosynthase (KS), acyltransferase (AT), and acyl carrier protein (ACP) domains. Seeing an unusual domain inserted into this pattern — say, a halogenase — would suggest the BGC makes a halogenated product, which could be pharmacologically interesting.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Assembly Scores
Each assembly carries four numerical scores that summarise its biosynthetic potential. These scores are described in detail on the Assembly Scores page:

Novelty (0–1): how different the assembly’s BGCs are from validated references, on average.
Diversity (0–1): what fraction of known BGC classes the assembly covers.
Density (BGCs per megabase): how tightly packed the BGCs are within the assembly.
Taxonomic Novelty (0–1): how underrepresented the assembly’s taxonomic lineage is in the database.

title: Key Concepts
text:
BGC Scores
Each BGC carries its own scores:

Novelty (0–1): the cosine distance between this BGC’s protein embedding and the nearest validated BGC embedding. Higher values mean less similarity to any known, characterised gene cluster.
Domain Novelty (0–1): the fraction of this BGC’s Pfam domains that are not found in any other BGC in the database. A value of 1.0 means every domain is unique to this BGC.
Nearest Validated Distance: the raw cosine distance to the closest MIBiG reference, along with that reference’s accession number.

title: Key Concepts
text:
Related pages

Quickstart — find your first assembly in five minutes
Assembly Scores — detailed explanation of how each score is computed
Filtering — how to use BGC class, ChemOnt, taxonomy, and biome filters
BGC Detail Panel — interpreting domain architecture, chemical compounds, and novelty scores

title: Upload an Assembly for Evaluation
text: To upload a genome assembly from your own sequencing project and receive the same comprehensive assessment report that the platform generates for database entries. The uploaded assembly is processed ephemerally — the platform annotates it, scores it, and generates the report, but never stores your data. When you close the tab or evaluate a different asset, the uploaded data is discarded.

title: Upload an Assembly for Evaluation
text: To upload a genome assembly from your own sequencing project and receive the same comprehensive assessment report that the platform generates for database entries. The uploaded assembly is processed ephemerally — the platform annotates it, scores it, and generates the report, but never stores your data. When you close the tab or evaluate a different asset, the uploaded data is discarded.

title: Upload an Assembly for Evaluation
text:
When to use this workflow
Use this workflow when:

You have assembled a genome or metagenome-assembled genome (MAG) and want to assess its biosynthetic potential before committing to downstream experiments.
A collaborator sent you an assembly and you want a quick comparison against the public catalogue without installing bioinformatics tools locally.
You are deciding between several candidate organisms for heterologous expression and want quantitative novelty and diversity scores for each.

If the assembly you want to evaluate is already in the platform’s database, use the faster Evaluate a Database Assembly workflow instead — it skips the annotation step.

title: Upload an Assembly for Evaluation
text:
Format requirements
The platform accepts a compressed archive in .tar.gz format. The archive must contain one of the following directory structures:

Minimal format: FASTA only
my_assembly/
  contigs.fasta          # Nucleotide FASTA of assembled contigs or scaffolds
The platform will run BGC prediction and annotation on the FASTA sequences. This is the simplest format but requires the most processing time.


Recommended format: pre-annotated
my_assembly/
  contigs.fasta          # Nucleotide FASTA
  contigs.gbk            # GenBank-format annotations (Prokka, DFAST, or similar)
  antismash/             # antiSMASH output directory (optional)
    contigs.region001.gbk
    contigs.region002.gbk
    ...
If you include GenBank annotations and antiSMASH output, the platform can skip the prediction step and go straight to scoring, reducing processing time from minutes to seconds.


File size limits

Maximum archive size: 500 MB
Maximum number of contigs: 10,000
Minimum contig length for BGC prediction: 5,000 bp (shorter contigs are silently skipped)

title: Upload an Assembly for Evaluation
text:
Step-by-step

Switch to Evaluate mode by clicking the Evaluate tab in the mode selector at the top of the dashboard.
Locate the upload area. In the sidebar, below the accession text field, you will see a dashed rectangle labelled “Drop archive here or click to browse”. This is the upload widget.
Upload your archive. Either drag your .tar.gz file from your file manager onto the upload area, or click the area to open a file picker and select the file. The widget shows the file name and size once the file is selected.
Click Upload. The platform begins uploading the file. A progress bar shows the transfer status.
Wait for processing. After upload completes, the platform extracts the archive, validates its contents, and runs the annotation pipeline. A status indicator shows the current step:

Extracting archive — unpacking the .tar.gz contents.
Predicting BGCs — running biosynthetic gene cluster prediction on the nucleotide sequences (skipped if antiSMASH output is included).
Computing embeddings — generating protein-level embedding vectors for each predicted BGC.
Scoring — computing novelty, diversity, and density scores and comparing them against the database.
Generating report — assembling the assessment report.

Processing time depends on assembly size and whether annotations were pre-computed:

Pre-annotated assembly with antiSMASH output: 10–30 seconds
FASTA only, small assembly (<50 contigs): 1–3 minutes
FASTA only, large assembly (500+ contigs): 3–10 minutes

Read the report. The main area displays the Assembly Assessment Report with an Uploaded badge in the header. The report is identical in structure to a database assembly report.

title: Upload an Assembly for Evaluation
text:
What you will see
The report contains all the same sections as a database assembly evaluation:

Priority Ranking — where your assembly would rank if it were added to the database.
Priority Score Radar — spider chart comparing your assembly’s novelty, diversity, and density against the database mean and 90th percentile.
Score Percentile Distributions — gauge charts for each metric.
BGC Triad — roster, space map, and detail panel for the BGCs predicted in your assembly.
Redundancy Matrix — how many of your assembly’s BGCs fall into novel versus known Gene Cluster Families.
BGC Embeddings Map — UMAP projection placing your assembly’s BGCs in the global embedding space.

The only difference is the Uploaded badge and a notice that the data is ephemeral.

title: Upload an Assembly for Evaluation
text:
Tips

Include antiSMASH output if you have it. This dramatically reduces processing time and ensures the platform uses the same annotations you have already reviewed.
Check your FASTA headers. The platform uses the first whitespace-delimited token in each FASTA header as the contig identifier. Avoid special characters (semicolons, pipes) in contig names.
Compress efficiently. Use tar -czf my_assembly.tar.gz my_assembly/ from the directory containing your assembly folder. The archive should unpack into a single top-level directory.
Large assemblies may time out. If your assembly has more than 5,000 contigs, consider filtering to only the longest contigs (those most likely to contain complete BGCs) before uploading.
Your data stays private. The platform processes your upload in an isolated session. No data is written to the database or shared with other users. When you close the tab or evaluate another asset, the uploaded data is purged.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions
text: The platform’s three modes — Explore, Search, and Evaluate — are not isolated silos. At many points in a Search workflow you will want to pivot: evaluate a promising BGC in detail, explore the neighbourhood of a hit in the full catalogue, or track down purchasable strains. This page catalogues every cross-mode action available from Search, explains what each one does, and describes what data carries over.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions
text: The platform’s three modes — Explore, Search, and Evaluate — are not isolated silos. At many points in a Search workflow you will want to pivot: evaluate a promising BGC in detail, explore the neighbourhood of a hit in the full catalogue, or track down purchasable strains. This page catalogues every cross-mode action available from Search, explains what each one does, and describes what data carries over.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when you see an action button in a Search results panel and want to know what it will do, or when you are planning a multi-step analysis that spans modes.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions
text:
Cross-mode actions reference
The table below lists all cross-mode transitions available from within Search mode. Each action appears as a button or menu item in the BGC Detail, Assembly Detail, or roster toolbar.









Action
Where it appears
Target mode
What it does




Find Similar BGCs
BGC Detail panel, BGC roster context menu
Search
Takes the selected BGC’s protein embedding and launches a new Sequence Search using it as the query. Useful for pivoting from one hit to find its relatives.


Evaluate BGC
BGC Detail panel
Evaluate
Opens the selected BGC in Evaluate mode, generating a comprehensive BGC Report with domain architecture, predicted products, novelty analysis, and MIBiG comparisons.


Evaluate Assembly
Assembly Detail panel, Assembly roster context menu
Evaluate
Opens the selected assembly in Evaluate mode, generating an Assembly Report with quality metrics, full BGC inventory, taxonomic placement, and culture-collection links.


Browse Similar Assemblies
Assembly Detail panel
Explore
Switches to Explore mode centred on the selected assembly’s neighbourhood in the Assembly Space Map. Assemblies with similar BGC profiles cluster together, letting you discover related organisms.


Find in Purchasable Strains
Assembly Detail panel, Assembly roster toolbar (batch)
Explore (filtered)
Switches to Explore mode with the Type Strain filter pre-applied and the Assembly Space Map zoomed to the region around the selected assembly. This surfaces culturable organisms that are genomically similar.


Add to Shortlist
BGC roster star icon, Assembly roster star icon
Stays in current mode
Adds the BGC or assembly to your shortlist. The shortlist persists across all mode switches and can be reviewed from any mode.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions
text:
Step-by-step: common cross-mode workflows

From a search hit to a full BGC evaluation

Run a search (domain, sequence, or chemical) and review the results in the BGC Roster.
Click a high-scoring BGC to open its BGC Detail panel.
Click Evaluate BGC in the detail panel.
The platform switches to Evaluate mode and generates a BGC Report. The report includes:

Complete domain architecture, gene by gene.
Predicted natural product structure (if available).
Novelty analysis against the MIBiG reference database.
Links to homologous BGCs and related literature.

From the BGC Report, you can navigate to the parent assembly’s evaluation or return to Search.



From a search hit to exploring its neighbourhood

In the BGC Roster, identify a BGC that scored well but belongs to an unfamiliar class or biome.
Open the Assembly Detail for its parent assembly (click the assembly accession in the BGC Detail panel).
Click Browse Similar Assemblies.
The platform switches to Explore mode, centred on that assembly in the Assembly Space Map. Nearby points represent assemblies with similar BGC profiles.
Pan and zoom the map to discover organisms in the same ecological or taxonomic neighbourhood. This is useful for understanding the broader distribution of your BGC type.



From a search hit to purchasable strains

Star several promising BGCs in the BGC Roster to add them to your shortlist.
The Assembly Triad below populates with the parent assemblies of your shortlisted BGCs.
In the Assembly Roster, click Find in Purchasable Strains (available as a batch action in the toolbar).
The platform switches to Explore mode with the Type Strain filter active. The Assembly Space Map zooms to the region containing your shortlisted assemblies.
The roster now shows only assemblies that correspond to strains available from culture collections, sorted by proximity to your original hits. Each entry includes a direct link to the culture collection catalogue.
Select an assembly and click Evaluate Assembly to review its full Assembly Report before placing an order.



Pivoting to find more relatives of a hit

In the BGC Detail panel for a search hit, click Find Similar BGCs.
The platform stays in Search mode but opens a new Sequence Search pre-loaded with the selected BGC’s protein sequence as the query.
Adjust the similarity threshold if desired and click Run Query.
The new results show all BGCs in the database that are similar to your original hit — potentially including BGCs that did not match your first query because they lack the required domains or chemical similarity, but are nonetheless close relatives at the sequence level.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions
text:
What data carries over
Understanding what persists across mode transitions helps you work efficiently:








Data
Persists?
Notes




Shortlist
Yes
Your shortlisted BGCs and assemblies are available in every mode.


Active filters
Partially
When a transition pre-applies a filter (e.g. Type Strain for “Find in Purchasable Strains”), it replaces your current filter set. Other transitions preserve filters where they apply to both modes.


Search query
No
Your query is specific to Search mode. When you return to Search, the query form retains your last inputs, but navigating to Explore or Evaluate does not carry the query.


Selected BGC/Assembly
Yes
The item you clicked to trigger the transition becomes the focal item in the target mode.


Sort order
No
Each mode has its own default sort. The target mode’s default applies.

title: Cross-Mode Transitions
text:
Tips

Use the shortlist as your bridge. Star items in Search, then switch to Explore or Evaluate. The shortlist is the most reliable way to carry a set of candidates between modes.
Use “Find Similar BGCs” iteratively. Start with one search, find a strong hit, then pivot with “Find Similar BGCs” to explore its neighbourhood in sequence space. Each iteration can uncover a different slice of diversity.
Combine “Browse Similar Assemblies” with biome filters. After switching to Explore mode, apply a biome filter to see whether your hit’s relatives appear in specific environments (e.g. only in marine assemblies, or distributed across soil and freshwater).
Return to Search easily. The mode selector at the top of the dashboard always lets you switch back. Your last query inputs are preserved in the Query tab.
