title: Export Formats
text: To describe every export format available in the BGC Discovery Platform, what each file contains, and where in the interface you can trigger each export. This page serves as a quick reference when you need to know what format to expect or how to use the exported files in downstream tools.

title: Export Formats
text: To describe every export format available in the BGC Discovery Platform, what each file contains, and where in the interface you can trigger each export. This page serves as a quick reference when you need to know what format to expect or how to use the exported files in downstream tools.

title: Export Formats
text:
When to use this reference
Consult this page when:

You are about to export a shortlist and want to know what format the file will be in.
You received an export file and want to understand its columns or structure.
You need to choose between export formats for a particular downstream workflow (for example, importing into a spreadsheet versus loading into a sequence analysis tool).

title: Export Formats
text:
Export summary table









Export
Format
Triggered from
Contains




Assembly Shortlist
CSV
Shortlist panel, “Export Assemblies” button
Accessions, organism names, scores, type strain flag


BGC Shortlist
GenBank .gbk
Shortlist panel, “Export BGCs” button
One annotated SeqRecord per BGC


Assembly Stats
JSON or TSV
Assembly Detail panel, “Download Stats” button
Full score breakdown for one assembly


BGC Stats
JSON or TSV
BGC Detail panel, “Download Stats” button
Full score breakdown for one BGC


Assessment Report
JSON
Assessment Report header, “Export Report” button
Complete assessment report data

title: Export Formats
text:
Assembly Shortlist (CSV)

What it contains
A comma-separated values (CSV) file with one row per assembly in your shortlist. The file includes a header row and the following columns:







Column
Description




accession
The assembly accession (for example, GCA_003150795.1).


organism_name
The organism or sample name associated with the assembly.


taxonomy
The full taxonomic lineage (domain; phylum; class; order; family; genus; species).


biome
The biome lineage path, if available (for example, root:Environmental:Terrestrial:Soil). Empty for isolate genomes.


bgc_count
The number of BGCs detected in this assembly.


novelty
The assembly Novelty Score (0–1).


diversity
The assembly Diversity Score (0–1).


density
The assembly Density (BGCs per megabase).


taxonomic_novelty
The Taxonomic Novelty Score (0–1).


type_strain
TRUE or FALSE, indicating whether this assembly corresponds to a type strain deposited in a public culture collection.





How to trigger

Open the Shortlist panel by clicking the star icon in the toolbar.
Switch to the Assemblies tab in the shortlist.
Click Export Assemblies.
The CSV file downloads to your browser’s default download location.



Downstream use
The CSV can be opened directly in Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets, or any spreadsheet application. It can also be loaded into R (read.csv()) or Python (pandas.read_csv()) for further analysis. The accession column can be used to retrieve sequences from INSDC databases (ENA, NCBI GenBank, DDBJ).

title: Export Formats
text:
BGC Shortlist (GenBank)

What it contains
A multi-record GenBank file (.gbk) with one sequence record per BGC in your shortlist. Each record includes:

The full nucleotide sequence of the BGC region.
CDS features with protein translations, gene names, and functional annotations.
Pfam domain annotations as /sec_met_domain= qualifiers.
BGC class and predicted product annotations.
The organism name and assembly accession in the source feature.



How to trigger

Open the Shortlist panel by clicking the star icon in the toolbar.
Switch to the BGCs tab in the shortlist.
Click Export BGCs.
The GenBank file downloads to your browser’s default download location.



Downstream use
The GenBank file is compatible with standard bioinformatics tools:

antiSMASH — upload the file for additional analysis or comparison with other BGC databases.
Artemis or SnapGene — open the file to view the gene cluster annotation graphically.
BiG-SCAPE or BiG-SLiCE — use the file as input for BGC networking and classification.
Biopython — parse the file with Bio.SeqIO.parse("shortlist.gbk", "genbank") for programmatic analysis.

Each record in the file is self-contained. You can split the multi-record file into individual files using standard tools if a downstream application requires single-record input.

title: Export Formats
text:
Assembly Stats (JSON or TSV)

What it contains
A complete score breakdown for a single assembly, including all metrics visible in the Assembly Detail panel plus additional metadata.
JSON format:
{
  "accession": "GCA_003150795.1",
  "organism_name": "Streptomyces sp. ABC123",
  "scores": {
    "novelty": 0.72,
    "diversity": 0.85,
    "density": 14.3,
    "taxonomic_novelty": 0.61
  },
  "bgc_count": 28,
  "genome_size_mb": 8.2,
  "type_strain": false,
  "biome": "root:Environmental:Terrestrial:Soil"
}
TSV format: The same fields arranged as a tab-separated header row and a single data row.


How to trigger

Select an assembly in the Assembly Roster to open the Assembly Detail panel.
Click the Download Stats button in the panel header.
Choose JSON or TSV from the format selector.

title: Export Formats
text:
BGC Stats (JSON or TSV)

What it contains
A complete score breakdown for a single BGC, including novelty scores, domain list, GCF membership, and predicted products.
JSON format:
{
  "bgc_id": "BGC_XXXX_region001",
  "assembly_accession": "GCA_003150795.1",
  "bgc_class": "Polyketide.Macrolide",
  "scores": {
    "novelty_score": 0.81,
    "domain_novelty": 0.45,
    "nearest_validated_distance": 0.78
  },
  "gcf": {
    "family_id": "GCF_00234",
    "member_count": 47,
    "validated_count": 0
  },
  "domains": ["PF00109", "PF00698", "PF02801"],
  "chemont_predictions": [
    {"term": "Macrolides and analogues", "probability": 0.82}
  ]
}
TSV format: The same fields flattened into a tab-separated header row and a single data row. Nested fields (like domain lists) are represented as semicolon-delimited strings.


How to trigger

Select a BGC in the BGC Roster to open the BGC Detail panel.
Click the Download Stats button in the panel header.
Choose JSON or TSV from the format selector.

title: Export Formats
text:
Assessment Report (JSON)

What it contains
The complete data underlying an Assembly or BGC Assessment Report, including all scores, percentile ranks, novelty decomposition values, GCF placement data, and redundancy matrix entries. This is the most comprehensive export and contains everything needed to reconstruct the visual report programmatically.


How to trigger

Generate an assessment report by evaluating an assembly or BGC (see Evaluate Overview).
In the report header, click Export Report.
The JSON file downloads immediately.



Downstream use
The assessment report JSON is designed for programmatic consumption. Use it to:

Build custom comparison tables across multiple evaluated assets.
Feed assessment data into institutional databases or LIMS (Laboratory Information Management Systems).
Generate publication-quality figures from the raw score data using R, Python, or any plotting tool.

title: Export Formats
text:
Tips

Export early, export often. Shortlists persist in your browser, but exporting a CSV or GenBank file gives you a permanent, portable record of your candidates.
Use JSON for programmatic workflows, TSV for spreadsheets. The JSON format preserves nested structures (like domain lists and ChemOnt predictions) more faithfully, while TSV is easier to open in Excel.
The GenBank export is your bridge to wet-lab follow-up. The exported BGC sequences contain enough annotation to design primers, plan cloning strategies, or set up heterologous expression experiments.
Assessment report JSON is the most complete export. If you are unsure which format you need, export the assessment report — it contains a superset of the information in all other export types.

title: Export Formats
text:
Related pages

Building and Exporting Your Shortlists — how to build shortlists and manage their contents
Assembly Assessment Report — the visual report whose data is exported as JSON
BGC Assessment Report — the visual report whose data is exported as JSON
Scores and Metrics Reference — definitions of every score that appears in exports
Glossary — quick definitions of all terms

title: The Assembly Space Map
text: The Assembly Space Map is an interactive scatter plot that shows every assembly in your current filtered set as a single point. Each axis represents one of the four assembly scores (Novelty, Diversity, Density, Taxonomic Novelty), so the position of each point tells you how that assembly ranks on two dimensions simultaneously. This page explains how to read the map, configure its axes, and use it to find candidates.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text: The Assembly Space Map is an interactive scatter plot that shows every assembly in your current filtered set as a single point. Each axis represents one of the four assembly scores (Novelty, Diversity, Density, Taxonomic Novelty), so the position of each point tells you how that assembly ranks on two dimensions simultaneously. This page explains how to read the map, configure its axes, and use it to find candidates.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when:

You want to understand what the scatter plot in the centre of the Explore Assemblies screen represents.
You want to change the axes to explore a different pair of scores.
You want to interpret clusters, outliers, and empty regions in the plot.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
Where the map appears
The Assembly Space Map occupies the centre panel of the Assembly Triad (the top half of the Explore Assemblies screen). It sits between the Assembly Roster on the left and the Assembly Stats panel on the right.
The map updates automatically whenever you change filters or re-run a query. Only assemblies that pass your current filter criteria appear as points.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
Reading the map

Axes
By default, the x-axis shows Diversity and the y-axis shows Novelty. You can change either axis using the dropdown selectors above the plot. The available axis options are:








Axis option
What it represents
Scale




Diversity
Fraction of known BGC classes represented
0 – 1


Novelty
Average distance of BGCs from validated references
0 – 1


Density
BGCs per megabase of assembled sequence
Open-ended (typically 0 – 6)


Taxonomic Novelty
Underrepresentation of the assembly’s genus
0 – 1





Point colour
Points are coloured by top-level taxonomy (phylum or equivalent rank). A legend at the bottom of the plot shows the colour assignments. This colouring helps you spot taxonomic patterns — for example, whether all the high-novelty assemblies come from a single phylum or are scattered across the tree of life.


Point size
All points are the same size in the default view. When you select an assembly (by clicking a roster row or a point), the selected point becomes larger and gains a highlight ring.


Tooltips
Hovering over a point displays a tooltip with:

Assembly accession
Organism name
The x-axis and y-axis values
Type strain status (if true)

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
Interacting with the map

Click to select
Click any point to select the corresponding assembly. This action:

Highlights the point in the map with a ring and increased size.
Scrolls the Assembly Roster to the corresponding row and highlights it.
Populates the BGC Triad (bottom half of the screen) with the BGCs belonging to that assembly.



Pan and zoom

Zoom in: Use your mouse scroll wheel or pinch gesture on a trackpad.
Zoom out: Scroll in the opposite direction or use the zoom-out button in the plot toolbar.
Pan: Click and drag on an empty area of the plot to move the viewport.
Reset view: Double-click on an empty area or use the reset button in the toolbar to return to the default zoom level.



Lasso selection
Some configurations support lasso (freehand) selection. Click the lasso icon in the toolbar, then draw a region around a group of points. The Assembly Roster updates to show only the assemblies within the lasso. This is useful when you spot an interesting cluster and want to inspect just those assemblies.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
Configuring the axes
To change an axis, click the dropdown selector above the plot and choose a different score. Common axis combinations and what they reveal:

Diversity (x) vs. Novelty (y) — Default
This is the recommended starting view. It answers the question: “Which assemblies combine broad biosynthetic repertoires with high novelty?”

Upper-right quadrant: High diversity, high novelty. These assemblies have many different BGC classes and their BGCs are unlike validated references. These are the strongest candidates for discovering new chemistry.
Upper-left quadrant: Low diversity, high novelty. These assemblies specialise in one or two BGC classes, but those BGCs are highly novel. Worth investigating if the BGC class matches your interest.
Lower-right quadrant: High diversity, low novelty. Versatile producers whose BGCs closely resemble known compounds. Useful for finding new variants of established compound families.
Lower-left quadrant: Low diversity, low novelty. Well-characterised organisms with a narrow biosynthetic repertoire. Least interesting for bioprospecting.



Novelty (x) vs. Density (y)
This combination answers: “Which assemblies are both novel and biosynthetically prolific?”

Upper-right quadrant: High novelty, high density. These organisms have packed their genomes with unusual BGCs. Strong candidates.
Points near the x-axis (zero density) may be Region assemblies without a known genome size, where density cannot be computed.



Taxonomic Novelty (x) vs. Novelty (y)
This combination answers: “Are the novel assemblies from underexplored lineages?”

Upper-right quadrant: High taxonomic novelty, high BGC novelty. These are rare organisms with unusual BGCs — the strongest signal that genuinely new chemistry may be present.
Lower-right quadrant: High taxonomic novelty, low BGC novelty. A rare organism whose BGCs nonetheless resemble known compounds. The organism itself is interesting, but its chemistry may be familiar.



Diversity (x) vs. Density (y)
This combination answers: “Which assemblies are both diverse and densely packed with BGCs?”

Upper-right quadrant: An organism that not only has many BGCs per megabase but also covers many different chemical classes. Likely an exceptional natural product producer.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
Interpreting clusters and outliers

Clusters
A dense cluster of points in the map means that many assemblies have similar scores on both axes. This often corresponds to a well-studied clade. For example, Streptomyces genomes tend to cluster at moderate-to-high diversity and moderate novelty, forming a recognisable cloud. Points outside this cloud are the ones most worth investigating.


Outliers
An isolated point far from the main cloud may represent:

A genuinely exceptional assembly with unusual biosynthetic potential.
A metagenome fragment with incomplete data, leading to extreme score values.
A data quality artefact (e.g. an assembly with a single highly novel but partial BGC).

Always click an outlier to inspect its BGCs in the BGC Triad before adding it to your shortlist.


Empty regions
Large empty areas in the plot indicate score combinations that no assembly achieves. For example, the upper-right corner of the Diversity vs. Density plot may be empty because the most diverse genomes tend to be large (diluting density). Understanding which regions are naturally empty helps you calibrate your expectations.

title: The Assembly Space Map
section: The map and the Assembly Roster
text:
The map and the Assembly Roster
The map and the roster are linked. Clicking a point in the map highlights the corresponding roster row and vice versa. Sorting the roster does not change the map, but filtering (via the sidebar filters) updates both the roster and the map simultaneously.
If you use lasso selection in the map, the roster temporarily shows only the selected assemblies. Click Clear Selection in the roster header to return to the full filtered set.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
Tips

When the map is crowded, zoom into a region of interest before clicking individual points. This reduces accidental mis-clicks.
Use the Taxonomy colour legend to spot clades. If a colour dominates the high-novelty region, it may be worth filtering to that phylum and exploring it in the roster.
Switch axes frequently. Each combination reveals a different facet of the data. There is no single “best” axis pair.
The map respects your current filters. If you see too few points, check that you have not over-constrained with filters.
Type strain assemblies do not receive special visual treatment in the map by default, but you can identify them via tooltips. Enable the Type Strain toggle if you want to see only type strains.

title: The Assembly Space Map
text:
Related pages

Assembly Scores — detailed explanation of each score shown on the axes
Explore Assemblies — the full Explore workflow, of which the map is one component
Filtering — how filter changes affect the map
The BGC Triad — what happens when you select an assembly in the map

title: Explore Assemblies
text: Use Explore Assemblies mode to screen the full catalogue of indexed assemblies without a specific molecular hypothesis. You apply filters, sort by score, and progressively narrow the field until you find assemblies whose biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) are worth pursuing in the laboratory.

title: Explore Assemblies
text: Use Explore Assemblies mode to screen the full catalogue of indexed assemblies without a specific molecular hypothesis. You apply filters, sort by score, and progressively narrow the field until you find assemblies whose biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) are worth pursuing in the laboratory.

title: Explore Assemblies
text:
When to use this mode
Choose Explore Assemblies when:

You are beginning a bioprospecting campaign and want to survey what is available.
You have a taxonomic, ecological, or biosynthetic class preference but no specific sequence or structure target.
You want to compare assemblies side by side on standardised scores (novelty, diversity, density).
You want to visually scan the landscape of assemblies using the Assembly Space Map.

If you already know the domain architecture, protein sequence, or chemical structure you want, use Search BGCs mode instead. If you have a single candidate you want to evaluate in depth, use Evaluate Asset mode.

title: Explore Assemblies
text:
Layout
The Explore Assemblies screen is divided into two triads:

Assembly Triad (top half)








Panel
Position
Purpose




Assembly Roster
Left
Paginated, sortable table of assemblies matching your filters


Assembly Space Map
Centre
Scatter plot with configurable axes, one point per assembly


Assembly Stats
Right
Summary statistics: taxonomy sunburst, score distributions, type strain counts





BGC Triad (bottom half)








Panel
Position
Purpose




BGC Roster
Left
Table of BGCs belonging to the selected or shortlisted assemblies


BGC Space Map
Centre
UMAP scatter plot of BGCs with MIBiG landmarks


BGC Stats
Right
BGC class distribution, ChemOnt sunburst, completeness counts



The BGC Triad is empty until you select an assembly (click a row) or activate your assembly shortlist. A badge at the top of the BGC Triad indicates the source of the currently displayed BGCs: “Selected assembly,” “Shortlisted assemblies,” or “All assemblies.”
For a detailed guide to the BGC Triad, see The BGC Triad.

title: Explore Assemblies
text:
Step-by-step workflow

1. Open the filter sidebar
Click the funnel icon on the left edge of the screen. The sidebar slides in, showing all available filters. See the Filtering page for a detailed reference of every filter.


2. Set your high-level strategy
Before adjusting individual filters, decide on your screening strategy:

Culturability-first: Enable the Type Strain toggle to restrict results to organisms you can order from a culture collection.
Novelty-first: Leave Type Strain off, and plan to sort by Novelty later. This casts the widest net.
Ecology-focused: Set a biome lineage filter (e.g. root.Environmental.Aquatic.Marine) to focus on a particular habitat.
Chemistry-focused: Enable specific BGC class toggles or select ChemOnt classes to focus on a particular type of natural product.



3. Apply taxonomy and biome filters
Expand the Taxonomy Tree to drill into a lineage. Each node shows the count of matching assemblies. Click a checkbox to include or exclude that lineage. You can select at any rank — phylum, class, order, family, genus, or species.
If you have an ecological focus, set the Biome Lineage filter. Type the first few characters and select from the autocomplete dropdown. Biome paths follow the GOLD classification scheme (e.g. root.Environmental.Terrestrial.Soil).


4. Apply BGC class and ChemOnt filters
Use the BGC Class toggles to enable or disable specific classes of biosynthetic machinery (Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, Terpene, Saccharide, Alkaloid, Other). Only assemblies containing at least one BGC of the selected class(es) will appear.
For finer chemical control, open the ChemOnt tree and select chemical ontology classes. This filters assemblies whose BGCs have predicted products matching the selected ChemOnt nodes.


5. Run the query
Click Run Query. The server returns matching assemblies and populates the Assembly Triad. The roster shows the first page of results; use the pagination controls at the bottom to navigate.


6. Sort the Assembly Roster
Click any column header to sort. The most common sorting strategies:

Novelty (descending): surfaces assemblies whose BGCs are furthest from validated references. Best for discovering new chemistry.
Diversity (descending): surfaces assemblies with the broadest range of BGC classes. Best for finding versatile producers.
Density (descending): surfaces assemblies where BGCs are tightly packed. These organisms have devoted a large fraction of their genome to secondary metabolism.
BGC Count (descending): surfaces assemblies with the most BGCs. Useful when you want the richest genomes.



7. Scan the Assembly Space Map
Look at the scatter plot in the centre panel. Each point represents an assembly. The axes default to Diversity (x) and Novelty (y), but you can change them using the axis selectors. Points are coloured by top-level taxonomy.
Outliers in the upper-right quadrant (high novelty, high diversity) are often the most interesting candidates. Click a point to select the corresponding assembly. For a detailed guide, see The Assembly Space Map.


8. Select an assembly
Click a row in the Assembly Roster or a point in the Assembly Space Map. The BGC Triad populates with the BGCs belonging to that assembly. You can now:

Browse the BGC Roster to see each individual BGC.
Inspect the BGC Space Map to see where the assembly’s BGCs sit relative to validated landmarks.
Review the BGC Stats for class and ChemOnt distributions.



9. Inspect and shortlist BGCs
Click a BGC in the BGC Roster to open the BGC Detail Panel. Evaluate the domain architecture, chemical compound predictions, novelty scores, and completeness status. If the BGC looks promising, click Add to BGC Shortlist.
To shortlist the entire assembly, click the pin icon in the Assembly Roster or use the Add to Assembly Shortlist button.


10. Iterate or switch modes
You can refine your filters and re-run the query at any time. Your shortlists are preserved across filter changes.
When you are ready for a deeper analysis of a single candidate, right-click an assembly or BGC row and select Evaluate to switch to Evaluate Asset mode with that item pre-loaded. To search for BGCs similar to one you have found, right-click and select Find Similar to switch to Search BGCs mode.

title: Explore Assemblies
text:
Detail panels

Assembly detail
Clicking the expand icon on an Assembly Roster row opens a flyout with full metadata:

Assembly accession and organism name
Source database (GTDB, MGnify, etc.)
Assembly type (genome, metagenome, or region) and size in megabases
Biome lineage
Type strain status with a link to the culture collection catalogue
All four scores (Novelty, Diversity, Density, Taxonomic Novelty) with percentile ranks
External links to the source database entry



BGC detail
See the dedicated BGC Detail Panel page.

title: Explore Assemblies
text:
Cross-mode actions
From Explore Assemblies, you can transition to other modes:

Evaluate an assembly: Right-click an assembly row and select Evaluate, or click the Evaluate button in the assembly detail flyout. The platform switches to Evaluate Asset mode and runs a full assessment.
Evaluate a BGC: Right-click a BGC row and select Evaluate. The platform switches to Evaluate Asset mode with that BGC’s assessment report.
Find similar BGCs: Right-click a BGC row and select Find Similar. The platform switches to Search BGCs mode and runs a sequence-similarity search using that BGC’s embedding.
View parent assembly: In the BGC Detail Panel, click the parent assembly link to scroll to and select that assembly in the Assembly Roster.

title: Explore Assemblies
text:
Tips

Use the Assembly Space Map’s configurable axes to explore different score dimensions. Novelty vs. Density often reveals organisms that are both novel and biosynthetically prolific.
After shortlisting several assemblies, click the shortlist badge in the BGC Triad header to view the merged BGC set from all shortlisted assemblies at once.
The Assembly Stats panel updates dynamically as you change filters. Use the taxonomy sunburst to verify that your filters are selecting the lineages you intended.
If the roster shows too many results, add more filters rather than scrolling through hundreds of pages.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text: Every search on the platform produces a similarity score for each hit. This page explains what each score means in plain language, how to interpret its value, and what to watch out for when comparing scores across different search methods.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text: Every search on the platform produces a similarity score for each hit. This page explains what each score means in plain language, how to interpret its value, and what to watch out for when comparing scores across different search methods.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page whenever you are unsure what a similarity score in your search results means, or when you want to set an appropriate threshold for a new query.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text:
The three similarity metrics
The platform uses three different similarity metrics, one per search method. All three produce scores on a 0-to-1 scale, where higher values indicate greater similarity — but the numbers are not directly comparable across methods.

Summary table



Search method
Metric
What is compared
Score range
Typical “good match”




Domain Query
Sorensen-Dice coefficient
Pfam domain sets
0–1
0.7+


Sequence Search
Cosine similarity
Protein embeddings (1,152-dimensional vectors)
0–1
0.7+


Chemical Search
Tanimoto coefficient
Morgan fingerprints of product structures
0–1
0.85+

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text:
Sorensen-Dice coefficient (domain query)

What it measures
The Sorensen-Dice coefficient quantifies the overlap between two sets. In the context of domain queries, it compares the set of Pfam domains you specified in your query (A) with the set of Pfam domains annotated in each BGC (B).


Formula
\[
\text{Dice}(A, B) = \frac{2 \times |A \cap B|}{|A| + |B|}
\]

\(|A \cap B|\) is the number of domains present in both sets.
\(|A|\) and \(|B|\) are the sizes of each set.



Plain-language interpretation
The Dice coefficient answers the question: “Of all the domains mentioned by either my query or the BGC, what fraction is shared?” The factor of 2 in the numerator means that shared domains are double-counted, which makes Dice slightly more generous than the Jaccard index for partial overlaps.


Score guide







Score
Interpretation




1.0
The BGC contains exactly the domains you searched for, and no others. In practice this is rare for large BGCs.


0.8–0.99
Very strong match. The BGC contains most or all of your query domains plus a few additional ones (or vice versa).


0.6–0.8
Moderate match. There is meaningful overlap but several domains differ.


0.4–0.6
Weak match. Some shared domains but substantial differences.


< 0.4
Little overlap. The BGC is unlikely to be closely related to your query.





Things to keep in mind

A small query set (e.g. one domain) can produce high Dice scores for BGCs that contain many domains, because the single shared domain still contributes heavily to the numerator.
Conversely, a very large query set penalises BGCs that contain only a subset of the requested domains. Consider using OR logic if you are not sure all domains will be present.
Dice does not account for domain copy number — a BGC with three ketosynthase copies is treated the same as one with a single copy.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text:
Cosine similarity (sequence search)

What it measures
Cosine similarity compares two protein embeddings — high-dimensional numerical vectors generated by a protein language model (ESM). Each embedding captures the biochemical properties, evolutionary relationships, and structural features of a protein in 1,152 numbers.


Formula
\[
\text{cosine}(\vec{u}, \vec{v}) = \frac{\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}}{|\vec{u}| \times |\vec{v}|}
\]

\(\vec{u} \cdot \vec{v}\) is the dot product of the two vectors.
\(|\vec{u}|\) and \(|\vec{v}|\) are their magnitudes (lengths).



Plain-language interpretation
Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, ignoring their length. If two protein embeddings point in exactly the same direction, the cosine is 1.0 — the proteins are effectively identical in the model’s learned representation. As the angle between them grows, the cosine drops toward 0.


Score guide







Score
Interpretation




0.95–1.0
Near-identical proteins. Likely the same protein or very close orthologs.


0.80–0.95
Strong similarity. Proteins probably share the same function and fold.


0.65–0.80
Moderate similarity. Related protein family; may have divergent functions.


0.50–0.65
Weak similarity. Distant relationship; worth investigating but not guaranteed to be functionally relevant.


< 0.50
Unlikely to be related. The platform uses 0.5 as the lower bound for search results.





Things to keep in mind

Cosine similarity in embedding space does not correspond directly to percent sequence identity. Two proteins at 40% identity might have a cosine similarity of 0.85 if they share the same fold and function; two proteins at 60% identity might score lower if they have diverged functionally.
The embedding captures global protein properties. Local motifs (e.g. a single active-site residue change) may not dramatically alter the embedding.
The search compares your query protein against the representative embedding of each BGC, which summarises all proteins in the cluster. This means a BGC will score high if it contains at least one protein very similar to your query, even if the other proteins in the cluster are unrelated.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text:
Tanimoto coefficient (chemical search)

What it measures
The Tanimoto coefficient compares Morgan fingerprints — binary vectors that encode the substructural features present in each molecule. It quantifies how much of each molecule’s structural inventory is shared with the other.


Formula
\[
T_c(A, B) = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}
\]

\(|A \cap B|\) is the number of fingerprint bits set to 1 in both molecules.
\(|A \cup B|\) is the number of bits set to 1 in at least one molecule.



Plain-language interpretation
The Tanimoto coefficient answers: “Of all the structural features found in either molecule, what fraction is shared by both?” Unlike Dice, there is no double-counting of shared features, making Tanimoto slightly more conservative.


Score guide







Score
Interpretation




0.95–1.0
Near-identical structures. Likely the same compound or a very minor analog (e.g. a single methylation).


0.85–0.95
Close analogs. Same core scaffold with different substituents.


0.70–0.85
Related scaffolds. Recognisably similar chemical family but meaningful structural differences.


0.50–0.70
Loosely related. May share a ring system or functional group but overall different structures.


< 0.50
Unrelated structures.





Things to keep in mind

Tanimoto is sensitive to molecule size. A small molecule compared against a much larger one will score lower than expected, because the larger molecule has many “extra” fingerprint bits that the small molecule lacks.
Stereochemistry is only partially captured by Morgan fingerprints. Two stereoisomers may have identical or near-identical Tanimoto scores despite having different biological activities.
The score reflects the best-matching product for each BGC. If a BGC has multiple predicted products, the highest Tanimoto score is reported.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text:
Combining multiple search methods
When you run more than one query type simultaneously (e.g. a domain query and a sequence search), the platform intersects the result sets. Only BGCs that meet the threshold for every active query appear in the final results.
For each BGC in the intersection, the results display the minimum score across the active methods. This tells you which criterion was hardest for that BGC to satisfy.

Example
You run a domain query (Dice threshold 0.6) and a sequence search (cosine threshold 0.7) together. A BGC scores 0.82 on Dice and 0.75 on cosine. The displayed similarity is 0.75 (the lower of the two), flagging that the sequence match, while above threshold, was the weaker link.

title: Understanding Similarity Scores
text:
Practical guidelines for setting thresholds

Start with defaults. The platform sets reasonable defaults for each method. Run your first search at these settings.
Look at the score distribution. After a search, scan the Similarity column. If there is a clear gap (e.g. several hits at 0.90+ and then a drop to 0.60), that gap often marks a natural boundary between true positives and noise.
Adjust incrementally. Lower or raise the threshold by 0.05 at a time. This gives you fine control without dramatic jumps in result set size.
Consider your goal. If you are doing an initial survey, favour sensitivity (lower thresholds). If you are selecting BGCs for expensive downstream work (e.g. synthesis or cloning), favour specificity (higher thresholds).

title: Filtering Assemblies and BGCs
text: This page is a complete reference for every filter available in the Discovery Platform. After reading it, you will know what each filter does, when to use it, and how it interacts with other filters.

title: Filtering Assemblies and BGCs
text: This page is a complete reference for every filter available in the Discovery Platform. After reading it, you will know what each filter does, when to use it, and how it interacts with other filters.

title: Filtering Assemblies and BGCs
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when:

You want to narrow a large result set to something manageable.
You are unsure what a particular filter controls.
You want to understand how filters combine (AND vs. OR logic).
You need to know which filters carry over when you switch modes.

title: Filtering Assemblies and BGCs
text:
How filters combine
All filters apply simultaneously using AND logic. This means that enabling the Type Strain toggle and selecting a Polyketide BGC class and choosing the phylum Actinomycetota returns only assemblies that satisfy all three conditions: they must be type strains, they must contain at least one polyketide BGC, and they must belong to Actinomycetota.
Within a single multi-select filter (e.g. the BGC Class toggles), options combine with OR logic. If you enable both Polyketide and NRP, the platform returns assemblies containing at least one polyketide BGC or at least one NRP BGC (or both).

title: Filtering Assemblies and BGCs
text:
Filter reference

Organism Search
What it does: A text search box that matches against organism names in the assembly catalogue. Typing “Streptomyces” returns all assemblies whose organism name contains that string. The search is case-insensitive and matches partial words.
When to use it: When you already know the genus or species you want and need to jump straight to it.
Tips:

You can type a strain designation (e.g. “ATCC 29253”) to find a specific culture.
The search matches anywhere in the organism name, so “coelicolor” will find Streptomyces coelicolor even if you omit the genus.
Clear the search box to return to the unfiltered view.



Type Strain Toggle
What it does: When enabled, only assemblies flagged as type strains appear in the results. A type strain is the designated reference culture for a species, deposited in a public culture collection (DSMZ, ATCC, JCM, NCTC, etc.).
When to use it: When culturability is essential to your workflow. Type strains can be ordered from culture collections, which means you can go from a database hit to a growing culture within weeks.
Tips:

Type strains represent a small fraction of the total catalogue. Enabling this toggle dramatically reduces the number of results, which makes manual review feasible.
Each type strain assembly includes a link to its culture collection catalogue entry. You can find this link in the assembly detail panel.
If you are willing to work with environmental DNA or uncultured organisms, leave this toggle off to access the full catalogue.



Assembly Type
What it does: Restricts results to one or more assembly types: Genome, Metagenome, or Region.








Type
Description
Typical use




Genome
Complete or draft genome from an isolated organism
Highest confidence; full organism-level scores available


Metagenome
Metagenome-assembled genome (MAG) from an environmental sample
Access to uncultured diversity; may be incomplete


Region
Short genomic fragment (one or a few contigs) from a targeted study
May contain high-value BGCs; organism-level scores unreliable



When to use it: When you want to control the quality and completeness of the assemblies you review. For the most reliable scoring, filter to Genomes only. To explore uncultured diversity, include Metagenomes.
Tips:

Region assemblies often have zero or near-zero Density scores because their genome size is unknown. Do not sort by Density when including Regions.
You can combine Assembly Type with the Type Strain toggle. Only Genome assemblies can be type strains, so enabling both is redundant but harmless.



Taxonomy Tree
What it does: A hierarchical tree of taxonomic ranks (Domain, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species) derived from the GTDB taxonomy. Each node shows the number of matching assemblies in parentheses. Clicking a checkbox selects that entire clade.
When to use it: When you have a taxonomic hypothesis — for example, you believe actinobacteria are the most promising source of a particular compound class, or you want to exclude a clade you have already screened.
How to use it:

Expand the tree by clicking the arrow icons next to each node.
Click the checkbox next to a node to include all assemblies in that clade.
You can select multiple nodes at any rank. The selections combine with OR logic: selecting both Actinomycetota and Bacillota returns assemblies from either phylum.
To deselect, uncheck the box. To clear all taxonomy filters, click the Reset link.

Tips:

The assembly count next to each node reflects the current filter state. If you have the Type Strain toggle on, the counts show only type strain assemblies in each clade.
You can select at mixed ranks. For example, selecting the phylum Actinomycetota and the genus Pseudomonas (within Pseudomonadota) returns assemblies from the entire Actinomycetota phylum plus all Pseudomonas assemblies.
The taxonomy follows GTDB naming conventions, which may differ from NCBI taxonomy for some clades.



Biome Lineage
What it does: Filters assemblies by the environment from which they were sampled. Biome paths use the GOLD (Genomes OnLine Database) classification scheme and follow a dot-delimited hierarchy.
Format: root.Environmental.Terrestrial.Soil or root.Host_associated.Human.Digestive_system
When to use it: When your research focuses on a specific habitat. Marine microbiologists can filter for root.Environmental.Aquatic.Marine; soil ecologists can filter for root.Environmental.Terrestrial.Soil.
How to use it:

Begin typing in the biome search box. An autocomplete dropdown appears with matching biome paths.
Select the desired biome. The filter matches the selected path as a prefix, so selecting root.Environmental.Aquatic includes all sub-biomes (Marine, Freshwater, Thermal springs, etc.).
To clear, remove the text from the box or click the X icon.

Tips:

Not every assembly has a biome annotation. Assemblies without biome data are excluded when a biome filter is active.
The prefix-matching behaviour means you can be as broad or as specific as you like. Selecting root matches everything (equivalent to no filter).
Biome paths use underscores instead of spaces (e.g. Digestive_system, not Digestive system).



BGC Class Toggles
What it does: A set of toggle buttons, one for each top-level BGC class: Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, Terpene, Saccharide, Alkaloid, and Other. Enabling a toggle restricts results to assemblies containing at least one BGC of that class.
When to use it: When you want to focus on a particular type of biosynthetic machinery. If you are looking for new nonribosomal peptides, enable only the NRP toggle.
How they combine: Multiple enabled toggles combine with OR logic. Enabling Polyketide and NRP returns assemblies with at least one polyketide BGC or at least one NRP BGC.
Tips:

BGC class describes the enzymatic machinery, not the product chemistry. Two polyketide BGCs may produce structurally unrelated molecules. For product-level filtering, use the ChemOnt tree.
The “Other” class is a catch-all that includes siderophores, bacteriocins, melanins, butyrolactones, and other biosynthetic systems that do not fit the six named categories.
Disabling all toggles is equivalent to enabling all of them: no BGC class filter is applied.



ChemOnt Tree
What it does: A hierarchical tree of Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt) classes. ChemOnt classifies molecules by their chemical structure — functional groups, ring systems, and substituent patterns — independently of how they are biosynthesised. The tree contains approximately 4,825 classes.
When to use it: When you want to filter by the predicted chemical structure of the natural products rather than by the biosynthetic machinery. This is the right filter when you need a compound with a specific pharmacophore or structural scaffold.
How to use it:

Expand the tree by clicking arrow icons. Top-level nodes include broad categories like “Organic compounds,” “Lipids and lipid-like molecules,” and “Organoheterocyclic compounds.”
Click a checkbox to select a node and all its descendants.
The platform filters to assemblies containing at least one BGC with a predicted natural product matching the selected ChemOnt class(es).

Tips:

ChemOnt annotations come with probability scores. The platform includes all annotations above a minimum confidence threshold.
ChemOnt and BGC class are independent classification systems. A polyketide BGC may produce a molecule classified as a “Macrolide” in ChemOnt, but another polyketide BGC may produce a “Phenol” — completely different ChemOnt branches.
Not every BGC has ChemOnt annotations. BGCs without predicted natural products are excluded when a ChemOnt filter is active.



Assembly / BGC Accession (Search mode)
What it does: In Search BGCs mode, you can enter a specific assembly accession (e.g. GCA_000203835.1) or BGC accession (e.g. MGYB00000123.ANT.01) to jump directly to that entity. This is not a filter in the traditional sense — it is a direct lookup.
When to use it: When you have an accession from a publication, a collaborator, or a previous session and want to go directly to it.
Tips:

Assembly accessions follow the pattern GCA_XXXXXXXXX.X (GenBank) or GCF_XXXXXXXXX.X (RefSeq).
BGC accessions in the platform follow the structured format MGYB{region_id}.{detector_code}.{bgc_number} (e.g. MGYB00000123.ANT.01).
If the accession is not found, the platform displays a “no results” message.

title: Filtering Assemblies and BGCs
text:
Filter persistence across modes
When you switch between Explore Assemblies, Search BGCs, and Evaluate Asset modes using the header tabs, your filter settings are preserved. This means:

If you set a taxonomy filter in Explore mode and then switch to Search mode, the search results will still be restricted to that taxon.
If you clear all filters in Search mode, they are also cleared when you return to Explore mode.

The only exception is mode-specific query parameters (e.g. the domain list in a domain search, or the SMILES string in a chemical search). These belong to the Search mode and do not carry over to Explore.

title: Filtering Assemblies and BGCs
text:
Resetting filters
To clear all filters at once, click the Reset All button at the bottom of the filter sidebar. This returns the platform to its default state: all assembly types, all BGC classes, no taxonomy restriction, no biome restriction, type strain toggle off.

Assembly Scores — understanding the score columns you see after filtering

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text: To explain how the BGC Discovery Platform uses Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt) annotations to describe the predicted chemical products of biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs), and how this classification complements the enzyme-based BGC class system.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text: To explain how the BGC Discovery Platform uses Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt) annotations to describe the predicted chemical products of biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs), and how this classification complements the enzyme-based BGC class system.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text:
When to use this reference
Consult this page when:

You see a ChemOnt term (such as “Macrolides and analogues” or “Benzene and substituted derivatives”) in the platform and want to know what it represents.
You want to filter BGCs by the type of molecule they are predicted to produce, rather than by the type of enzyme they use.
You are comparing a BGC’s class assignment with its ChemOnt annotation and the two seem contradictory.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text:
What ChemOnt is
ChemOnt (Chemical Ontology) is a hierarchical classification system for chemical compounds, developed as part of the ClassyFire project. It organises molecules into categories based on their structural features — the rings, functional groups, and carbon frameworks that define a compound’s chemical identity.
The BGC Discovery Platform uses ChemOnt to annotate each BGC with a prediction of what kind of molecule its gene cluster is likely to produce. These predictions come from machine learning models that examine the BGC’s domain architecture and compare it against BGCs with known products.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text:
Key properties of ChemOnt annotations

Term format
Each ChemOnt term has a unique identifier in the format CHEMONTID:XXXXXXX (seven digits). In the platform interface, the human-readable name is displayed rather than the identifier, but you can see the full identifier by hovering over any ChemOnt label.


Probability scores
Each ChemOnt annotation carries a probability score between 0 and 1. This score reflects the model’s confidence that the BGC produces a compound belonging to that ChemOnt category. A score of 0.9 means the model is highly confident; a score of 0.3 means the prediction is uncertain.
The platform displays probability scores as coloured bars or numbers next to each ChemOnt term. High-confidence predictions (above 0.7) are shown in bold; low-confidence predictions (below 0.3) are shown in grey italics.


Hierarchical structure
ChemOnt is organised as a tree, from broad categories at the top to specific structural classes at the bottom. For example:
Organic compounds
  Lipids and lipid-like molecules
    Prenol lipids
      Terpene lactones
        Sesquiterpene lactones
A BGC can be annotated at multiple levels of the hierarchy. In general, broader categories receive higher confidence scores (it is easier to predict that a BGC makes “a lipid” than to predict that it makes “a sesquiterpene lactone specifically”).


Ontology size
The full ChemOnt ontology contains approximately 4,825 terms. Not all of them are relevant to microbial natural products. The platform uses a curated subset of terms that appear in the MIBiG database or have been predicted for BGCs in the catalogue.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
section: How ChemOnt differs from BGC class
text:
How ChemOnt differs from BGC class
This distinction is fundamental and worth emphasising:








Property
BGC Class
ChemOnt




What it describes
The enzymatic machinery (the assembly line)
The chemical product (what comes off the line)


Basis
Protein domains detected in the gene cluster
Predicted molecular structure of the product


Example
Polyketide
Macrolides and analogues


Determined by
Domain signature matching
Machine learning model



A single BGC class can produce many different ChemOnt categories. For example, Polyketide BGCs can produce macrolides, aromatic polyketides, enediynes, or linear polyketides — each a different ChemOnt category. Conversely, molecules in the same ChemOnt category can sometimes be produced by different BGC classes (though this is less common).

Practical example
Consider a BGC classified as Polyketide.Macrolide (BGC class) with a ChemOnt annotation of Macrolides and analogues (probability 0.85). The BGC class tells you the gene cluster uses a modular polyketide synthase that terminates with a cyclising thioesterase. The ChemOnt annotation tells you the predicted product is a large-ring lactone. In this case, the two classifications align — but they measure different things and can diverge for unusual BGCs.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
section: Novel BGCs and missing ChemOnt annotations
text:
Novel BGCs and missing ChemOnt annotations
Novel BGCs — those with high Novelty Scores — frequently lack ChemOnt annotations or carry only low-confidence predictions. This happens because the machine learning model was trained on characterised BGCs, and it struggles to predict products for gene clusters that are very different from anything in the training set.
A missing or low-confidence ChemOnt annotation on a high-novelty BGC is not a problem; it is the expected outcome. The absence of a confident prediction is itself informative: it reinforces the signal that this BGC produces something unlike known compounds.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text:
How to filter by ChemOnt
In the platform’s filter panel (available in Explore and Search modes), the ChemOnt filter presents a searchable dropdown of terms. You can:

Type a term name to search for it (for example, “macrolide” or “alkaloid”).
Select one or more terms. The filter restricts results to BGCs annotated with at least one of the selected terms above the minimum probability threshold.
Adjust the probability threshold. A slider below the term selector lets you set the minimum confidence required. The default is 0.5, which excludes low-confidence predictions.

Selecting a parent term in the hierarchy includes all its children. For example, selecting “Lipids and lipid-like molecules” will include BGCs annotated as “Prenol lipids”, “Fatty acids”, and all other subcategories.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text:
Tips

Use ChemOnt when you have a target product in mind. If you are looking for BGCs that might produce a macrolide antibiotic, filter by the ChemOnt term “Macrolides and analogues” rather than filtering by the Polyketide BGC class (which includes many non-macrolide polyketides).
Lower the probability threshold for exploratory searches. Setting the threshold to 0.3 instead of 0.5 will include more speculative predictions but may surface interesting candidates that a strict filter would miss.
Combine ChemOnt with BGC class for precision. Filtering by both Polyketide (BGC class) and Macrolides and analogues (ChemOnt) gives you the subset of polyketide BGCs specifically predicted to produce macrolides.
Do not rely on ChemOnt alone for novel BGCs. High-novelty BGCs often have weak or absent ChemOnt predictions. If you are specifically hunting for novelty, use the Novelty Score as your primary filter and treat ChemOnt as supplementary information.

title: Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt)
text:
Related pages

BGC Classes — the enzyme-based classification system
Scores and Metrics Reference — how ChemOnt predictions relate to novelty decomposition
Biome Ontology — another hierarchical classification used for filtering
Glossary — quick definitions of all terms

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
The BGC Discovery Platform is a web application that helps experimental biochemists identify organisms and gene clusters worth pursuing in the laboratory. It indexes thousands of assemblies — genomes, metagenomes, and genomic regions — together with the biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) they carry. Each BGC encodes the enzymatic machinery that an organism uses to produce a natural product: an antibiotic, a siderophore, a pigment, or any of the thousands of small molecules that microbes synthesise to survive.
Rather than reading through spreadsheets of accession numbers and BLAST reports, the platform lets you browse, filter, score, and compare assemblies and their BGCs in an interactive dashboard. You start with the full catalogue and narrow it down until you have a shortlist of candidates ready for wet-lab follow-up.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
The BGC Discovery Platform is a web application that helps experimental biochemists identify organisms and gene clusters worth pursuing in the laboratory. It indexes thousands of assemblies — genomes, metagenomes, and genomic regions — together with the biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) they carry. Each BGC encodes the enzymatic machinery that an organism uses to produce a natural product: an antibiotic, a siderophore, a pigment, or any of the thousands of small molecules that microbes synthesise to survive.
Rather than reading through spreadsheets of accession numbers and BLAST reports, the platform lets you browse, filter, score, and compare assemblies and their BGCs in an interactive dashboard. You start with the full catalogue and narrow it down until you have a shortlist of candidates ready for wet-lab follow-up.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
Who is it for?
The platform is designed for researchers who:

Work at the bench and need to choose which organisms to culture, extract, or engineer.
Have chemistry or biology training but do not routinely run bioinformatics pipelines.
Want to prioritise novelty — assemblies whose BGCs are unlike anything already characterised.
Need to evaluate whether a particular assembly or BGC is worth the investment of time and reagents.

If you already have a target organism, the platform can tell you how its biosynthetic potential compares with the rest of the database. If you are starting from scratch, it can help you find the most promising candidates.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
Two entity levels
Everything in the platform revolves around two levels of biological organisation:
Assemblies are the organisms (or communities) you might choose to work with. Each assembly is a sequenced genome, a metagenome-assembled genome (MAG), or a genomic region from an environmental sample. An assembly carries one or more BGCs, and the platform scores each assembly on how novel, diverse, and dense its BGC repertoire is.
Biosynthetic Gene Clusters (BGCs) are the stretches of DNA that encode the enzymes, regulators, and transport proteins needed to produce a natural product. Each BGC belongs to one assembly and is classified by the type of enzymatic machinery it uses (its BGC class) and by the chemical structure of the product it is predicted to make (its ChemOnt classification). The platform scores each BGC on how different it is from known, validated gene clusters.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
Three modes of operation
The dashboard is organised into three modes, accessible via tabs in the header. Filters you set in one mode carry over when you switch to another.

Explore Assemblies
Browse the full assembly catalogue without a specific hypothesis. Apply filters — taxonomy, BGC class, biome, type strain status — and sort the results by novelty, diversity, or density. The Assembly Space Map lets you see where each assembly sits relative to the rest of the catalogue. Select an assembly to inspect its BGCs in the BGC Triad below.
Use this mode when you want to screen broadly and discover candidates you might not have considered. See Explore Assemblies for a full walkthrough.


Search BGCs
Query the database with a specific molecular target in mind. You can search by Pfam domain combination (to find BGCs with a particular enzymatic architecture), by protein sequence (to find BGCs encoding similar enzymes), or by chemical structure (to find BGCs whose predicted products resemble a compound of interest). Results are ranked by similarity, and you can pivot from a hit BGC to its parent assembly with one click.
Use this mode when you already know what kind of chemistry or enzyme you are looking for.


Evaluate Asset
Perform a deep assessment of a single assembly or BGC. The platform computes percentile ranks, novelty breakdowns, redundancy analyses, and chemical-space context to help you decide whether a candidate is worth pursuing. You can evaluate assets already in the database or upload your own GenBank or FASTA files.
Use this mode when you have narrowed your search to a handful of candidates and need to make a final decision.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
Shortlists
As you work, you can pin assemblies and BGCs to your shortlists. Each shortlist holds up to 20 items. Assembly shortlists export as CSV files with scores and metadata. BGC shortlists export as multi-record GenBank files suitable for downstream annotation tools.
Shortlists persist in your browser between sessions and remain available across all three modes. See Building and Exporting Your Shortlists for details.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
What the platform indexes
The database behind the platform is built from public genomic repositories and in-house annotation pipelines. At a high level, the data flow works like this:

Genome assemblies are collected from GTDB (the Genome Taxonomy Database), MIBiG (the curated repository of validated BGCs), and MGnify (EBI’s metagenomics analysis service).
BGC detection tools (such as antiSMASH) scan each assembly for biosynthetic gene clusters. Every detected BGC is recorded with its coordinates, classification, and detection metadata.
Protein language models (ESM) compute embedding vectors for each BGC’s proteins. These 1,152-dimensional vectors capture the overall biosynthetic character of the cluster and enable similarity searches that go beyond simple sequence alignment.
Chemical annotation pipelines predict the natural products each BGC is likely to produce and classify them using the ChemOnt ontology.
Scoring algorithms compute novelty, diversity, density, and taxonomic novelty for every assembly and BGC. Gene cluster families (GCFs) are built by clustering BGC embeddings.
UMAP projections reduce the high-dimensional embedding space to two dimensions for the scatter plots you see in the dashboard.

All of this preprocessing happens before you open the platform. When you interact with the dashboard, you are querying precomputed scores and indexes, which is why the interface responds in seconds even across thousands of assemblies.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
A typical workflow
Most users follow a funnel-shaped workflow:

Set broad filters in Explore Assemblies mode. Start with a taxonomic restriction (e.g. Actinomycetota) and optionally enable the Type Strain toggle.
Sort by novelty in the Assembly Roster to surface the most promising organisms.
Scan the Assembly Space Map to spot outliers and clusters.
Select an assembly to inspect its BGCs in the BGC Triad.
Evaluate individual BGCs using the BGC Detail Panel — check domain architecture, novelty scores, completeness, and chemical predictions.
Shortlist the best assemblies and BGCs as you go.
Switch to Evaluate mode for your top candidates to get a comprehensive assessment report with percentile ranks, redundancy analysis, and chemical-space context.
Export your shortlists as CSV (assemblies) or GenBank (BGCs) for wet-lab follow-up.

This workflow is not mandatory. You can jump into Search mode with a specific query, skip Explore entirely, or start in Evaluate mode with a known accession. The three modes are designed to complement each other, and your filters and shortlists carry over between them.

title: BGC Discovery Platform
text:
Where to go next

First time? Start with the Quickstart — it walks you through finding an assembly in five minutes.
Unfamiliar with BGC terminology? Read the Key Concepts page first.
Ready to explore? Jump to the Explore Assemblies overview.
Have a target in mind? See the Search BGCs overview for domain, sequence, and chemical queries.
Evaluating a candidate? See the Evaluate Asset overview for deep assessment.
Want to understand the scores? Read Assembly Scores and the BGC Detail Panel guide.
Managing your selections? See Building and Exporting Your Shortlists.

title: Tutorials
text:
This section contains guided tutorials that walk you through complete workflows from start to finish. Each tutorial begins with a specific research question, shows you every click and decision along the way, and ends with a concrete result you can take to the bench.
Unlike the reference pages (which explain individual features) or the mode overviews (which describe the interface layout), tutorials follow a narrative arc: you start with a question, work through the platform, and arrive at an answer.

title: Tutorials
text:

Unlike the reference pages (which explain individual features) or the mode overviews (which describe the interface layout), tutorials follow a narrative arc: you start with a question, work through the platform, and arrive at an answer.

title: Tutorials
text:
Before you start
All tutorials assume you have read the Platform Overview and the Quickstart guide. If you have not used the platform before, start with the Quickstart — it takes about five minutes and introduces the basic interface.
You do not need any bioinformatics software installed on your computer. Everything happens in the browser. You may want to have a spreadsheet application (Excel, Google Sheets, or similar) available for reviewing exported CSV files.

title: Tutorials
text:
Available tutorials
The tutorials below are planned for this section. Each one addresses a practical question that experimental biochemists commonly face when using computational tools to guide their research.

Finding a purchasable strain for a target compound
Research question: You are interested in a specific class of natural product (for example, lanthipeptide antibiotics) and want to find a microorganism that (a) carries promising BGCs for that compound class and (b) is available as a type strain from a public culture collection so you can order it and begin experiments immediately.
What you will learn:

How to use the BGC class filter in Explore mode to narrow the catalogue to a specific compound type.
How to apply the Type Strain filter to restrict results to purchasable organisms.
How to sort and compare assemblies by novelty, diversity, and density.
How to read the Assembly Detail panel to confirm culture collection availability.
How to export your shortlist as a CSV for sharing with collaborators or for ordering strains.

Estimated time: 15–20 minutes.


Evaluating your own assembly
Research question: You have sequenced a promising microorganism and assembled its genome. Before investing weeks of effort in extraction, purification, and structural elucidation, you want to know: how does this organism’s biosynthetic potential compare with everything in the public catalogue? Are its BGCs novel, or do similar gene clusters already exist in well-studied organisms?
What you will learn:

How to prepare your assembly files for upload (FASTA format, optional antiSMASH annotations).
How to use Evaluate mode to upload and analyse your assembly.
How to read the Assembly Assessment Report — priority ranking, radar chart, percentile distributions, and redundancy matrix.
How to drill into individual BGCs from the scoped BGC Triad.
How to interpret the BGC Embeddings Map to understand where your organism sits in the global chemical landscape.
How to use cross-mode transitions to compare your assembly with similar database entries.

Estimated time: 20–30 minutes (depending on assembly size and processing time).


Exploring RiPPs in marine metagenomes
Research question: You are interested in ribosomally synthesised and post-translationally modified peptides (RiPPs) from marine environments. This compound class has attracted increasing attention because RiPPs are genetically encoded (making them easier to engineer) and marine environments are a rich source of novel chemistry. You want to find the most promising marine metagenome assemblies carrying novel RiPP gene clusters.
What you will learn:

How to combine the BGC class filter (RiPP) with the biome filter (marine environments) in Explore mode.
How to use the Assembly Space Map to identify clusters of related marine assemblies.
How to sort assemblies by novelty to find those carrying the most unusual RiPP gene clusters.
How to use the BGC Triad to inspect individual RiPP clusters, including their subclass (lanthipeptide, thiopeptide, sactipeptide, etc.).
How to evaluate top candidates and interpret the Novelty Decomposition to understand what makes each RiPP novel.
How to search for similar RiPP architectures using the Domain Query in Search mode.

Estimated time: 25–35 minutes.

title: Tutorials
text:
How tutorials are structured
Each tutorial follows a consistent format:

The question — what you want to find out, stated in plain language.
Prerequisites — any data or preparation needed before you begin.
Walkthrough — numbered steps with descriptions of what you will see at each stage. Screenshots and annotations highlight key interface elements.
Interpreting the results — what the outputs mean and how to decide on next steps.
Going further — suggestions for extending the workflow, such as evaluating additional candidates or combining with other search strategies.

title: Tutorials
text:
Requesting new tutorials
If you have a workflow that you would like documented as a tutorial, please open an issue on the GitHub repository or contact the team using the details on the About page. We prioritise tutorials based on community demand.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
text: To produce a comprehensive assessment report for a single assembly that exists in the BGC Discovery Platform database. The report tells you how this assembly’s biosynthetic potential compares with every other assembly in the catalogue, helping you decide whether it deserves wet-lab follow-up.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
text: To produce a comprehensive assessment report for a single assembly that exists in the BGC Discovery Platform database. The report tells you how this assembly’s biosynthetic potential compares with every other assembly in the catalogue, helping you decide whether it deserves wet-lab follow-up.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
text:
When to use this workflow
Use this workflow when:

You are browsing assemblies in Explore mode and one catches your eye — you want more detail than the summary scores provide.
A collaborator shared an accession number and you want to understand its biosynthetic profile before ordering a strain.
You have a shortlist of two or three assemblies and need to make a final selection based on quantitative evidence.

If you want to evaluate your own unpublished assembly, see Upload an Assembly instead.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
section: Entry point 1: right-click from the Assembly Roster
text:
Entry point 1: right-click from the Assembly Roster
This is the fastest route when you are already browsing in Explore or Search mode.

Locate the assembly in the Assembly Roster table. You can sort by any column or use the search box at the top of the roster.
Right-click the row. A context menu appears with several options.
Select “Evaluate Assembly”. The dashboard switches to Evaluate mode and immediately begins generating the assessment report.
Wait for the report. A progress spinner appears briefly while the platform retrieves pre-computed data and calculates percentile ranks. This usually takes two to five seconds.
Read the report. The main area now displays the Assembly Assessment Report.

The right-click menu also offers other actions (pin to shortlist, view in Assembly Detail). You can always return to Explore mode using the mode selector — your filters and shortlist are preserved.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
section: Entry point 2: the Evaluate tab with an accession
text:
Entry point 2: the Evaluate tab with an accession
Use this route when you already know the accession.

Switch to Evaluate mode by clicking the Evaluate tab in the mode selector at the top of the dashboard.
Select the Assembly tab in the sidebar input area. The sidebar presents two sub-tabs: Assembly and BGC. Make sure Assembly is selected.
Type or paste the accession. Enter the assembly accession in the text field (for example, GCA_003150795.1 or ERZ1234567). The platform accepts INSDC accessions and internal identifiers.
Press Enter or click Evaluate. The platform validates the accession against the database. If the accession is not found, an error message appears with suggestions for similar accessions.
Wait for the report.
Read the report.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
text:
What happens behind the scenes
When you trigger evaluation of a database assembly, the platform:

Retrieves pre-computed BGC annotations for every BGC belonging to that assembly, including domain architectures, predicted products, BGC class, and ChemOnt (Chemical Ontology) annotations.
Retrieves pre-computed scores — novelty, diversity, and density — stored during the last database ingestion cycle.
Computes percentile ranks by comparing each score against the full distribution of all assemblies in the database. This step runs on demand because the database grows over time and percentiles need to reflect the current catalogue.
Builds the assessment report by combining raw scores, percentiles, contextual analyses (redundancy matrix, embedding maps), and the scoped BGC Triad.

Because most of the heavy computation was done during ingestion, database evaluations are fast — typically under five seconds.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
text:
What you will see
The main area fills with the Assembly Assessment Report. A section-navigation bar on the right edge lists every report section so you can jump directly to any part:

Priority Ranking — the assembly’s absolute rank among all assemblies in the database, based on a weighted combination of novelty, diversity, and density.
Priority Score Radar — a spider chart comparing this assembly’s novelty, diversity, and density against the database mean and the 90th percentile.
Score Percentile Distributions — gauge charts showing where each score falls in the overall distribution.
BGC Triad — the familiar three-panel BGC view (roster, space map, detail), scoped to show only the BGCs belonging to this assembly.
Redundancy Matrix — a breakdown of this assembly’s BGCs into three categories: those belonging to novel Gene Cluster Families (GCFs), those in known GCFs without a type strain representative, and those in known GCFs where a type strain is already available.
BGC Embeddings Map — a UMAP projection showing where this assembly’s BGCs sit in the global embedding space, with MIBiG reference landmarks highlighted.

For a detailed guide to each section, see Reading the Assembly Assessment Report.

title: Evaluate a Database Assembly
text:
Tips

Check the Redundancy Matrix first. If most of this assembly’s BGCs fall into the “Known GCF, type strain available” column, the assembly offers limited novelty — someone else has already characterised similar gene clusters in a culturable organism.
Use the BGC Triad to drill into specific clusters. Click any BGC in the scoped roster to see its domain architecture and predicted products. If one BGC looks particularly novel, right-click it and select “Evaluate BGC” to get the BGC Assessment Report.
Compare with the Assembly Space Map. Before evaluating, note where the assembly sits on the Assembly Space Map in Explore mode. Assemblies in sparse regions of the map tend to produce higher novelty scores.
Switch assemblies without leaving Evaluate mode. After reading one report, type a new accession in the sidebar and press Enter to generate a fresh report. The previous report is replaced.
