title: Biome Ontology
text: To explain how the BGC Discovery Platform uses biome annotations to describe the environmental origin of each assembly, and how to use biome filters to focus your exploration on organisms from specific habitats.

title: Biome Ontology
text: To explain how the BGC Discovery Platform uses biome annotations to describe the environmental origin of each assembly, and how to use biome filters to focus your exploration on organisms from specific habitats.

title: Biome Ontology
text:
When to use this reference
Consult this page when:

You see a biome lineage path (such as root:Environmental:Terrestrial:Soil) in the platform and want to understand its structure.
You want to filter assemblies by the environment they were sampled from — for example, marine sediments, human gut, or hot springs.
You are designing a bioprospecting campaign focused on a particular ecological niche and want to know how the platform represents that niche.

title: Biome Ontology
text:
What biome annotations represent
Every metagenomic assembly in the database carries a biome annotation describing the environment from which the original sample was collected. These annotations come from the GOLD Ecosystem Classification, a standardised vocabulary maintained by the Joint Genome Institute (JGI). GOLD stands for Genomes OnLine Database.
Biome annotations are available only for metagenome-derived assemblies (metagenome-assembled genomes, or MAGs, and metagenomic contigs). Isolate genomes — assemblies derived from pure cultures of a single organism — do not carry biome annotations because they are not tied to a specific environmental sample.

title: Biome Ontology
text:
Lineage path format
Biome annotations are stored as colon-delimited hierarchical paths that read from broad to specific:
root : top-level domain : habitat : sub-habitat : specific environment
Each level narrows the ecological context. Here are several examples:







Lineage path
Meaning




root:Environmental:Terrestrial:Soil
An environmental sample from terrestrial soil.


root:Environmental:Aquatic:Marine:Sediment
An environmental sample from marine sediment.


root:Environmental:Aquatic:Freshwater:Lake
A sample from a freshwater lake.


root:Host-associated:Human:Digestive system
A sample from the human digestive tract.


root:Host-associated:Human:Skin
A sample from human skin.


root:Host-associated:Plants:Rhizosphere
A sample from the root zone of a plant.


root:Engineered:Wastewater
A sample from an engineered wastewater treatment system.




Top-level domains
The GOLD classification uses three top-level domains:







Domain
Description




Environmental
Samples taken directly from natural environments (soil, water, air, sediment).


Host-associated
Samples taken from or within a living host organism (human, animal, plant, insect).


Engineered
Samples from human-made environments (bioreactors, wastewater treatment plants, fermentation systems).

title: Biome Ontology
text:
How to filter by biome
The platform provides a biome filter in the sidebar filter panel, available in both Explore and Search modes.

Using the biome filter

Open the Filters panel in the sidebar.
Locate the Biome filter. It appears as a searchable, hierarchical dropdown.
Browse or search. You can expand the hierarchy by clicking the arrow icons next to each level, or type a keyword (for example, “marine” or “gut”) to search across all levels.
Select one or more biome nodes. Selecting a parent node includes all its children. For example, selecting root:Environmental:Aquatic:Marine will include assemblies from marine water, marine sediment, hydrothermal vents, and all other marine sub-environments.
Apply the filter. The Assembly Roster and Assembly Space Map immediately update to show only assemblies matching the selected biomes.



Combining biome with other filters
Biome filters work alongside all other filters in the platform:

Biome + BGC class: Find polyketide-producing organisms specifically from marine environments.
Biome + Novelty threshold: Find the most novel assemblies from a particular habitat.
Biome + Type Strain: This combination will typically return few or no results, because type strains are isolate genomes and do not carry biome annotations. If you want culturable organisms from a specific environment, filter by biome first, then look for assemblies with taxonomic relatives that have type strains.

title: Biome Ontology
text:
Why biome matters for bioprospecting
Different environments impose different selective pressures on microbial communities. Organisms from competitive, nutrient-limited environments (such as soil or coral reefs) tend to invest more heavily in secondary metabolism — they produce more natural products because they need them to compete, defend territory, or acquire scarce resources.
Some general patterns observed in the database:

Soil environments tend to harbour assemblies with high BGC density and diversity, particularly in genera like Streptomyces and Bacillus.
Marine sediment environments are a rich source of novel chemistry. Marine organisms face unique chemical challenges (high salinity, high pressure) that drive the evolution of unusual natural products.
Host-associated environments (especially the human gut) contain assemblies with moderate BGC density but often high novelty, because gut microbiota produce specialised molecules for interacting with the host and with competing microbes.
Engineered environments tend to have lower BGC diversity, as the microbial communities are often selected for metabolic efficiency rather than secondary metabolism.

These are trends, not rules. Novel chemistry can come from any environment.

title: Biome Ontology
text:
Tips

Start broad, then narrow. Select a top-level domain first (for example, Environmental:Aquatic) and examine the Assembly Space Map to see the landscape. Then narrow to a specific sub-environment if a region of the map looks interesting.
Use biome to contextualise novelty. An assembly with moderate novelty from a well-studied environment (like soil) may be less interesting than an assembly with the same novelty score from an under-explored environment (like deep-sea hydrothermal vents).
Check biome coverage in the database. Some environments are more densely sampled than others. The platform’s database reflects the biases of public sequencing projects — soil and human gut are heavily represented, while extreme environments may have fewer assemblies.
Combine biome with taxonomic filters to find specific organisms in specific environments. For example, filter by root:Environmental:Aquatic:Marine and the genus Salinispora to find marine actinobacteria known for producing novel natural products.

text:

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
Evaluate mode produces a detailed assessment report for one assembly or one biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC). The report answers a practical question: is this candidate worth investing bench time and reagents in? It does so by computing percentile ranks, novelty breakdowns, redundancy analyses, and chemical-space context that go well beyond the summary scores visible in Explore or Search mode.
You can evaluate assets that already exist in the database or upload your own files for ephemeral analysis. Uploaded files are never added to the database — the platform processes them in memory, generates the report, and discards the data when you close the tab.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
Evaluate mode produces a detailed assessment report for one assembly or one biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC). The report answers a practical question: is this candidate worth investing bench time and reagents in? It does so by computing percentile ranks, novelty breakdowns, redundancy analyses, and chemical-space context that go well beyond the summary scores visible in Explore or Search mode.
You can evaluate assets that already exist in the database or upload your own files for ephemeral analysis. Uploaded files are never added to the database — the platform processes them in memory, generates the report, and discards the data when you close the tab.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
When to use Evaluate
Use Evaluate mode when you can answer “yes” to any of these questions:

Have you narrowed your candidates to a handful of assemblies or BGCs and need to make a final selection?
Do you want to understand why a particular BGC scores high or low on novelty, and which aspects of its biology drive that score?
Have you sequenced or assembled your own organism and want to see how its BGCs compare with everything in the database before committing to wet-lab work?
Did a collaborator send you a GenBank file and you want a quick assessment without running a full bioinformatics pipeline?

If you are still browsing broadly, start with Explore mode. If you are searching for specific enzyme architectures or chemical scaffolds, use Search mode first and come back here once you have candidates.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
Three entry points
There are three ways to enter Evaluate mode, each suited to a different moment in your workflow.

1. Right-click from any roster
While browsing assemblies in Explore mode or reviewing BGC hits in Search mode, right-click any row in the roster table. The context menu offers Evaluate Assembly or Evaluate BGC. Selecting either option switches the dashboard to Evaluate mode and immediately begins generating the assessment report for that item.
This is the fastest path when you spot an interesting candidate while exploring or searching.


2. Switch to Evaluate mode and enter an accession
Click the Evaluate tab in the mode selector at the top of the dashboard. The sidebar presents a text field where you can type or paste a database accession (for example, GCA_003150795.1 for an assembly or BGC0000001 for a BGC). Press Enter or click Evaluate to generate the report.
Use this path when you already know the accession — perhaps from a publication, a collaborator’s email, or your own notes.


3. Upload a file
In Evaluate mode the sidebar also contains a drag-and-drop upload area. You can upload a .tar.gz archive containing an assembly or a single GenBank file containing a BGC. The platform processes the upload ephemerally: it annotates the file, computes scores, and generates the same assessment report you would see for a database entry, but it never stores your data.
See Upload an Assembly and Upload a BGC for format requirements and step-by-step instructions.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
What happens when you evaluate
Regardless of which entry point you use, the platform performs the same analysis pipeline:

Retrieves or computes metadata. For database entries it pulls pre-computed scores. For uploads it runs annotation on the fly.
Computes percentile ranks. Every score is compared against the full database distribution to produce a percentile rank, telling you where this asset stands relative to all others.
Generates the assessment report. The report layout depends on whether you are evaluating an assembly or a BGC:

Assembly Assessment Report — priority ranking, radar chart, score distributions, BGC Triad, redundancy matrix, and embedding map. See Reading the Assembly Report.
BGC Assessment Report — GCF placement, novelty decomposition, domain architecture analysis, comparison with the nearest validated BGC, and embedding maps. See Reading the BGC Report.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
Step-by-step: evaluating a database assembly

Switch to Evaluate mode using the tab selector at the top of the dashboard.
Enter the assembly accession in the text field in the sidebar (for example, GCA_003150795.1).
Press Enter or click Evaluate.
Wait for the report. A progress indicator appears while the platform computes percentile ranks and contextual analyses. This typically takes a few seconds for database entries.
Read the report. The main area now displays the Assembly Assessment Report. Scroll through the sections or use the section navigation on the right.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
Step-by-step: evaluating an uploaded file

Switch to Evaluate mode.
Drag your file onto the upload area in the sidebar, or click the area to open a file picker.
Wait for processing. Uploaded files require annotation, so processing takes longer than database entries — typically 30 seconds to a few minutes depending on assembly size.
Read the report. The same assessment report appears, with an Uploaded badge in the header to indicate that this asset is ephemeral.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
What you will see
The report fills the entire main area of the dashboard. A navigation bar along the right edge lists the report sections so you can jump directly to any part. The sidebar remains visible with the accession field and upload area, allowing you to evaluate another asset without leaving the mode.
Key visual elements common to both report types include:

Percentile gauges — semicircular gauges showing where each score falls in the database distribution.
Embedding maps — UMAP projections that place the evaluated asset in the context of the full catalogue and MIBiG reference landmarks.
Score tables — structured tables listing every metric with its raw value, percentile, and a brief interpretation.

title: Evaluate Asset
text:
Tips

Evaluate before and after filtering. Percentile ranks are computed against the entire database. If you filtered heavily in Explore mode before right-clicking “Evaluate”, the report will still show the global percentile, which may differ from the asset’s rank within your filtered subset.
Compare two candidates side by side. Open a second browser tab, navigate to Evaluate mode, and enter a different accession. Place the tabs side by side to compare reports visually.
Use the cross-mode links. Every assessment report includes links to pivot into Explore or Search mode scoped to the evaluated asset’s neighbourhood. See Cross-Mode Transitions from Evaluate.
Ephemeral uploads are private. Uploaded files are processed in your browser session and are never persisted to the server or shared with other users.

title: Domain Query Builder
text: The Domain Query Builder lets you search for biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) based on the protein domains encoded in their genes. Every protein in the platform has been annotated against the Pfam database — a curated collection of protein family models. By specifying which Pfam domains must be present (or absent), you describe the enzymatic machinery of the BGC you are looking for, and the platform returns all BGCs whose domain composition matches.

title: Domain Query Builder
text: The Domain Query Builder lets you search for biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) based on the protein domains encoded in their genes. Every protein in the platform has been annotated against the Pfam database — a curated collection of protein family models. By specifying which Pfam domains must be present (or absent), you describe the enzymatic machinery of the BGC you are looking for, and the platform returns all BGCs whose domain composition matches.

title: Domain Query Builder
text:
When to use

You know the enzyme families involved in your pathway of interest (e.g. ketosynthase, acyltransferase, thioesterase for a type I polyketide synthase).
You want to find BGCs that encode an unusual combination of tailoring enzymes — for example, a halogenase alongside a glycosyltransferase.
You want a fast, broad search that catches distant relatives. Domain-based searches are tolerant of sequence divergence because they operate on domain annotations rather than raw sequence.

title: Domain Query Builder
text:
Key concepts

Pfam domain

A Pfam domain (e.g. PF00109, “Beta-ketoacyl synthase”) is a conserved protein region with a defined function. A single protein can contain multiple Pfam domains. The platform stores the complete set of Pfam domains found across all genes in each BGC.

Domain set

The collection of distinct Pfam domains present in a BGC. For scoring purposes, duplicates are collapsed — if a BGC encodes three copies of PF00109, the domain set still contains PF00109 once.

Sorensen-Dice coefficient

The similarity metric used to rank results. It measures the overlap between the domain set you requested and the domain set of each BGC in the database. The formula is:


\[
\text{Dice} = \frac{2 \times |A \cap B|}{|A| + |B|}
\]
where A is your query domain set and B is the BGC’s domain set. A score of 1.0 means perfect overlap; 0.0 means no shared domains. See Understanding Similarity Scores for a deeper explanation.

title: Domain Query Builder
text:
Step-by-step: building a domain query

1. Open the Query tab
In Search mode, click the Query tab in the sidebar. The Domain Query section is the first input area.


2. Add domains
Type a Pfam accession (e.g. PF00109) or a domain name (e.g. “Beta-ketoacyl synthase”) into the search box. The platform auto-completes as you type. Click a suggestion to add it to your query.
Repeat for each domain you want to include. Added domains appear as chips (small labelled tags) below the search box.


3. Choose the logic: AND or OR
The logic toggle controls how the platform combines your requested domains:








Mode
Meaning
When to use




AND (default)
The BGC must contain all listed domains.
You know the exact domain architecture you want.


OR
The BGC must contain at least one of the listed domains.
You are exploring and want to cast a wide net.





4. Mark domains as required or excluded
Each domain chip has a toggle that lets you mark it as:

Required (green, default) — the domain must be present.
Excluded (red) — the domain must not be present. Excluded domains act as negative filters: any BGC containing an excluded domain is removed from results regardless of its score.

Excluding domains is useful when you want to eliminate common but uninteresting hits. For example, if you search for PF00109 (ketosynthase) but exclude PF00698 (acyl transferase), you will skip standard modular PKS clusters and surface unusual architectures.


5. Run the query
Click Run Query at the bottom of the sidebar. Results appear in the BGC Roster, ranked by Sorensen-Dice similarity score.

title: Domain Query Builder
text:
What you will see
The BGC Roster displays one row per matching BGC. In Search mode, the Similarity column shows the Sorensen-Dice score for each hit. Additional columns include:







Column
Description




Accession
The unique identifier for the BGC.


Classification
The predicted BGC class (e.g. “Type I PKS”, “NRPS”, “Terpene”).


Novelty
A score (0–1) indicating how different this BGC is from any characterised BGC in the MIBiG reference database. Higher means more novel.


Domain Novelty
A score (0–1) reflecting how unusual the BGC’s domain composition is relative to all other BGCs of the same class.


Size (bp)
The length of the BGC region in base pairs.



Click any row to open the BGC Detail panel, where you can inspect the full domain architecture gene by gene and see which of your query domains matched.

title: Domain Query Builder
section: Worked example: finding type I PKS clusters with a methyltransferase
text:
Worked example: finding type I PKS clusters with a methyltransferase
Suppose you are studying polyketide natural products and want to find type I PKS clusters that include a methyltransferase domain, suggesting the product undergoes methylation.

Add PF00109 (Beta-ketoacyl synthase) — the core catalytic domain of PKS.
Add PF08242 (Methyltransferase domain) — the tailoring domain you are interested in.
Set logic to AND — the BGC must contain both.
Click Run Query.

The results will show PKS clusters ranked by how well their full domain set overlaps with your two-domain query. Clusters that contain many other domains alongside PF00109 and PF08242 will score slightly lower than a hypothetical minimal cluster containing only those two, because the Sorensen-Dice coefficient penalises unmatched domains on either side. In practice, the top hits will be the BGCs whose domain sets are most enriched for your query domains relative to their total domain count.

title: Domain Query Builder
section: Worked example: excluding common domains to surface novelty
text:
Worked example: excluding common domains to surface novelty
You want to find BGCs that encode a halogenase (PF04820) but are not standard NRPS clusters.

Add PF04820 (Tryptophan halogenase) as required.
Add PF00501 (AMP-binding domain, the hallmark of NRPS adenylation) as excluded.
Set logic to AND.
Click Run Query.

The results now exclude any BGC containing an AMP-binding domain, filtering out typical NRPS clusters and surfacing unusual BGC architectures that employ halogenation.

title: Domain Query Builder
text:
Tips

Use Pfam accessions for precision. Domain names can be ambiguous (“methyltransferase” matches dozens of families), but accessions like PF08242 are unambiguous.
Start with one or two domains. Adding too many required domains at once can return zero results if no BGC in the database contains all of them. Start small and add domains iteratively.
Combine with filters. If you only want results from marine biomes or from a specific BGC class, set those filters in the Filters tab before running your domain query.
Combine with other query types. You can run a domain query alongside a Sequence Search or Chemical Structure Search. The platform intersects the results, returning only BGCs that satisfy all active queries.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text: To explain what Gene Cluster Families (GCFs) are, how the platform builds them, and why they are one of the most important concepts for prioritising biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) in a bioprospecting workflow.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text: To explain what Gene Cluster Families (GCFs) are, how the platform builds them, and why they are one of the most important concepts for prioritising biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) in a bioprospecting workflow.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text:
When to use this reference
Consult this page when:

You see a GCF identifier or member count in the BGC Roster or BGC Assessment Report and want to understand what it means.
You want to know how the platform decides that two BGCs are “related” and groups them into the same family.
You are trying to interpret the Redundancy Matrix in the Assembly Assessment Report.
You encounter the term “Novel Singleton” and want to understand its significance.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
section: What is a Gene Cluster Family?
text:
What is a Gene Cluster Family?
A Gene Cluster Family (GCF) is a group of BGCs from across the database that share similar biochemical characteristics — similar domain architectures, similar protein sequences, or both. Think of a GCF as a “natural product family”: all members likely produce related (though not necessarily identical) chemical compounds.
GCFs are analogous to protein families in sequence analysis. Just as proteins with similar sequences are grouped into families (like Pfam families), BGCs with similar biosynthetic machinery are grouped into Gene Cluster Families.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text:
How clustering works
The platform builds GCFs using a two-step process:

Step 1: Compute embeddings
Each BGC is represented as a 1152-dimensional embedding vector — a numerical fingerprint that captures the BGC’s overall biochemical character. This vector is computed by passing the BGC’s protein sequences through a pre-trained protein language model (ESM, Evolutionary Scale Modeling). The embedding captures both the sequence content and the functional properties of the encoded enzymes.


Step 2: Cluster by similarity
The platform clusters BGCs by measuring the cosine similarity between their embedding vectors. BGCs whose embeddings are sufficiently similar (above a defined threshold) are grouped into the same family. The clustering algorithm identifies a representative member (the medoid — the BGC closest to the centre of the cluster) for each family.
This approach has a key advantage over traditional sequence-based clustering: it can group BGCs that share functional similarity even when their raw DNA or protein sequences have diverged beyond the detection limit of BLAST or other alignment-based tools. Two BGCs with different gene orders, different numbers of genes, or divergent sequences can still end up in the same GCF if their overall biochemical logic is similar.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text:
Key GCF properties

Member count
The number of BGCs in the database that belong to this family. Member counts range from 1 (a Novel Singleton) to several thousand (for ubiquitous biosynthetic strategies like siderophore production in Enterobacteriaceae).
A large member count means the biosynthetic strategy is widespread across many organisms in the database.


Validated members
The number of family members that have been experimentally characterised — meaning they appear in the MIBiG database with a known product structure and literature reference. Validated members serve as reference points: if a GCF has validated members, you know (approximately) what kind of molecule the family produces.


Mean novelty
The average Novelty Score across all members of the family. A GCF with high mean novelty contains BGCs that are, on average, distant from characterised reference BGCs.


Distance to representative
For any given BGC, its distance to the family representative indicates how typical it is within its family. A BGC very close to the representative is a canonical family member. A BGC far from the representative may be an outlier that produces a variant product.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text:
Why GCFs matter for discovery
GCFs are the concept that connects individual BGC scores to actionable research decisions. Here is why:

Pattern 1: Large family, zero validated members
This is the highest-value pattern for natural product discovery. A GCF with many members but no experimentally validated BGCs represents a widespread, completely uncharacterised biosynthetic strategy. Multiple organisms across diverse environments have evolved and maintained this gene cluster family, suggesting it produces something biologically important — but no one has yet isolated and characterised the product.
If you find a BGC in such a family, you are not just discovering a single new compound; you are opening up an entire family of related natural products.


Pattern 2: Novel Singleton
A Novel Singleton is a GCF with exactly one member — the BGC you are looking at. No other BGC in the database is similar enough to be grouped with it. This is the strongest possible novelty signal: not only is the BGC distant from all characterised references, it is also unlike everything else in the uncharacterised catalogue.
Novel Singletons carry both the highest potential reward and the highest risk. The reward: you may have found a truly unique biosynthetic pathway. The risk: with no relatives to guide you, experimental characterisation will be harder — you cannot borrow knowledge from related family members.


Pattern 3: Large family, some validated members
A GCF with validated members tells you approximately what kind of chemistry the family produces. Unvalidated members of the same family may produce variants of the known product — perhaps with different stereochemistry, additional functional groups, or modified sugar moieties. These variants can have dramatically different bioactivity.
If you are interested in a specific compound class (say, macrolide antibiotics) and you find a GCF whose validated members produce macrolides, the unvalidated members become targeted candidates for finding new macrolide variants.


Pattern 4: Small family, validated members, type strain available
This pattern has the lowest discovery potential. The chemistry is known, few organisms carry it, and a culturable reference strain already exists. Unless you have a specific reason to study this family (for example, you are interested in the regulation of the pathway rather than the product itself), other candidates offer better return on investment.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text:
GCFs in the platform interface
GCF information appears in several places:

BGC Roster — the GCF column shows the family identifier and member count for each BGC.
BGC Detail panel — the GCF section shows the family identifier, member count, validated count, and distance to representative.
BGC Assessment Report — the GCF Placement section provides the most detailed view, including Novel Singleton status and mean family novelty.
Assembly Assessment Report — the Redundancy Matrix uses GCF membership to categorise each assembly’s BGCs.

title: Gene Cluster Families (GCF)
text:
Tips

Sort the BGC Roster by GCF member count to find BGCs in the largest families. Then filter for families with zero validated members to find widespread uncharacterised strategies.
Use “Browse Family” in the BGC Assessment Report to switch to Explore mode and see all family members. This lets you check whether family members come from diverse environments (a sign that the pathway is broadly important) or from a single niche.
Do not dismiss small families. A GCF with 5 members and zero validated members may represent a rare but genuine biosynthetic strategy found only in a specific ecological niche.
Combine GCF information with the Redundancy Matrix when evaluating assemblies. An assembly whose BGCs all belong to large, validated GCFs offers less discovery potential than one with even a single BGC in a novel or unvalidated family.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text: To understand every section of the BGC Assessment Report so you can determine whether a specific biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) warrants laboratory follow-up. This page explains what each panel shows, how the numbers are calculated, and what patterns to look for when making decisions.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text: To understand every section of the BGC Assessment Report so you can determine whether a specific biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) warrants laboratory follow-up. This page explains what each panel shows, how the numbers are calculated, and what patterns to look for when making decisions.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
section: When you will see this report
text:
When you will see this report
The BGC Assessment Report appears whenever you evaluate a single BGC, whether from the database or uploaded. You reach it by:

Right-clicking a BGC in any roster and selecting “Evaluate BGC”
Entering a BGC accession in the Evaluate sidebar
Uploading a GenBank file containing a single BGC

See Evaluate Overview for full entry-point details.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text:
Section 1: GCF Placement
The top section identifies which Gene Cluster Family (GCF) this BGC belongs to. A GCF is a group of BGCs across the database that share similar domain architecture and sequence content — think of it as a “family” of gene clusters that likely produce related natural products.
The GCF Placement panel displays:







Field
What it means




Family ID
The internal identifier for this GCF.


Member count
How many BGCs in the database belong to this family. A large family indicates a widespread biosynthetic strategy.


Validated count
How many family members are experimentally validated (present in MIBiG with a known product). Zero validated members means the entire family’s chemistry is uncharacterised.


Mean novelty
The average novelty score across all family members.


Distance to representative
How far this specific BGC sits from the GCF’s representative member (the medoid of the family). A small distance means this BGC is typical of its family; a large distance means it is an outlier within the family.


Novel Singleton
A yes/no indicator. If “yes”, this BGC is the sole member of its family — no other BGC in the database resembles it closely enough to be grouped together. This is the strongest possible novelty signal.



How to read it:

Large family, zero validated members = a widespread, completely uncharacterised biosynthetic strategy. This is high-value territory for discovery.
Novel Singleton = no relatives at all. Maximum novelty, but also maximum uncertainty — you have no close reference to guide experimental work.
Small distance to representative = this BGC is a typical member of its family. The family’s average properties (novelty, class, products) describe this BGC well.
Large distance to representative = this BGC is an outlier in its family. It may carry unique modifications or domain rearrangements not seen in other members.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text:
Section 2: Novelty Decomposition
Below GCF Placement, three horizontal bar charts break the overall novelty score into three independent axes:

Sequence Novelty (0–1)
Measures how different this BGC’s protein sequences are from the nearest validated BGC in MIBiG. A score of 1.0 means the proteins share essentially no sequence similarity with any characterised BGC. A score near 0 means close homologues exist in the validated reference set.
This axis captures enzyme-level divergence — whether the catalytic machinery is new or well-known.


Chemistry Novelty (0–1)
Measures how different this BGC’s predicted chemical product is from known compounds. The platform compares ChemOnt (Chemical Ontology) predictions and predicted molecular fingerprints against the MIBiG compound catalogue. A high score means the predicted product does not resemble any known natural product.
This axis captures product-level divergence — whether the molecule itself is likely to be new.


Architecture Novelty (0–1)
Measures how different the arrangement of protein domains in this BGC is from known BGCs. Even if individual enzymes are well-characterised, a novel arrangement of domains can produce a novel product through combinatorial biochemistry.
This axis captures pathway-level divergence — whether the biosynthetic logic (the order and combination of enzymatic steps) is new.
How to read the three axes together:

High on all three = a genuinely novel BGC in every respect. Rare and exciting, but also the hardest to characterise because you have no reference.
High Sequence, low Architecture = new enzymes arranged in a familiar pattern. The product may resemble known compounds but is made by divergent machinery.
Low Sequence, high Architecture = well-known enzymes arranged in an unusual way. The biosynthetic logic is new, potentially producing an unprecedented product from familiar parts.
High Chemistry, low Sequence = the predicted product is unlike known compounds, but the enzymes have recognisable homologues. This can happen when small changes in substrate specificity or tailoring reactions dramatically alter the final product.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text:
Section 3: Domain Architecture Differential
This section lists every Pfam protein domain detected in the BGC and categorises each one relative to the assigned GCF:








Category
Symbol
Meaning




Core
Solid circle
Present in more than 80% of family members. These domains define the conserved enzymatic backbone of the family.


Variable
Half circle
Present in 20–80% of family members. These domains represent optional pathway branches or tailoring steps.


Rare
Open circle
Present in fewer than 20% of family members, or unique to this BGC. These domains are the most distinctive features of this particular cluster.



The domains are listed in their order along the BGC (5’ to 3’), so you can read the table as a left-to-right map of the biosynthetic pathway.
How to read it:

Rare domains are the most interesting from a discovery standpoint. They suggest enzymatic steps that most family members lack, which may produce structural modifications not seen in the family’s typical products.
A BGC with many Variable and Rare domains relative to Core domains is an atypical family member and may produce a distinct product variant.
A BGC composed almost entirely of Core domains is a canonical representative of its family and is likely to produce a product similar to other family members.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text:
Section 4: Domain Architecture Comparison
This section displays a side-by-side visual alignment of the evaluated BGC’s domain architecture with that of the nearest MIBiG-validated BGC — the experimentally characterised BGC that is most similar in sequence space.
The two architectures are drawn as horizontal arrow diagrams, with each arrow representing a gene and coloured blocks within each arrow representing Pfam domains. Shared domains are connected by lines between the two diagrams. Domains present in the evaluated BGC but absent in the MIBiG reference are highlighted in a distinct colour, and vice versa.
How to read it:

Aligned blocks with connecting lines = conserved biosynthetic steps shared with the validated reference. The chemistry performed by these steps is likely similar.
Highlighted blocks without connections = unique domains. In the evaluated BGC, these represent biosynthetic steps not present in the reference, suggesting novel modifications. In the MIBiG reference, these represent steps that the evaluated BGC lacks, suggesting a simplified or alternative pathway.
The MIBiG accession and compound name are shown above the reference architecture. You can look up this entry on the MIBiG website to see the known product structure, bioactivity data, and literature references.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text:
Section 5: BGC Space Map
A UMAP projection (a two-dimensional map generated by reducing high-dimensional data) centred on the evaluated BGC. Nearby BGCs from the database are shown as small dots coloured by BGC class (Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, and so on). MIBiG-validated BGCs appear as diamond-shaped landmarks with compound-name labels.
How to read it:

The evaluated BGC appears as a large, labelled point at or near the centre of the view.
Nearby dots represent BGCs with similar overall biochemical profiles. If the nearest neighbours are mostly from a single class, the evaluated BGC likely belongs to that chemical space.
Distance from MIBiG diamonds indicates how far the evaluated BGC is from known chemistry. BGCs in “empty” regions of the map — far from any landmark — are the most novel.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text:
Section 6: Embeddings Map
A second UMAP projection, this time based on the protein embedding vectors rather than the domain-level features used in the BGC Space Map. This provides a complementary view: while the BGC Space Map reflects domain architecture and predicted products, the Embeddings Map reflects raw protein sequence similarity.
How to read it:
The interpretation is similar to the BGC Space Map, but discrepancies between the two maps are informative:

Same neighbourhood in both maps = the BGC is consistently similar to the same set of neighbours regardless of whether you compare domains or full sequences. Strong signal.
Different neighbourhood = the BGC shares domain architecture with one group but has divergent protein sequences, or vice versa. This can indicate convergent evolution (unrelated enzymes producing similar domain architectures) or recent horizontal gene transfer.

title: Reading the BGC Assessment Report
text:
Tips

Start with GCF Placement and Novel Singleton status to gauge the overall discovery potential.
Use the Novelty Decomposition to plan your experimental approach. High Architecture Novelty suggests you should focus on understanding the pathway logic. High Chemistry Novelty suggests the product itself is the main prize. High Sequence Novelty suggests the enzymes deserve biochemical characterisation in their own right.
The Domain Architecture Comparison is your bridge to the literature. The nearest MIBiG BGC gives you a starting point for understanding what this BGC might produce and how to detect its product.
Cross-check the two maps (BGC Space Map and Embeddings Map). Consistent placement across both views increases confidence in the novelty assessment.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text: To understand every section of the Assembly Assessment Report so you can make informed decisions about which assemblies to pursue in the laboratory. This page walks through each panel, explains what the numbers mean, and suggests how to interpret them in the context of a bioprospecting workflow.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text: To understand every section of the Assembly Assessment Report so you can make informed decisions about which assemblies to pursue in the laboratory. This page walks through each panel, explains what the numbers mean, and suggests how to interpret them in the context of a bioprospecting workflow.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
section: When you will see this report
text:
When you will see this report
The Assembly Assessment Report appears whenever you evaluate a single assembly, whether from the database or uploaded. It fills the main area of the dashboard in Evaluate mode. You reach it by:

Right-clicking an assembly in any roster and selecting “Evaluate Assembly”
Entering an accession in the Evaluate sidebar
Uploading an assembly archive

See Evaluate Overview for full entry-point details.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text:
Section 1: Priority Ranking
The first element at the top of the report is the Priority Ranking — a single number showing where this assembly falls in the ranked list of all assemblies in the database. The ranking is based on a weighted composite of novelty, diversity, and density scores, designed to surface assemblies with the broadest and most novel biosynthetic potential.
How to read it:

A ranking of 1 means this assembly has the highest composite score in the entire database.
A ranking in the top 100 (out of tens of thousands of assemblies) indicates an exceptionally promising candidate.
The ranking updates each time the database grows, so the same assembly may shift position over time.

What to look for:
If the ranking is high (numerically low), this assembly is a strong candidate. If the ranking is surprisingly low for an assembly you expected to be interesting, drill into the individual score sections below to see which metric is pulling the composite down.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text:
Section 2: Priority Score Radar
Below the ranking you will find a spider chart (also called a radar chart) with three axes:







Axis
What it measures




Diversity
How many different BGC classes are represented among this assembly’s gene clusters. A highly diverse assembly produces multiple types of natural products.


Novelty
The average novelty of this assembly’s BGCs — how distant they are from known, experimentally characterised BGCs in the MIBiG database.


Density
The number of BGCs per megabase of assembled sequence. A dense assembly packs more biosynthetic potential into its genome.



The chart overlays three shapes:

This assembly (solid coloured polygon) — the evaluated assembly’s scores.
Database mean (dashed grey polygon) — the average scores across all assemblies in the database.
90th percentile (dotted green polygon) — the scores at the 90th percentile, representing the threshold for the top 10% of assemblies.

How to read it:

If the solid polygon extends beyond the dashed grey polygon on all three axes, this assembly is above average in every dimension.
If the solid polygon reaches or exceeds the dotted green polygon, the assembly is in the top 10% for that metric.
An assembly that excels on one axis but falls short on others may still be a good candidate depending on your priorities. For example, if you are specifically hunting for novel chemistry, a high-novelty assembly with moderate diversity and density may be exactly what you need.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text:
Section 3: Score Percentile Distributions
This section shows a row of gauge charts, one for each metric:

Novelty Percentile — what fraction of all assemblies have a lower novelty score than this one.
Diversity Percentile — what fraction of all assemblies have fewer distinct BGC classes.
Density Percentile — what fraction of all assemblies have fewer BGCs per megabase.

Each gauge is a semicircular dial with the needle pointing to the percentile value (0–100). The dial is colour-coded: green for the top quartile (75–100), yellow for the middle two quartiles (25–75), and red for the bottom quartile (0–25).
How to read it:
A percentile of 85 means that 85% of all assemblies in the database score lower than this one on that metric. The higher the percentile, the more exceptional the assembly is on that dimension.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text:
Section 4: BGC Triad (scoped)
Below the score panels you will find the familiar BGC Triad — the same three-panel layout used in Explore mode (BGC Roster, BGC Space Map, BGC Detail) — but scoped to show only the BGCs that belong to the evaluated assembly.
How to use it:

BGC Roster — scan the table to see how many BGCs the assembly carries, their classes, and their individual novelty scores. Sort by novelty to find the most unusual clusters.
BGC Space Map — the UMAP projection shows where this assembly’s BGCs sit relative to each other. Tight clusters suggest redundant chemistry; scattered points suggest diverse potential.
BGC Detail — click any BGC in the roster or on the map to see its domain architecture, predicted products, and related information. Right-click a BGC and select “Evaluate BGC” to generate the full BGC Assessment Report for that cluster.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text:
Section 5: Redundancy Matrix
The Redundancy Matrix is a table that categorises every BGC in this assembly according to how well characterised its Gene Cluster Family (GCF) is:








Category
Meaning
Implication




Novel GCF
This BGC belongs to a GCF that contains no experimentally validated members from MIBiG.
Highest novelty. The chemistry produced by this family is unknown.


Known GCF, no type strain
The GCF has validated members, but none of them come from a type strain (a publicly deposited, purchasable culture).
Moderate novelty. The chemistry is partly known, but you cannot easily obtain the reference organism.


Known GCF, type strain available
The GCF has validated members from at least one type strain.
Low novelty for this specific chemistry. You could obtain the type strain and study the same pathway more conveniently.



How to read it:

If most BGCs fall into the “Novel GCF” column, this assembly is a strong candidate for discovering new natural products.
If most BGCs fall into the “Known GCF, type strain available” column, this assembly offers limited discovery potential — similar chemistry is already accessible in culturable organisms.
A mix of categories is common. Focus your attention on the Novel GCF entries.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text:
Section 6: BGC Embeddings Map
The final section is a UMAP projection of the full BGC embedding space. Every BGC in the database appears as a small dot, coloured by BGC class. The evaluated assembly’s BGCs are highlighted as large, labelled points. MIBiG reference BGCs (experimentally validated clusters with known products) appear as diamond-shaped landmarks.
How to read it:

BGCs from the evaluated assembly that sit far from any MIBiG landmark are the most novel — they occupy regions of chemical space where no characterised compound exists.
BGCs that cluster near MIBiG landmarks are likely to produce similar chemistry to the known compound.
The relative positions of the assembly’s BGCs tell you about internal diversity. If they are spread across the map, the assembly produces chemically diverse products. If they cluster together, the products are likely related.

title: Reading the Assembly Assessment Report
text:
Tips

Start with the radar chart for a quick overview, then drill into individual sections for detail.
The Redundancy Matrix is the most actionable section for making a go/no-go decision. It directly answers: “Can I find similar chemistry elsewhere in a more convenient organism?”
Use the scoped BGC Triad to identify specific BGCs worth evaluating individually. The assembly report tells you whether the organism is interesting; the BGC report tells you which specific gene cluster to target.
Cross-reference with the Assembly Space Map from Explore mode. An assembly in a densely populated region of the map may score well individually but offer little that its neighbours do not.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text: Shortlists let you collect promising assemblies and BGCs as you work, without losing track of them. When you are ready, you export the shortlist as a file suitable for sharing with collaborators or importing into downstream tools. This page explains how to add items, manage your shortlists, export them, and how shortlists interact with the rest of the platform.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text: Shortlists let you collect promising assemblies and BGCs as you work, without losing track of them. When you are ready, you export the shortlist as a file suitable for sharing with collaborators or importing into downstream tools. This page explains how to add items, manage your shortlists, export them, and how shortlists interact with the rest of the platform.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when:

You want to save an assembly or BGC for later review.
You need to understand the export file formats (CSV for assemblies, GenBank for BGCs).
You want to know how shortlisted assemblies affect the BGC Triad in Explore mode.
You want to clear or manage your shortlists.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
Two independent shortlists
The platform maintains two separate shortlists:









Shortlist
Max items
Export format
Contains




Assembly Shortlist
20
CSV
Assemblies (organisms) with their scores and metadata


BGC Shortlist
20
GenBank
Individual biosynthetic gene clusters with sequences and annotations



The two shortlists are independent. Adding an assembly does not automatically add its BGCs, and adding a BGC does not automatically add its parent assembly. You choose what to save at each level.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
Adding items to shortlists

Adding an assembly
You can add an assembly to the Assembly Shortlist in three ways:

Pin icon in the Assembly Roster: Each row in the Assembly Roster has a small pin icon on the right side. Click it to add the assembly. The icon fills in to indicate the assembly is shortlisted.
Assembly detail flyout: When you expand an assembly’s detail view, an Add to Assembly Shortlist button appears near the top.
Right-click context menu: Right-click any assembly row in the Assembly Roster and select Add to Assembly Shortlist from the context menu.



Adding a BGC
You can add a BGC to the BGC Shortlist in three ways:

Pin icon in the BGC Roster: Each row in the BGC Roster has a pin icon. Click it to add the BGC.
BGC Detail Panel: The BGC Detail Panel has an Add to Shortlist button at the bottom.
Right-click context menu: Right-click any BGC row in the BGC Roster and select Add to BGC Shortlist.



Confirming the addition
When you add an item, two things happen:

A counter in the header bar increments. There are separate counters for assemblies and BGCs (e.g. “Assemblies: 3” and “BGCs: 5”).
The pin icon in the corresponding roster row fills in, providing a visual reminder that the item is already shortlisted.

If the shortlist is already at its maximum of 20 items, the add action is disabled and a tooltip explains the limit. You must remove an existing item before adding a new one.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
Viewing your shortlists
Click the shortlist icon in the header bar to open the Shortlist Panel. This is a slide-out drawer that shows both shortlists as two tabs:

Assembly Shortlist tab
A table listing every shortlisted assembly with the following columns:







Column
Content




Accession
The assembly accession (e.g. GCA_000203835.1)


Organism
Species or strain name


Type Strain
Whether the assembly is a type strain, with a link to the culture collection catalogue if available


BGC Count
Number of BGCs detected


Novelty
Assembly novelty score (0–1)


Diversity
Assembly diversity score (0–1)


Density
BGCs per megabase


Tax. Novelty
Taxonomic novelty score (0–1)



Each row has a Remove button (X icon) to remove the assembly from the shortlist.


BGC Shortlist tab
A table listing every shortlisted BGC with the following columns:







Column
Content




Accession
The BGC accession (e.g. MGYB00000123.ANT.01)


Classification
BGC class hierarchy (e.g. Polyketide.Macrolide)


Size (kb)
Length in kilobases


Novelty
BGC novelty score (0–1)


Domain Novelty
Fraction of unique domains (0–1)


Completeness
Complete or Partial badge


Parent Assembly
The accession of the assembly this BGC belongs to



Each row has a Remove button.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
Exporting shortlists

Assembly CSV export
Click the Export CSV button in the Assembly Shortlist tab. The browser downloads a comma-separated file named assembly_shortlist.csv with one row per assembly. The columns are:
accession, organism_name, source, assembly_type, is_type_strain,
type_strain_catalog_url, bgc_count, l1_class_count,
bgc_novelty_score, bgc_diversity_score, bgc_density,
taxonomic_novelty, assembly_size_mb, biome_path, url
This file is ready to open in Excel, Google Sheets, or any spreadsheet application. It is also suitable for sharing with collaborators who do not have access to the platform.
Typical uses for the CSV:

Prioritise organisms for culturing by sorting on novelty and type strain status.
Attach to a grant application as evidence of bioprospecting targets.
Import into a laboratory information management system (LIMS) to track which organisms are in progress.



BGC GenBank export
Click the Export GenBank button in the BGC Shortlist tab. The browser downloads a multi-record GenBank file named bgc_shortlist.gbk. Each record contains:

The nucleotide sequence of the BGC region.
Gene annotations (CDS features) with protein translations.
Pfam domain annotations as /note qualifiers on the corresponding CDS features.
BGC boundary annotations as cluster or region features.
Metadata: BGC accession, classification path, detection tool, and parent assembly accession.

Typical uses for the GenBank file:

Open in antiSMASH to re-annotate or compare with its own predictions.
Open in Artemis or SnapGene to inspect gene arrangements manually.
Feed into a cloning design tool to plan heterologous expression constructs.
Share with collaborators as a self-contained record of each BGC.







Tip



The GenBank file includes the full nucleotide sequence plus a small flanking window on each side of the BGC. This flanking context can be helpful for designing PCR primers or identifying regulatory elements upstream of the cluster.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
Removing items

Remove one item
Click the X icon next to any row in the Shortlist Panel. The item is immediately removed, and the header counter decrements.


Clear the entire shortlist
At the bottom of each shortlist tab, a Clear All button removes every item from that shortlist. A confirmation dialog appears to prevent accidental clearing.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
Persistence
Shortlists are stored in your browser’s localStorage. This means:

They survive page reloads and browser restarts.
They persist between sessions. If you close the browser and return tomorrow, your shortlists are still there.
They are specific to the browser and device. If you switch to a different browser or computer, you will start with empty shortlists.
They are not tied to a user account (the platform does not require login).







Warning



Clearing your browser’s site data or using private/incognito mode will erase your shortlists. If you have important selections, export them before clearing browser data.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
How shortlists affect views

In Explore Assemblies mode
When you have assemblies in your Assembly Shortlist, you can click the shortlist badge in the BGC Triad header to switch the BGC Triad from showing a single selected assembly’s BGCs to showing the merged BGC set from all shortlisted assemblies. This lets you:

Compare the BGC repertoires of multiple assemblies side by side.
Find the single best BGC across all shortlisted assemblies by sorting the merged roster by novelty.
Spot redundancy — if two assemblies contribute BGCs that cluster together in the BGC Space Map, they may produce the same compound.

The merged BGC Roster includes an additional Parent Assembly column so you can trace each BGC back to its source organism.


In Search BGCs mode
When you run a search (domain, sequence, or chemical), the results may include BGCs from assemblies that are in your Assembly Shortlist. These results are not treated differently from other results — there is no automatic boosting or filtering — but you can sort or scan for parent assemblies you recognise.
If you find a promising BGC in Search mode and add it to your BGC Shortlist, that shortlist remains available when you switch back to Explore mode.


In Evaluate Asset mode
When you evaluate an assembly, the assessment report does not reference your shortlists. However, after reviewing the report, you can add the assembly to your shortlist from the report view using the Add to Shortlist button.


Cross-mode persistence
Both shortlists are global and persist across all three modes. Adding a BGC in Explore mode and then switching to Search mode does not remove it. The header counters are visible in all modes so you always know how many items you have saved.

title: Building and Exporting Your Shortlists
text:
Tips

Use the Assembly Shortlist to build a prioritised list of organisms for culturing. Export the CSV and use it as a procurement checklist.
Use the BGC Shortlist to collect the specific gene clusters you want to clone or re-annotate. The GenBank export gives you everything you need to start designing experiments.
If you reach the 20-item limit, review your shortlist and remove lower-priority candidates rather than stopping your exploration. The limit exists to keep exports focused and actionable.
Remember that shortlists are browser-local. If you work across multiple devices, export your shortlists at the end of each session.
Right-click shortcuts are the fastest way to add items without opening detail panels. Use them when scanning through a long roster.

title: Scores and Metrics Reference
text: To provide a single reference page where you can look up the definition, range, and interpretation of every quantitative score displayed in the BGC Discovery Platform. This page covers scores at the BGC level, the assembly level, and the percentile and decomposition scores available only in Evaluate mode.

title: Scores and Metrics Reference
text: To provide a single reference page where you can look up the definition, range, and interpretation of every quantitative score displayed in the BGC Discovery Platform. This page covers scores at the BGC level, the assembly level, and the percentile and decomposition scores available only in Evaluate mode.

title: Scores and Metrics Reference
text:
BGC-level scores
These scores are computed for every individual biosynthetic gene cluster (BGC) in the database and are visible in the BGC Roster, BGC Detail panel, and BGC Assessment Report.

Novelty Score







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The cosine distance between this BGC’s protein embedding vector and the embedding of the nearest experimentally validated BGC in MIBiG.


Interpretation
A score of 0 means the BGC is identical (in embedding space) to a known, characterised BGC. A score of 1 means it is maximally distant from every characterised BGC. Higher values indicate greater novelty.


How it is calculated
The platform computes a 1152-dimensional embedding vector for each BGC by passing its protein sequences through a pre-trained protein language model (ESM). It then calculates the cosine distance between this vector and every MIBiG BGC’s vector, and reports the minimum distance as the Novelty Score.



The Novelty Score is the single most important metric for bioprospecting. A BGC with a Novelty Score above 0.7 has no close relative among characterised natural products and is a strong candidate for novel chemistry.


Domain Novelty







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The fraction of Pfam protein domains in this BGC that are not found in any MIBiG-validated BGC.


Interpretation
A score of 0 means every domain in this BGC also appears in at least one validated BGC. A score of 1 means none of its domains have been seen in any validated BGC.


How it is calculated
The platform identifies all Pfam domains in the BGC, counts how many are absent from the full MIBiG domain catalogue, and divides by the total number of domains.



Domain Novelty complements the Novelty Score. A BGC can have a moderate Novelty Score (its overall embedding is not far from known BGCs) but high Domain Novelty (it contains specific enzyme domains never seen in validated clusters). This combination suggests a BGC that uses a familiar biosynthetic framework but incorporates unusual enzymatic steps.


Nearest Validated Distance







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The Euclidean distance in embedding space between this BGC and the nearest MIBiG-validated BGC, normalised to the maximum observed distance in the database.


Interpretation
Functionally similar to the Novelty Score but uses Euclidean rather than cosine distance. Provided for users who prefer a distance metric that accounts for vector magnitude as well as direction.



In practice, the Novelty Score (cosine distance) and Nearest Validated Distance (Euclidean distance) are highly correlated. Most users can rely on the Novelty Score alone.

title: Scores and Metrics Reference
text:
Assembly-level scores
These scores are computed for every assembly in the database and are visible in the Assembly Roster, Assembly Detail panel, and Assembly Assessment Report.

Assembly Novelty







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The mean Novelty Score across all BGCs belonging to this assembly.


Interpretation
An assembly with high novelty carries BGCs that are, on average, distant from all characterised gene clusters.





Diversity







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The normalised richness of BGC classes represented in this assembly. Calculated as the number of distinct BGC broad classes (Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, Terpene, etc.) divided by the total number of broad classes observed across the entire database.


Interpretation
A score of 1 means this assembly carries every BGC class present in the database. A score near 0 means it carries only one class. Higher diversity suggests a broader biosynthetic repertoire.



An assembly with high novelty but low diversity may carry many novel BGCs that all belong to the same chemical class. Conversely, an assembly with moderate novelty and high diversity produces many types of natural products, even if none is individually groundbreaking.


Density







Property
Value




Range
0 to theoretical maximum (unbounded, but typically 0–50)


Definition
The number of BGCs per megabase (Mb) of assembled sequence.


Interpretation
A higher density means the organism devotes a larger fraction of its genome to secondary metabolism. Organisms like Streptomyces species commonly have densities above 10 BGCs/Mb, while typical E. coli strains carry fewer than 2 BGCs/Mb.


Units
BGCs per megabase



Density is useful for identifying organisms that are “biosynthetic specialists” — they invest heavily in natural product production relative to their genome size.


Taxonomic Novelty







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
A measure of how under-represented this assembly’s taxonomic lineage is in the database. Calculated as the inverse frequency of the assembly’s genus (or the nearest resolved taxonomic rank) among all assemblies.


Interpretation
A score near 1 means very few assemblies in the database belong to this taxonomic group. A score near 0 means the group is well-represented. High Taxonomic Novelty suggests that the organism comes from an under-explored lineage, which often correlates with novel chemistry.

title: Scores and Metrics Reference
text:
Percentile scores (Evaluate mode only)
When you evaluate a single assembly or BGC, the platform computes percentile ranks for every score. Percentile ranks are not stored in the database; they are calculated on demand against the current distribution of all assets.

How percentiles work
A percentile of 85 means that 85% of all assemblies (or BGCs) in the database have a lower value for that metric. The platform bins the full distribution into 100 equal-width buckets and reports the bucket in which the evaluated asset falls.
Percentile ranks are useful for comparing assets across metrics that have different scales. A Novelty Score of 0.6 and a Density of 15 BGCs/Mb are not directly comparable, but if both correspond to the 90th percentile, you know the asset is in the top 10% on both dimensions.


Available percentiles
In the Assembly Assessment Report:

Novelty Percentile
Diversity Percentile
Density Percentile
Taxonomic Novelty Percentile

In the BGC Assessment Report:

Novelty Score Percentile
Domain Novelty Percentile

title: Scores and Metrics Reference
text:
Novelty decomposition (BGC Evaluate only)
When you evaluate a single BGC, the platform decomposes its overall novelty into three independent axes. These are displayed only in the BGC Assessment Report and are not pre-computed for every BGC in the database.

Sequence Novelty







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The average pairwise sequence identity between this BGC’s proteins and the best-matching proteins in the nearest MIBiG BGC, inverted (1 minus identity).


Interpretation
High values mean the enzymes encoded by this BGC are sequence-divergent from their nearest characterised homologues.





Chemistry Novelty







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The Tanimoto distance between the predicted molecular fingerprint of this BGC’s product and the fingerprints of all known MIBiG compounds.


Interpretation
High values mean the predicted product is chemically dissimilar to all known natural products in MIBiG.





Architecture Novelty







Property
Value




Range
0 to 1


Definition
The Jaccard distance between the ordered set of Pfam domains in this BGC and the ordered domain sets of all MIBiG BGCs.


Interpretation
High values mean the arrangement of biosynthetic domains is unlike any known BGC.



These three axes are independent. A BGC can score high on one axis and low on the others. See the BGC Assessment Report page for guidance on interpreting combinations of these scores.

title: The BGC Triad
text: The BGC Triad occupies the bottom half of the Explore Assemblies screen. It shows the biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) associated with the assembly (or assemblies) you have selected or shortlisted. This page explains how the triad populates, what each of its three panels shows, and how to navigate from a BGC back to its parent assembly.

title: The BGC Triad
text: The BGC Triad occupies the bottom half of the Explore Assemblies screen. It shows the biosynthetic gene clusters (BGCs) associated with the assembly (or assemblies) you have selected or shortlisted. This page explains how the triad populates, what each of its three panels shows, and how to navigate from a BGC back to its parent assembly.

title: The BGC Triad
text:
When to use this page
Refer to this page when:

You have selected an assembly in the Assembly Roster or Assembly Space Map and want to understand the BGC panels that appeared.
You want to know the difference between viewing BGCs for a single selected assembly versus your entire assembly shortlist.
You need to interpret the BGC Space Map’s UMAP coordinates and MIBiG landmarks.
You want to understand the BGC Stats charts.

title: The BGC Triad
text:
How the BGC Triad populates
The BGC Triad is empty when you first open the Explore Assemblies screen. It fills with data in three ways:








Trigger
What appears
Badge label




Click a single assembly row or point
All BGCs belonging to that assembly
“Selected assembly”


Activate the assembly shortlist
All BGCs from all shortlisted assemblies, merged into one set
“Shortlisted assemblies (N)”


Click the “All BGCs” toggle (when available)
The full BGC catalogue for the current filter set
“All assemblies”



A small badge at the top of the BGC Triad always indicates which source is active. When you switch between sources, the badge updates and all three panels refresh.

title: The BGC Triad
text:
Panel 1: BGC Roster
The BGC Roster is a paginated, sortable table on the left side of the BGC Triad. Each row represents one BGC.

Columns







Column
What it shows




Accession
The structured BGC accession (e.g. MGYB00000123.ANT.01). The format is {region_accession}.{detector_code}.{bgc_number}.


Classification
The BGC class hierarchy as a dot-delimited path (e.g. Polyketide.Macrolide). The first segment is the top-level class.


Size (kb)
The length of the BGC in kilobases. Larger BGCs often encode more complex products.


Novelty
Cosine distance to the nearest validated (MIBiG) BGC embedding. Ranges from 0 (identical to a known cluster) to 1 (maximally different).


Domain Novelty
Fraction of this BGC’s Pfam domains that are unique to this BGC across the entire database. A value of 1.0 means every domain hit is exclusive.


Completeness
A badge indicating whether the BGC is complete or partial. Partial BGCs sit at the edge of a contig and may be truncated.


Nearest Validated
The MIBiG accession of the closest validated BGC by embedding distance, along with the raw distance value.


Detector
The tool and version that detected the BGC (e.g. antiSMASH v7.1).





Sorting
Click any column header to sort. The most useful sorts:

Novelty (descending): puts the most unusual BGCs at the top. Best for finding new chemistry.
Domain Novelty (descending): surfaces BGCs with the most unique domain compositions.
Size (descending): surfaces the largest BGCs, which often encode the most complex biosynthetic pathways.



Row actions

Click a row: Opens the BGC Detail Panel as a flyout on the right side of the screen.
Right-click a row: Opens a context menu with actions: Add to Shortlist, Find Similar (switches to Search mode), Evaluate (switches to Evaluate mode).

title: The BGC Triad
text:
Panel 2: BGC Space Map
The BGC Space Map is a UMAP scatter plot in the centre of the BGC Triad. UMAP (Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection) is a dimensionality reduction technique that places each BGC as a point on a two-dimensional canvas so that BGCs with similar protein-level embeddings appear near each other.

How to read the map

Each point represents one BGC. Points that are close together encode similar enzymatic machinery and are likely to produce related natural products.
Colour indicates the top-level BGC class (Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, Terpene, Saccharide, Alkaloid, Other). The colour legend appears below the plot.
MIBiG landmark points are validated BGCs from the MIBiG database. They appear as labelled markers (often diamond-shaped or larger) so you can see where characterised chemistry sits in the landscape. Landmark labels show the compound name or MIBiG accession.
Highlighted points belong to the currently selected or shortlisted assemblies. Unrelated BGCs appear as faded background points.



Axes
The x- and y-axes represent the two UMAP dimensions. These do not have direct biochemical meaning — they are computed to preserve neighbourhood relationships from the original 1,152-dimensional embedding space. Do not over-interpret absolute positions; focus on relative proximity.


Interpreting patterns

A highlighted point near a MIBiG landmark: The BGC is similar to a known, characterised cluster. The nearby MIBiG label tells you what compound the reference produces. This BGC is likely making a structural variant.
A highlighted point in a dense cloud far from any landmark: The BGC belongs to a gene cluster family (GCF) that has many members but no validated representative. The chemistry is uncharacterised.
A highlighted point in empty space: The BGC is a novel singleton with no close neighbours. It may encode fundamentally new enzymology — or it may be a partial, misassembled cluster. Check the BGC Detail Panel for completeness and domain architecture.



Interactions

Click a point to open its BGC Detail Panel.
Hover to see a tooltip with accession, BGC class, and novelty score.
Pan and zoom work the same as in the Assembly Space Map.

title: The BGC Triad
text:
Panel 3: BGC Stats
The BGC Stats panel on the right side of the BGC Triad provides summary visualisations of the currently displayed BGC set.

BGC Class Distribution
A horizontal bar chart showing the count of BGCs in each top-level class (Polyketide, NRP, RiPP, Terpene, Saccharide, Alkaloid, Other). This gives you an immediate sense of the biosynthetic repertoire.

A single dominant bar means the assembly (or shortlisted assemblies) specialise in one type of chemistry.
Multiple bars of similar height indicate diverse biosynthetic capacity.



ChemOnt Sunburst
A sunburst chart showing the Chemical Ontology (ChemOnt) classification of predicted natural products from the displayed BGCs. The inner ring shows the broadest chemical categories; outer rings show progressively finer classifications.

Click a segment to zoom into that branch of the ChemOnt tree.
Click the centre to zoom back out.
Hover to see the class name and count.

This chart is particularly useful for spotting the dominant chemical scaffolds produced by an assembly. If a large segment corresponds to a compound class you are not interested in, you can use ChemOnt filters to exclude it.


Completeness Summary
A simple count showing how many of the displayed BGCs are complete versus partial. A high ratio of partial BGCs may indicate that the assembly is fragmented (common with metagenomes) and that some BGCs may be truncated.


Total BGC Count
The total number of BGCs currently displayed in the triad, shown as a prominent number at the top of the panel.

title: The BGC Triad
section: How shortlists affect the BGC Triad
text:
How shortlists affect the BGC Triad
When you activate your assembly shortlist, the BGC Triad merges all BGCs from all shortlisted assemblies into a single view. This is useful for:

Comparing BGC repertoires across assemblies. The BGC Space Map shows where each assembly’s BGCs sit relative to each other and to MIBiG landmarks.
Finding the single best BGC across multiple candidates. Sort the merged BGC Roster by novelty to surface the most novel BGC regardless of which assembly it belongs to.
Checking for redundancy. If two shortlisted assemblies contribute BGCs that cluster together in the BGC Space Map, they may be producing the same compound. You might choose to keep only one.

Each BGC in the merged roster shows its parent assembly accession in an additional column, so you can always trace a BGC back to its organism.

title: The BGC Triad
text:
Navigating between levels

From BGC to parent assembly
In the BGC Detail Panel, the parent assembly is shown as a clickable link. Clicking it scrolls the Assembly Roster to the corresponding row and selects it.


From Assembly Roster to BGC Triad
Clicking any row in the Assembly Roster populates the BGC Triad automatically. You do not need to do anything extra.


From BGC to Evaluate or Search
Right-click a BGC row in the BGC Roster to access cross-mode actions:

Evaluate: Switches to Evaluate Asset mode and runs a BGC assessment, producing a detailed report with GCF context, domain differential, novelty decomposition, and chemical space analysis.
Find Similar: Switches to Search BGCs mode and runs a sequence similarity search using the BGC’s embedding vector, returning ranked matches from the entire database.

title: The BGC Triad
text:
Tips

When viewing BGCs from a single assembly, the BGC Stats panel gives you that assembly’s biosynthetic profile at a glance. Use it to quickly assess whether the assembly’s chemistry matches your interests before drilling into individual BGCs.
The BGC Space Map is most informative when viewed with MIBiG landmarks visible. If the map looks cluttered, zoom into the region around your highlighted points.
If you have multiple assemblies shortlisted, look for overlap in the BGC Space Map. If BGCs from different assemblies cluster together, they probably belong to the same GCF and produce similar compounds.
Use the completeness badge in the BGC Roster as a quick filter. Partial BGCs may be truncated and can give misleading novelty scores. Prioritise complete BGCs for shortlisting.
